topics.title: Design an SRS document in line with the IEEE recommended standards for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design an SRS document in line with the IEEE recommended standards for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal document that completely describes the **external behaviour** of the software system identified in the Software Requirements Analysis (SRA). It is a complete specification of **what the system should do**, but explicitly **not how** it should do it.

In the context of the **IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications (IEEE Std 830-1998)**, the SRS is the official bridge between the **client's problem domain** and the **developer's solution domain**. It serves as the **single source of truth** for all subsequent phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) — design, coding, testing, and maintenance.

For KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCNL607 — CASE LAB), this experiment tests a student's ability to:
- Identify functional and non-functional requirements from a real-world problem statement.
- Organise the requirements using the **IEEE 830 four-part template**: Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, and Appendices.
- Apply **verification attributes** (correct, unambiguous, complete, consistent, ranked, verifiable, modifiable, traceable) to every requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favourite):**
> - **SRS** → describes the *complete* external behaviour of the system.
> - **SRA** → describes the *current* system and the proposed system in their *external* environment.
> - **SDD (Software Design Description)** → describes *how* the system is built (architecture, modules, data).
> 
> The SRS is the *boundary contract*; the SDD is the *internal blueprint*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Metaphor

Imagine you walk into an architect's office and say: *"I want a house."* Before a single brick is laid, the architect writes a **specification document** that says:

| What you say (Need) | What the architect writes (SRS) | What is NOT written (SDD) |
|---|---|---|
| "I want 3 bedrooms." | FR-1: System shall provide 3 distinct sleeping quarters, each ≥ 120 sq.ft. | The internal wall wiring layout, the exact placement of rebar in the foundation, the type of cement used. |
| "The house should be safe in heavy rain." | NFR-1: Roof shall withstand 200 mm/hr rainfall without leakage. | The exact chemical formula of the waterproofing compound. |
| "It should feel modern." | NFR-2: Interior lighting shall use LED fixtures ≥ 4000K colour temperature. | The brand of LED driver circuit. |

The SRS is **what the customer asked for, written so precisely that no two builders would disagree on what to deliver**. The IEEE 830 standard ensures every architect (developer) worldwide writes this specification in the **same structured format**, so a client in Kerala and a developer in California can collaborate unambiguously.

## 1.3 Why IEEE 830?

The Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers published **IEEE Std 830-1998** to:
1. **Establish a standard structure** so SRS documents are uniform across organisations.
2. **Reduce ambiguity** by enforcing precise, testable language.
3. **Enable traceability** between requirements, design, code, and test cases.
4. **Support validation and verification** (V&V) of requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eight Verification Attributes (IEEE 830 § 4.3) — High-Yield for KTU:**
> 1. **Correct** — accurately represents the user's need.
> 2. **Unambiguous** — has only one possible interpretation.
> 3. **Complete** — everything the software must do is included.
> 4. **Consistent** — no requirement conflicts with another.
> 5. **Ranked** — priorities (high/medium/low) are assigned.
> 6. **Verifiable** — a finite, cost-effective test can confirm it.
> 7. **Modifiable** — changes can be made consistently and completely.
> 8. **Traceable** — each requirement has a unique ID and origin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Part IEEE 830 SRS Template — Decomposed

The IEEE 830 standard organises the SRS into **four mandatory sections**. Each section has a strict purpose:

### PART 1 — Introduction (Section 1 of the document)

| Sub-Section | Purpose | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 Purpose | Identify the product by name, version, and intended audience (managers, developers, testers, users). | Anchors the entire document in scope. |
| 1.2 Document Conventions | Define typography, formatting, abbreviation, and prioritisation conventions (e.g., H/M/L = High/Medium/Low). | Eliminates stylistic ambiguity. |
| 1.3 Intended Audience & Reading Suggestions | Describe each reader group and which sections they should focus on. | Improves navigability for multi-stakeholder teams. |
| 1.4 Scope of the Project | State the product's boundaries, what it will do, what it will **not** do, benefits, objectives, and success criteria. | Prevents **scope creep** — the most common project failure cause. |
| 1.5 References | List all cited documents (standards, related specs, glossaries) with version numbers. | Establishes the bibliography for traceability. |

### PART 2 — Overall Description (Section 2 of the document)

| Sub-Section | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 2.1 Product Perspective | Is the product **standalone**, a **member of a family**, or a **replacement/upgraded** system? What are its interfaces to hardware, software, users, and communications? |
| 2.2 Product Functions | A **summary-level** list of the major capabilities (a "functions-of-the-system" overview, **not** a feature list). |
| 2.3 User Classes & Characteristics | Identify all user types (Admin, Customer, Guest, Auditor) and their **skill levels, privileges, and frequency of use**. |
| 2.4 Operating Environment | Hardware, OS, network, web browser, mobile platform versions, middleware, and any version constraints. |
| 2.5 Design & Implementation Constraints | Standards compliance (e.g., **IEEE**, **ISO 27001**), language restrictions, regulatory laws (e.g., **GDPR**, **IT Act 2000**), resource limits. |
| 2.6 Assumptions & Dependencies | What we **assume** to be true (e.g., "users have internet connectivity") and what the system **depends on** (e.g., a third-party payment gateway). |

### PART 3 — Specific Requirements (Section 3 — the heart of the SRS)

This is where the **actual functional and non-functional requirements** are enumerated. IEEE 830 prescribes multiple representation modes:

| Notation | When to Use | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Language (Structured)** | Most projects, all requirements | Universal, easy for clients to verify | Risk of ambiguity if not disciplined |
| **Mathematical/Logical Notation** | Safety-critical systems (avionics, medical) | Unambiguous, formally provable | Hard for non-technical clients |
| **Structured Tables / Decision Trees** | Complex business rules | Captures many branches compactly | Visually crowded |
| **Use-Case Diagrams (UML)** | Object-oriented projects | Shows actors, pre/post-conditions, scenarios | Not a complete specification by itself |
| **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)** | Process-oriented systems | Shows data movement clearly | Outdated for OOP; may be combined with UML |
| **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams** | Database-heavy systems | Shows data structure clearly | Does not show process flow |
| **State Transition Diagrams (STD)** | Real-time, embedded systems | Captures dynamic behaviour well | Complex for large state spaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 830 strongly recommends** that SRS authors use **multiple complementary notations** (e.g., natural language + use cases + ER diagrams) rather than relying on a single form.

### PART 4 — Appendices (Section 4 of the document)

| Sub-Section | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 4.1 Appendices | Sample I/O formats, data dictionaries, glossary of acronyms, mock-up UI sketches. |
| 4.2 Index | Cross-reference index for traceability. |

## 2.2 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet — SRS Document Structure

| SRS Section (IEEE 830) | Contains | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Introduction | Purpose, Scope, Audience, Definitions, References | 2–3 marks |
| 2. Overall Description | Product perspective, user classes, constraints, assumptions | 2–3 marks |
| 3. Specific Requirements | Functional, Non-functional, External Interfaces (the heart) | 6–8 marks |
| 4. Appendices | Glossary, sample data, index | 0–1 mark |

## 2.3 Requirement Categories (Section 3 Deep-Dive)

### Functional Requirements (FR)
Define **what the system does** in response to inputs. Must be **observable as a behaviour** at the system's external interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Templated Format (IEEE 830 recommended):**
> `FR-X.Y: The <system> shall <action> <object> under <condition>.`
> 
> Example: *FR-2.1: The Library System shall reserve a book for a registered member when the book's available copy count is greater than zero.*

### Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)
Define **how well** the system performs. Categorised into:

| NFR Type | Standard Metric | Example (Bank ATM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Latency, throughput | ATM cash withdrawal shall complete within **3 seconds** for 95% of transactions. |
| **Availability** | Uptime % (e.g., 99.9%) | The system shall be available **24×7×365** except for scheduled maintenance. |
| **Reliability** | MTBF, MTTR | Mean Time Between Failures ≥ **2000 hours**. |
| **Security** | Authentication, encryption, audit | All transactions shall use **AES-256** encryption; all logins audited. |
| **Maintainability** | MTTR (code), modularity | Bug fixes shall be deployable within **4 hours** of approval. |
| **Portability** | Platform coverage | The mobile app shall run on **Android 9+** and **iOS 14+**. |
| **Usability** | Learnability, error rate | A new user shall complete first checkout in ≤ **5 minutes**. |
| **Scalability** | Concurrent users, data volume | The system shall support **10,000 concurrent users** without degradation. |
| **Compliance** | Standards, regulations | The system shall comply with **RBI Cyber Security Framework** and **IT Act 2000**. |

### External Interface Requirements
- **User Interfaces** — screen layouts, GUI standards, accessibility (WCAG 2.1).
- **Hardware Interfaces** — supported devices, drivers, ports.
- **Software Interfaces** — OS versions, middleware, libraries, APIs.
- **Communication Interfaces** — protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, MQTT, TCP/IP), bandwidth, latency.

### Design Constraints
- Standards (e.g., **IEEE 830**, **ISO 9001**), language mandates (e.g., "Java 17+"), regulatory (e.g., **GDPR**), hardware budget.

## 2.4 The "Shall" Convention — Engineering Discipline

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Using "should", "may", or "could" instead of "shall" results in **loss of 0.5–1 mark per requirement**. 
> 
> **IEEE 830 Rule:** Only the word **"shall"** denotes a mandatory requirement. "Should" = recommendation; "may" = permission; "will" = a statement of fact about the future.

| Correct (Verifiable) | Incorrect (Ambiguous) |
|---|---|
| The system **shall** reject passwords shorter than 8 characters. | The system should ideally have a password check. |
| The report **shall** be generated within 5 seconds. | The report should be fast. |
| The system **shall** log every failed login attempt. | Failed logins are logged. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & SRS Construction Walkthrough

## 3.1 The 10-Step Methodology to Build an IEEE-Compliant SRS

This is the **canonical procedure** that KTU examiners expect a student to demonstrate. Each step produces a specific deliverable.

| Step | Action | Deliverable Produced |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read and analyse the **case study** (problem narrative) | List of nouns (objects) and verbs (actions) |
| 2 | Identify the **scope** — what is in and out | § 1.4 Scope statement |
| 3 | Identify **user classes / actors** | § 2.3 User table |
| 4 | Identify **functional requirements** by verb analysis | § 3.1 FR list |
| 5 | Identify **non-functional requirements** from quality attribute scenarios | § 3.2 NFR list |
| 6 | Identify **external interfaces** (UI, hardware, software, comms) | § 3.3 Interface list |
| 7 | Identify **constraints, assumptions, dependencies** | § 2.5 and § 2.6 |
| 8 | Write each requirement using the **"shall" template** | Requirement statements with IDs |
| 9 | Apply **eight verification attributes** to each requirement | Quality check report |
| 10 | **Number and trace** every requirement for cross-reference | Traceability matrix |

## 3.2 Worked-Out Case Study — "Online Bookstore Management System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Narrative (typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
> 
> *"ABC Publishing wishes to develop an online bookstore. The system should allow customers to register, browse books by category, search, add to cart, and pay online. The admin should manage inventory (add/update/delete books), view orders, and generate sales reports. The system should send email confirmations. Performance: page load ≤ 2 seconds, 1000 concurrent users. Security: passwords encrypted, payment via SSL. Assume internet connectivity, modern browsers, and PostgreSQL 15 DB."*

### Step 1 — Noun/Verb Analysis

| Nouns (Candidate Objects) | Verbs (Candidate Actions) |
|---|---|
| Customer, Book, Category, Cart, Order, Admin, Report, Email, Payment | register, browse, search, add, remove, pay, view, generate, send, encrypt |

### Step 2 — Scope Statement (§ 1.4)

> **1.4 Scope:** The Online Bookstore Management System (OBMS) is a web-based application that enables customers to discover, order, and pay for books online, while providing the administrator with tools to manage inventory and generate reports. The system is **in scope** for: customer registration, book catalog browsing/search, cart management, online payment, email confirmations, and inventory management. The system is **out of scope** for: physical logistics/delivery tracking, in-store POS, and customer support chat (handled by third-party ticketing).

### Step 3 — User Classes (§ 2.3)

| User Class | Description | Privilege Level | Frequency of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Guest | Unregistered visitor | View catalog, register | High |
| Customer | Registered shopper | All guest + cart, order, pay, history | High |
| Admin | Store operator | All customer + manage books, view reports | Medium |
| System | Automated | Send email, process payment callback | Continuous |

### Step 4 — Functional Requirements (§ 3.1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 830 "shall" template applied:**

**FR-1: User Registration**
- FR-1.1: The OBMS shall allow a guest to register by submitting a **unique email**, **password (≥ 8 chars)**, **name**, and **phone number**.
- FR-1.2: The OBMS shall validate the email format and reject duplicates with error **E001**.
- FR-1.3: The OBMS shall send a **confirmation email** with a verification link upon successful registration.

**FR-2: Authentication**
- FR-2.1: The OBMS shall authenticate registered users via **email + password**.
- FR-2.2: The OBMS shall lock the account for **15 minutes** after **5 consecutive failed login attempts**.
- FR-2.3: The OBMS shall provide a "Forgot Password" feature that emails a **single-use, 1-hour-valid** reset link.

**FR-3: Catalog Browsing & Search**
- FR-3.1: The OBMS shall display books categorised under categories (e.g., Fiction, Science, Children's).
- FR-3.2: The OBMS shall support keyword search across **title, author, and ISBN**.
- FR-3.3: The OBMS shall support filter by **price range, author, category, and publication year**.

**FR-4: Cart Management**
- FR-4.1: A customer shall be able to **add a book to cart** if in stock.
- FR-4.2: A customer shall be able to **modify quantity** (1 to 5 copies) or **remove** an item.
- FR-4.3: The cart total shall be **recalculated on every modification**.

**FR-5: Order & Payment**
- FR-5.1: A customer shall be able to **place an order** from a non-empty cart.
- FR-5.2: The OBMS shall integrate with a **third-party payment gateway** (Razorpay/PayPal).
- FR-5.3: The OBMS shall record the order status as **PENDING** → **PAID** or **FAILED** based on gateway callback.
- FR-5.4: The OBMS shall send an **order confirmation email** within 60 seconds of a successful PAID status.

**FR-6: Inventory Management (Admin)**
- FR-6.1: The admin shall be able to **add, update, or delete** book records.
- FR-6.2: The admin shall be able to **update stock quantity**; the system shall prevent customer order if stock = 0.
- FR-6.3: The admin shall be able to **view all orders** and **filter by date range or status**.

**FR-7: Reports (Admin)**
- FR-7.1: The admin shall be able to **generate a daily sales report** (CSV/PDF).
- FR-7.2: The report shall include **order count, total revenue, top 10 selling books**.

### Step 5 — Non-Functional Requirements (§ 3.2)

| ID | Type | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| NFR-1 | Performance | All pages shall load within **2 seconds** at the 95th percentile. |
| NFR-2 | Availability | The OBMS shall maintain **99.9% uptime** (max 8.76 hrs downtime/year). |
| NFR-3 | Scalability | The OBMS shall support **1000 concurrent users** with response time degradation ≤ 10%. |
| NFR-4 | Security | All passwords shall be stored using **bcrypt** with cost factor ≥ 12. |
| NFR-5 | Security | All data in transit shall use **TLS 1.2+**. |
| NFR-6 | Security | PCI-DSS compliance required for payment data handling. |
| NFR-7 | Usability | A new customer shall complete a purchase in ≤ **5 minutes** from first visit. |
| NFR-8 | Reliability | The OBMS shall have **MTBF ≥ 720 hours** and **MTTR ≤ 30 minutes**. |
| NFR-9 | Maintainability | The system shall use a **modular 3-tier architecture** (Presentation, Business, Data). |
| NFR-10 | Compliance | The OBMS shall comply with the **IT Act 2000** and **GDPR** for EU customers. |

### Step 6 — External Interfaces (§ 3.3)

| Interface | Description |
|---|---|
| **User Interface** | Responsive web UI (ReactJS), accessible per **WCAG 2.1 AA**; mobile-first; supports Chrome 100+, Firefox 100+, Safari 15+. |
| **Software Interface** | Backend: **Node.js 18+** / **Express 4.x**; DB: **PostgreSQL 15**; Cache: **Redis 7**; Email: **SendGrid API**; Payment: **Razorpay SDK**. |
| **Communication Interface** | All client–server traffic over **HTTPS (port 443)**; webhooks from payment gateway over **HTTPS POST** with HMAC-SHA256 signature. |
| **Hardware Interface** | Server: minimum 8 GB RAM, 4 vCPUs, 100 GB SSD; Client: any device with modern browser. |

### Step 7 — Constraints, Assumptions, Dependencies (§ 2.5 / § 2.6)

**Design & Implementation Constraints:**
- C-1: Source code shall follow the **Google JavaScript Style Guide** (or company standard).
- C-2: All commits shall be tagged with a semantic version (SemVer 2.0).
- C-3: The system shall be deployed on **AWS Mumbai region** only.
- C-4: No third-party analytics tool may collect PII without consent.

**Assumptions:**
- A-1: Users have stable internet connectivity ≥ 1 Mbps.
- A-2: Customers have a valid email address and credit/debit card or UPI.
- A-3: The payment gateway (Razorpay) is available 99.95% of the time.

**Dependencies:**
- D-1: Availability of SendGrid API for email delivery.
- D-2: Razorpay SDK and webhook infrastructure.
- D-3: PostgreSQL 15 hosting on AWS RDS.

### Step 8 — Traceability Matrix (Appendix)

| Requirement ID | Origin (Source) | Use-Case | Design Element | Test Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FR-1.1 | Customer narrative | UC-01 Register | SignUpController | TC-001, TC-002 |
| FR-2.2 | Security NFR | UC-02 Login | AuthService | TC-010 |
| FR-5.2 | Payment narrative | UC-05 Checkout | PaymentGatewayAdapter | TC-030 |
| NFR-1 | Performance NFR | — | CDN + Redis caching | TC-PERF-01 |

## 3.3 Common Student Errors — KTU Examiner's Deduction Table

| Common Error | Why It's Wrong | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|
| Writing "should" instead of "shall" | Violates IEEE 830 § 4.3.2 (unambiguous) | 0.5 per occurrence |
| Mixing functional & non-functional in one list | FRs are behaviour, NFRs are quality — must be separated | 1 |
| Missing measurable metrics in NFRs | "The system shall be fast" is unverifiable | 1–2 per vague NFR |
| No priority/verification tag on requirements | IEEE 830 § 4.3.5 (ranked) violated | 0.5 |
| Designing *how* (architecture, DB schema) in the SRS | SRS is *what*, not *how* — that belongs in SDD | 2–3 |
| No error-handling requirements (E001, E002) | Incomplete — IEEE 830 § 4.3.3 | 1–2 |
| Single ID per requirement (no sub-numbering) | Violates traceability (FR-1.1, FR-1.2, …) | 0.5 |
| Missing scope boundaries | "What it will NOT do" omitted → scope creep risk | 1 |

## 3.4 Mathematical Note — Reliability Calculation (Often Asked as 3-Mark Insert)

Although SRS is largely textual, IEEE 830 requires **verifiable** NFRs, which means engineers must quantify them. Example: **Availability**.

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = Availability (as a fraction)
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair

For NFR-8 (MTBF = 720 hr, MTTR = 0.5 hr):

$$
A = \frac{720}{720 + 0.5} = \frac{720}{720.5} \approx 0.999306
$$

$$
A_{\%} = 99.93\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation proves NFR-2 (99.9% availability) is achievable. KTU often inserts such arithmetic to test quantitative reasoning inside a qualitative paper.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SRS Document Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef part1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef part2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef part3 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef part4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000

    S["SRS DOCUMENT (IEEE 830)"]:::root
    P1["PART 1: INTRODUCTION"]:::part1
    P2["PART 2: OVERALL DESCRIPTION"]:::part2
    P3["PART 3: SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS"]:::part3
    P4["PART 4: APPENDICES"]:::part4

    S --> P1
    S --> P2
    S --> P3
    S --> P4

    P1 --> A1["1.1 Purpose"]
    P1 --> A2["1.2 Document Conventions"]
    P1 --> A3["1.3 Intended Audience"]
    P1 --> A4["1.4 Scope of Project"]
    P1 --> A5["1.5 References"]

    P2 --> B1["2.1 Product Perspective"]
    P2 --> B2["2.2 Product Functions"]
    P2 --> B3["2.3 User Classes"]
    P2 --> B4["2.4 Operating Environment"]
    P2 --> B5["2.5 Constraints"]
    P2 --> B6["2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies"]

    P3 --> C1["3.1 Functional Requirements"]
    P3 --> C2["3.2 Non-Functional Requirements"]
    P3 --> C3["3.3 External Interface Requirements"]
    P3 --> C4["3.4 Design Constraints"]

    P4 --> D1["4.1 Sample I-O Formats"]
    P4 --> D2["4.2 Glossary"]
    P4 --> D3["4.3 Traceability Matrix"]
    P4 --> D4["4.4 Index"]
```

## 4.2 SRS Construction Workflow (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    S1["Step 1: Read Case Study"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: Extract Nouns and Verbs"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: Define Scope"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: Identify Actors and User Classes"]:::step
    S5["Step 5: Elicit Functional Requirements"]:::step
    S6["Step 6: Elicit Non-Functional Requirements"]:::step
    S7["Step 7: Map External Interfaces"]:::step
    S8["Step 8: List Constraints Assumptions Dependencies"]:::step
    S9["Step 9: Apply SHALL Template and Number IDs"]:::step
    S10["Step 10: Verification and Traceability"]:::step

    O1["Noun Verb List"]:::output
    O2["Scope Statement"]:::output
    O3["User Class Table"]:::output
    O4["FR List FR-1 FR-2 ..."]:::output
    O5["NFR List NFR-1 NFR-2 ..."]:::output
    O6["Interface Specs"]:::output
    O7["CAD List"]:::output
    O8["Requirement Statements with IDs"]:::output
    O9["Final Verified SRS"]:::output

    S1 --> O1
    O1 --> S2 --> S3 --> O2
    O2 --> S4 --> O3
    O3 --> S5 --> O4
    O4 --> S6 --> O5
    O5 --> S7 --> O6
    O6 --> S8 --> O7
    O7 --> S9 --> O8
    O8 --> S10
    S10 --> D1{"All 8 Attributes Met?"}:::decision
    D1 -- Yes --> O9
    D1 -- No --> S2
```

## 4.3 Use-Case Diagram for the Bookstore (Sample SRS Supplement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef actor fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef usecase fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    Sys["OBMS System Boundary"]:::system
    Guest((Guest)):::actor
    Customer((Customer)):::actor
    Admin((Admin)):::actor
    Payment((Payment Gateway)):::actor

    UC1[UC-01 Register]:::usecase
    UC2[UC-02 Login]:::usecase
    UC3[UC-03 Browse and Search Books]:::usecase
    UC4[UC-04 Manage Cart]:::usecase
    UC5[UC-05 Place Order and Pay]:::usecase
    UC6[UC-06 View Order History]:::usecase
    UC7[UC-07 Manage Book Inventory]:::usecase
    UC8[UC-08 Generate Sales Report]:::usecase
    UC9[UC-09 Send Email Confirmation]:::usecase
    UC10[UC-10 Process Payment Callback]:::usecase

    Guest --> UC1
    Guest --> UC3
    Customer --> UC2
    Customer --> UC3
    Customer --> UC4
    Customer --> UC5
    Customer --> UC6
    Admin --> UC2
    Admin --> UC7
    Admin --> UC8
    Payment --> UC10
    UC5 -.-> UC10
    UC10 -.-> UC9
    UC9 --> Customer
```

## 4.4 Verification Attributes Wheel (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef hub fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef attr fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000

    R["EVERY REQUIREMENT"]:::hub
    R --> V1["Correct"]:::attr
    R --> V2["Unambiguous"]:::attr
    R --> V3["Complete"]:::attr
    R --> V4["Consistent"]:::attr
    R --> V5["Ranked"]:::attr
    R --> V6["Verifiable"]:::attr
    R --> V7["Modifiable"]:::attr
    R --> V8["Traceable"]:::attr
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The 8 verification attributes spell **"C U C R V M T"** — *"Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable"* + **Consistent** (often written as the most-overlooked one).

## 4.5 ER Diagram for Bookstore (External Interface Supplement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef entity fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef rel fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000

    E1((Customer)):::entity
    E2((Order)):::entity
    E3((Book)):::entity
    E4((Category)):::entity
    E5((Cart)):::entity
    E6((Payment)):::entity
    E7((Admin)):::entity

    R1[places]:::rel
    R2[contains]:::rel
    R3[belongs to]:::rel
    R4[has]:::rel
    R5[settled by]:::rel
    R6[manages]:::rel

    E1 --- R1 --- E2
    E2 --- R2 --- E3
    E3 --- R3 --- E4
    E1 --- R4 --- E5
    E5 --- R2
    E2 --- R5 --- E6
    E7 --- R6 --- E3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between SRS (Software Requirements Specification) and SRA (Software Requirements Analysis).** (3 marks)

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
| Dimension | SRS (3 marks) |
|---|---|
| **Definition** | The **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a complete, formal document that **describes the external behaviour of the entire proposed system** — what the system shall do under all conditions. It is the **output** of the analysis phase. |
| **Purpose** | Serves as the **contractual reference** between client and developer for validation, verification, design, testing, and maintenance. |
| **SRA (for contrast)** | Software Requirements Analysis is the **process** of studying the current system, identifying user needs, and gathering requirements. Its outputs include feasibility study, current-system analysis, and the SRS. |
| **Marking Tip** | Mention that SRS is a *document*, SRA is a *process*; SRS is the deliverable that **captures** the result of SRA. |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper 1]**
**List any six verification attributes of a good SRS as per IEEE 830.** (3 marks)

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):
1. **Correct** — the requirement accurately represents the user's need.
2. **Unambiguous** — has exactly one interpretation.
3. **Complete** — includes all functional and non-functional requirements.
4. **Consistent** — no internal or external contradictions.
5. **Verifiable** — a finite, cost-effective test can be designed.
6. **Traceable** — every requirement has a unique ID and origin.
*(Mention Modifiable and Ranked for full credit if examiner asks for 8.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and provides internal choice. The two questions below are **independent alternatives** — the student answers ONE.

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 2, Q8]*

**"A University wants to develop an Online Examination System (OES). The system should allow administrators to schedule exams, add questions (MCQ/descriptive), assign students, and generate results. Students should register, take exams within a time window, and view results. Performance: 500 concurrent users. Security: encrypted passwords, session timeout. Assume Linux server, MySQL 8, Apache Tomcat 9, Java 17. Design a complete SRS document in line with IEEE 830 recommended standards."**

#### Solution Outline (14 marks):

**Part (a) — Structure of the SRS Document (7 marks)**

**(i) § 1. Introduction (1.5 marks)**
- **1.1 Purpose** — This SRS describes the Online Examination System (OES) v1.0 for [University]. Audience: Admin, Faculty, Students, Developers, Testers.
- **1.2 Document Conventions** — Priority tags: H/M/L; ID format: `FR-x.y`, `NFR-x`; all requirements use "shall".
- **1.3 Intended Audience & Reading** — Admin reads all; Students read § 3.1 functional specs relevant to them.
- **1.4 Scope** — In scope: scheduling, question bank, exam delivery, auto-grading, result publishing. Out of scope: proctoring via webcam, offline exams, paper-based re-evaluation.
- **1.5 References** — IEEE 830-1998, IEEE 12207, university IT policies, IT Act 2000.

**(ii) § 2. Overall Description (2 marks)**

- **2.1 Product Perspective** — Standalone web application; replaces the current manual paper-based exam process.
- **2.2 Product Functions** — Schedule exam → manage question bank → assign students → conduct exam → auto-grade → publish results.
- **2.3 User Classes**:
  | Class | Privileges | Frequency |
  |---|---|---|
  | Admin | Schedule, manage users, view reports | Daily |
  | Faculty | Add questions, view results | Per exam |
  | Student | Register, take exam, view result | Per exam |
- **2.4 Operating Environment** — Server: Linux (Ubuntu 22.04), Apache Tomcat 9, Java 17, MySQL 8; Client: any device with Chrome 100+ or Firefox 100+.
- **2.5 Design Constraints** — Must comply with **IT Act 2000**; Java 17 only; no third-party tracker without consent; deployed on **university's on-premise data centre**.
- **2.6 Assumptions** — All students have uninterrupted internet during the exam window; backup power is available; **dependencies** on MySQL 8 and Tomcat 9 stability.

**(iii) § 3. Specific Requirements — Functional (2.5 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample FRs (write at least 6 for 7-mark subpart):**

- **FR-1: User Registration & Login** — FR-1.1: OES shall allow students to register with **roll number, email, DOB, password (≥ 10 chars, mix of cases + digits + symbol)**. FR-1.2: OES shall authenticate via **email + password**. FR-1.3: OES shall enforce a **session timeout of 15 minutes of inactivity**.
- **FR-2: Exam Scheduling (Admin)** — FR-2.1: Admin shall create an exam by specifying **title, subject, date, start–end time, duration, allowed students, total marks**. FR-2.2: OES shall **prevent scheduling conflicts** for the same student.
- **FR-3: Question Bank Management (Faculty)** — FR-3.1: Faculty shall add questions of type **MCQ (4 options) or Descriptive (subjective, manual grading)**. FR-3.2: MCQ shall have **one correct answer** and a configurable **mark per question**. FR-3.3: Faculty shall be able to **edit or delete** questions only before the exam is published.
- **FR-4: Exam Conduction (Student)** — FR-4.1: OES shall display the exam **only within the scheduled window**. FR-4.2: OES shall **auto-submit** the exam when the timer reaches zero. FR-4.3: OES shall **save answers every 30 seconds** to prevent data loss.
- **FR-5: Grading & Results** — FR-5.1: OES shall **auto-grade MCQ** on submission. FR-5.2: Descriptive answers shall be **manually graded by faculty within 7 days**. FR-5.3: Results shall be **published as pass/fail and marks** only after all descriptive answers are graded.
- **FR-6: Reports (Admin)** — FR-6.1: Admin shall generate **attendance, marks distribution, and pass percentage** reports (CSV/PDF).

**(iv) § 3. Specific Requirements — Non-Functional (1 mark)**

- **NFR-1 Performance**: Page load ≤ 2 s; auto-save ≤ 1 s.
- **NFR-2 Scalability**: 500 concurrent users with no degradation.
- **NFR-3 Security**: Passwords stored using **bcrypt (cost ≥ 12)**; all traffic via **TLS 1.2+**; CSRF tokens on all form submissions.
- **NFR-4 Availability**: 99.5% uptime during exam windows.
- **NFR-5 Reliability**: Zero data loss during exam; daily backups retained for 1 year.
- **NFR-6 Compliance**: IT Act 2000; university data privacy policy.

**(v) § 3.3 External Interfaces (sample) (extra credit)**
- UI: Responsive Bootstrap 5, WCAG 2.1 AA compliant.
- Software: MySQL 8 connector, Apache Tomcat 9.
- Communication: HTTPS on port 443.
- Hardware: Server — 16 GB RAM, 8 vCPUs, 500 GB SSD.

**Part (b) — Verification Attributes & Sample Use-Case (7 marks)**

**(i) Verification of each FR against IEEE 830 attributes (3.5 marks)** — Pick **FR-4.1** as worked example:

> *"OES shall display the exam only within the scheduled window."*

| Attribute | Verification |
|---|---|
| **Correct** | Matches the university's stated need: exams must be available only in the defined period. |
| **Unambiguous** | "Scheduled window" is defined as `start_time ≤ now ≤ end_time` — no room for misinterpretation. |
| **Complete** | Specifies both conditions: before start (block), after end (block), inside (allow). |
| **Consistent** | Does not conflict with FR-2.1 (scheduling) or FR-5 (grading). |
| **Verifiable** | Test: log in as student at T-1 min (denied), T+1 min (allowed), T_end+1 min (denied). |
| **Traceable** | Origin: "exam must be time-bound" in case study. ID FR-4.1 → UC-04 → ExamTimer Servlet. |
| **Modifiable** | Time-window rule centralised in `ExamAccessPolicy` class — easy to change. |
| **Ranked** | Priority: **High** (affects exam integrity). |

**(ii) Sample Use-Case: "Take Exam" (3.5 marks)**

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Use-Case ID** | UC-04 |
| **Name** | Take Exam |
| **Actor** | Student |
| **Pre-condition** | Student is registered and logged in; exam is scheduled and currently within its time window; student is assigned to the exam. |
| **Main Flow** | 1. Student selects "Take Exam" from dashboard. 2. OES verifies time window and assignment. 3. OES loads exam instructions and consent page. 4. Student clicks "Start". 5. OES displays questions one per page (or all on one page) with a **countdown timer**. 6. Student answers each question; OES **auto-saves every 30 s**. 7. Student clicks "Submit" OR timer reaches zero. 8. OES computes score for MCQs and stores descriptive answers. 9. OES displays "Submission successful" message. |
| **Post-condition** | Student's answers are stored; exam marked as **SUBMITTED**; timer invalidated. |
| **Alternate Flow A** | Network failure → OES caches answers locally and retries on reconnect. |
| **Alternate Flow B** | Student closes browser → on re-login, exam continues from last auto-save if within window. |
| **Exception Flow E1** | Student attempts to access exam outside window → OES shows **error E101: Exam not available**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Don't write design** (database schema, class diagrams) in the SRS — that belongs in the SDD. Marks deducted: **2–3**.
> - **Don't forget "shall"** — using "should/will" loses **0.5 per requirement**.
> - **Don't omit quantifiable NFRs** — "fast" or "secure" is not enough; use "≤ 2 s" and "TLS 1.2+".
> - **Don't skip the scope** (what is *not* in the system) — leaves the contract ambiguous.

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**"Design an SRS document for a 'Hospital Appointment Management System' (HAMS) following IEEE 830 standards. The system should allow patients to book/cancel appointments with doctors, doctors to view their schedule, and receptionists to manage slots and generate daily reports. Assume 200 OPD patients/day, web-based, deployed on Linux + MySQL 8 + Node.js. Provide all four sections of the SRS."**

#### Solution Outline (14 marks):

**Part (a) — SRS Document Sections 1, 2, and 3.1 (Functional Requirements) (7 marks)**

**§ 1. Introduction (2 marks)**
- **1.1 Purpose** — Define HAMS v1.0; audience: Hospital Admin, Doctors, Receptionists, Patients, Developers.
- **1.2 Conventions** — "shall" for mandates; priority H/M/L; ID pattern `FR-x.y`, `NFR-x`.
- **1.3 Intended Audience** — Receptionist reads § 3.1 booking-related; Doctor reads § 3.1 schedule-related.
- **1.4 Scope** — In: online appointment booking, slot management, schedule view, daily report. Out: telemedicine, in-patient (IPD) records, billing, pharmacy.
- **1.5 References** — IEEE 830-1998, ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission) standards, hospital's data policy.

**§ 2. Overall Description (2.5 marks)**
- **2.1 Product Perspective** — Standalone web app; integrates with existing HIS via REST API (read-only patient demographics).
- **2.2 Product Functions** — Book → Manage → View → Report.
- **2.3 User Classes**:
  | Class | Role | Skill |
  |---|---|---|
  | Patient | Book/cancel/view appointments | Basic web user |
  | Doctor | View daily schedule, mark availability | Moderate |
  | Receptionist | Create slots, override bookings, generate report | Trained staff |
  | Admin | Manage users, system config | IT-literate |
- **2.4 Operating Environment** — Node.js 18+, Express, MySQL 8, Nginx reverse proxy; client: any modern browser; mobile-responsive.
- **2.5 Constraints** — Comply with **ABDM** and **DISHA Act**; on-premise hosting in hospital data centre; no PII in logs.
- **2.6 Assumptions** — Patients have valid mobile number for OTP; Doctors available 9 AM–5 PM; HIS API is 99% available.
- **2.7 Dependencies** — HIS API for patient data; SMS gateway for OTP; hospital network uptime.

**§ 3.1 Functional Requirements (2.5 marks)** — Sample FRs:

- **FR-1: Patient Registration & OTP Login** — FR-1.1: Patient shall register with **name, mobile, email, age, gender**. FR-1.2: HAMS shall send a **6-digit OTP** valid for 5 minutes. FR-1.3: Patient shall be logged in upon successful OTP verification.
- **FR-2: View Doctor Directory** — FR-2.1: Patient shall be able to filter doctors by **department, availability date, and rating**.
- **FR-3: Book Appointment** — FR-3.1: Patient shall select a **doctor, date, and available slot**. FR-3.2: HAMS shall prevent **double-booking** by checking slot availability atomically. FR-3.3: HAMS shall send a **confirmation SMS** with appointment ID and token number.
- **FR-4: Cancel Appointment** — FR-4.1: Patient shall be able to cancel an appointment **up to 2 hours before slot time**. FR-4.2: On cancellation, the slot shall be **immediately released** for re-booking. FR-4.3: Repeated no-shows (≥ 3) shall **flag the patient** for receptionist review.
- **FR-5: Doctor Schedule View** — FR-5.1: Doctor shall see **today's appointments sorted by time** with patient name and reason. FR-5.2: Doctor shall be able to **block slots** for emergency or personal time.
- **FR-6: Slot Management (Receptionist)** — FR-6.1: Receptionist shall be able to **create recurring slot templates** (e.g., 15-min slots, 9 AM–1 PM, Mon–Sat). FR-6.2: Receptionist shall be able to **manually override** bookings in case of walk-ins.
- **FR-7: Daily Report** — FR-7.1: Receptionist shall generate a **daily OPD report** (total appointments, doctor-wise count, cancellations, no-shows) exportable to PDF.

**Part (b) — Non-Functional Requirements, External Interfaces, and Verification Sample (7 marks)**

**(i) Non-Functional Requirements Table (2.5 marks)**

| ID | Type | Requirement (must be quantitative) |
|---|---|---|
| NFR-1 | Performance | Page load ≤ 1.5 s; appointment confirmation SMS delivered ≤ 30 s. |
| NFR-2 | Availability | 99.9% uptime (max 43.8 min downtime/month). |
| NFR-3 | Scalability | Support **200 concurrent users** with p95 latency ≤ 2 s. |
| NFR-4 | Security | All PII encrypted at rest (**AES-256**); all traffic via **TLS 1.3**; OTPs expire in 5 min and are single-use. |
| NFR-5 | Usability | New patient shall book an appointment in ≤ 3 minutes (validated via usability test with 5 users). |
| NFR-6 | Reliability | Daily automated DB backup at 02:00 IST; RPO ≤ 24 h, RTO ≤ 2 h. |
| NFR-7 | Maintainability | 3-tier architecture; CI/CD pipeline; MTTR for critical bugs ≤ 4 h. |
| NFR-8 | Compliance | **DISHA Act**, **ABDM**, **IT Act 2000**; audit log retention ≥ 3 years. |

**(ii) External Interface Requirements (1.5 marks)**
- **User Interface** — Mobile-first responsive; **WCAG 2.1 AA**; support for English + Malayalam; support Chrome 100+ and Safari 15+.
- **Software Interface** — Backend: **Node.js 18**, **Express 4**; DB: **MySQL 8**; Cache: **Redis 7**; SMS: **Twilio/Gupshup API**; HIS: **RESTful JSON over HTTPS**.
- **Communication Interface** — All traffic **HTTPS port 443**; rate limit 100 req/min per IP.
- **Hardware Interface** — Server: 16 GB RAM, 4 vCPUs, 250 GB SSD; Client: any modern device.

**(iii) Verification Walk-through — Sample Requirement FR-3.2 (3 marks)**

> *"HAMS shall prevent double-booking by checking slot availability atomically."*

| Attribute | Justification (0.4 mark each) |
|---|---|
| **Correct** | Reflects the real-world need: two patients cannot occupy the same doctor's slot. |
| **Unambiguous** | "Atomically" is defined as a single DB transaction with row-level lock (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` in MySQL). |
| **Complete** | Covers the read-then-write race condition explicitly. |
| **Consistent** | Does not conflict with FR-6.2 (receptionist override) — override is an authorised exception. |
| **Verifiable** | Test: spawn 100 concurrent requests for the same slot → exactly **1 success + 99 failures**. |
| **Traceable** | Origin: case study mentions "no double booking"; mapped to UC-03; test TC-BOOK-007. |
| **Modifiable** | Slot-locking logic isolated in `SlotService.reserve()` — single point of change. |
| **Ranked** | Priority: **High** (data integrity critical). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Writing NFRs without numbers** — "system should be fast" is **worth 0 marks** for an NFR.
> - **Forgetting the "What it will NOT do" clause** in § 1.4 Scope — examiner specifically tests scope discipline.
> - **Putting design/architecture in the SRS** — e.g., "use React frontend, MongoDB database" in § 3.1 — these are constraints (§ 2.5), not requirements.
> - **Missing error codes** (E001, E101) — loses 0.5–1 mark per requirement.
> - **No traceability** — every FR must end up linked to a test case in the appendix.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Summary — Memorise These 12 Bullets:**

- ✅ **IEEE 830-1998** is the recommended standard for SRS. The document has **4 parts**: Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices.
- ✅ **SRS = WHAT**, **SDD = HOW**. Never describe architecture, algorithms, or DB schema in the SRS.
- ✅ Use the **"shall"** keyword for every mandatory requirement. Avoid "should", "may", "could", "will".
- ✅ Every requirement needs a **unique ID** (FR-1.1, NFR-2) for **traceability**.
- ✅ Separate **Functional Requirements (FR)** from **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)**. Use tables, not paragraphs.
- ✅ **8 Verification Attributes**: Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable — recall as **"C-U-C-R-V-M-T"**.
- ✅ **NFRs must be quantitative** — always include a number (e.g., ≤ 2 s, 99.9% uptime, AES-256, MTBF ≥ 720 h).
- ✅ Always include the **"What the system will NOT do"** clause in § 1.4 Scope.
- ✅ **User Classes** in § 2.3 must list **privileges and frequency of use** — not just names.
- ✅ **External Interfaces** = User + Hardware + Software + Communication — all four categories.
- ✅ Always include **Assumptions and Dependencies** in § 2.6 — they prevent contractual disputes.
- ✅ A good SRS uses **multiple notations** (natural language + use cases + ER diagram + state diagrams) — IEEE 830 § 3.5.

> **End of Notes — PCCNL607 CASE LAB | Module Experiment 2 | IEEE 830 SRS Document Design**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw the use case diagram and specify the role of each of the actors. Also state the precondition, post condition and function of each use case
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw the use case diagram and specify the role of each of the actors. Also state the precondition, post condition and function of each use case

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Use Case Diagram** is a behavioral diagram in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) that belongs to the family of *structural* (or, more precisely, *interaction-overview*) diagrams used in the requirements elicitation phase of software engineering. It captures the **functional requirements** of a system by graphically depicting the set of *use cases* (functions or services the system provides), the *actors* (external entities that interact with the system), and the **relationships** that connect them — all enclosed within a defined *system boundary*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCNL607 – CASE LAB)**
> "A Use Case Diagram is a UML diagram that describes the proposed functionality of a new system. It represents the actors, use cases, and their interactions in a single graphical notation."

**Lexical Building Blocks:**

| Term | UML Notation | Meaning |
|------|--------------|---------|
| **Actor** | Stick figure (or classifier with `<<actor>>` stereotype) | An external entity (human user, hardware device, or another system) that interacts with the system under consideration |
| **Use Case** | Ellipse / Oval | A specific, meaningful piece of functionality the system delivers to an actor |
| **System Boundary** | Rectangle enclosing use cases | Defines the scope of the system being modelled |
| **Association** | Solid line | A direct communication link between an actor and a use case |
| **Include** | Dashed arrow labelled `<<include>>` | Mandatory reuse — one use case always invokes another |
| **Extend** | Dashed arrow labelled `<<extend>>` | Optional/conditional behaviour inserted into a base use case |
| **Generalization** | Solid line with hollow triangle | Inheritance — child actor or child use case inherits parent behaviour |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Theatre Play" View

Imagine the software system as a **theatrical play** being staged inside a closed auditorium (the *system boundary*).

- **Actors** = the **characters** walking on stage and interacting with the play (e.g., the Hero, the Villain, the Banker).
- **Use Cases** = the **scenes** the play enacts (e.g., "The Loan Scene", "The Chase Scene"). Each scene delivers one self-contained story fragment.
- **System Boundary** = the **auditorium walls** — the play runs *inside* these walls, and the characters enter from *outside*.
- **Include** = a **sub-scene** that *must* happen inside the main scene (e.g., the "Hero Authentication Scene" always precedes the "Hero Withdraws Money Scene").
- **Extend** = a **bonus scene** played *only under certain conditions* (e.g., the "Receipt Printing Scene" extends "Withdraw Money" only if the customer presses the receipt button).

Just as a play’s *script* lists what each character does in each scene, a **Use Case Specification** documents what each actor can do, the **precondition** that must hold *before* the use case starts, and the **post-condition** that will hold *after* it successfully completes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Components of a Use Case Specification (as per KTU & Sommerville):**
> 1. **Pre-condition** — what must be *true before* the use case begins.
> 2. **Post-condition** — what will be *true after* the use case completes successfully.
> 3. **Function / Main Flow** — the step-by-step dialogue between actor and system.

## 1.3 Why Use Case Diagrams Matter in CASE Lab

In KTU’s CASE Lab, this experiment sits in **Module 2 – Requirement Modelling**, immediately after Module 1 (SDLC & requirement gathering). The use case diagram is the **first UML artefact** that bridges informal requirements (textual SRS) and formal design (class, sequence, activity diagrams). Mastering it is a direct assessment of **CO2 (Model requirements using UML diagrams)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Weighting Hint:** In a typical 14-mark Module-2 viva/lab-record question, expect **5 marks** for the diagram, **4 marks** for actor identification and role description, and **5 marks** for use-case specifications (pre/post/function).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Use Case Diagram — Structured Breakdown

A complete use case model consists of **two deliverables**:

1. **The Diagram (graphical view)** — shows *who* can do *what*.
2. **The Specification (textual view)** — describes *how* each use case behaves.

### 2.1.1 Identifying Actors (The "WHO")

Actors are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

- **By Origin:**
  - **Primary Actor** — initiates the use case; has a *goal* (e.g., *Customer* wanting to withdraw cash).
  - **Secondary Actor** — provides a service to the system; does *not* initiate (e.g., *Bank Server* authorizing the transaction).
- **By Type:**
  - **Human Actor** — represented as stick figure (Customer, Admin, Teller).
  - **Non-Human Actor** — represented as stick figure with `<<actor>>` stereotype or as a classifier (Payment Gateway, SMTP Server, Database).
- **By Cardinality:**
  - One instance (e.g., a single logged-in user).
  - Many instances (e.g., multiple concurrent ATM users).

### 2.1.2 Identifying Use Cases (The "WHAT")

A robust technique is the **"Actor–Goal List"** method:

1. List every actor.
2. For each actor, ask: *"What goal does this actor want to achieve from the system?"*
3. Each goal ⇒ one candidate use case (ellipse).

Use cases must be expressed as **verb–noun phrases** in the **active voice**: `Withdraw Cash`, not `Cash Withdrawal by Customer`.

### 2.1.3 Specifying Use Cases — The "HOW"

The KTU-endorsed template (synthesised from Cockburn and Sommerville) contains the following fields:

| # | Field | Purpose |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Use Case Name** | Verb–noun identifier |
| 2 | **Actor(s)** | Who participates |
| 3 | **Precondition** | State that must hold *before* execution |
| 4 | **Post-condition** | State guaranteed *after* success |
| 5 | **Main Flow (Function)** | Step-by-step happy path |
| 6 | **Alternative Flow** | Optional or variant branches |
| 7 | **Exception Flow** | Error / failure handling |

## 2.2 The Three Pillars: Precondition, Post-condition, Function — Formal Semantics

Let $U$ denote a use case. We define three predicates:

- $P(U)$ = precondition predicate (input contract)
- $Q(U)$ = post-condition predicate (output contract — only true if use case *succeeds*)
- $F(U)$ = function / main flow (the algorithmic dialogue)

The **Design by Contract** principle (B. Meyer) demands:

$$ P(U) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{execute } F(U) \;\Rightarrow\; Q(U) \;\; \lor \;\; \text{Exception} $$

In words: *If the precondition holds when the use case is invoked, then upon (successful) completion of the function flow, the post-condition will hold. Otherwise, an exception is raised.*

| Element | Holds When? | Example (Withdraw Cash) |
|---------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Precondition** | Just *before* `F(U)` starts | `Authenticated = true` **AND** `Balance ≥ Amount` |
| **Post-condition** | Just *after* `F(U)` ends successfully | `Balance' = Balance − Amount` **AND** `Cash dispensed = Amount` |
| **Function** | The dialogue itself | Steps 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 (insert card → enter PIN → select amount → take cash) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Rule:** Preconditions describe *system state*, not user steps. "User inserts card" is a *trigger*, not a precondition. A valid precondition is `Customer_Authenticated = TRUE`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Notation Cheat Sheet

| Notation | UML Symbol | When to Use |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| Association | Solid line | An actor **directly participates** in a use case |
| `<<include>>` | Dashed arrow + label | Use case A **always invokes** use case B (mandatory reuse) |
| `<<extend>>` | Dashed arrow + label | Use case B **optionally extends** use case A under a condition |
| Generalization | Hollow triangle arrow | Sub-actor / sub-use-case **inherits** parent behaviour |
| System Boundary | Rectangle | Encloses all use cases to **show scope** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Requirements Engineering:** Captures the *what* before the *how*.
- **Test Case Derivation:** Each main flow step ⇒ one black-box test case.
- **Stakeholder Communication:** Non-technical clients can read use-case specs.
- **Traceability:** Bridges SRS → Design → Test artefacts in CASE tools (Rational Rose, StarUML, Visual Paradigm, Lucidchart).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction Methodology & Worked Example

## 3.1 The Five-Step Procedure (KTU Board-Standard Methodology)

This is the exact procedure examiners expect students to write in lab records:

### **Step 1 — Identify the System Scope**
Write a one-line problem statement:
> *"The ATM System enables a bank customer to perform basic banking transactions using a debit card and PIN authentication."*

### **Step 2 — Identify Actors**
Apply the **Actor–Goal List** method:

| Actor | Type | Goal (becomes a Use Case) |
|-------|------|----------------------------|
| Customer | Primary, Human | Authenticate self, Withdraw, Deposit, Check Balance, Transfer, Print Statement, Change PIN |
| Bank Server | Secondary, Non-Human | Authorize transactions, Validate PIN, Deduct balance |

### **Step 3 — Identify Use Cases**
Convert each goal into a verb–noun phrase:
`Login`, `Check Balance`, `Withdraw Cash`, `Deposit Cash`, `Transfer Funds`, `Print Statement`, `Change PIN`.

### **Step 4 — Draw the Diagram**
Place system boundary, place use cases *inside*, place actors *outside*, connect with associations. Add `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>` where applicable.

### **Step 5 — Write Use Case Specifications**
For **every** use case (or at minimum the top 4 in a 14-mark answer), write Pre-condition, Post-condition, and Function.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Use Case Specification for `Withdraw Cash`

Below is a fully worked-out specification that scores full marks in a KTU lab exam.

### **Use Case ID:** UC-03
### **Use Case Name:** Withdraw Cash

**Actors:**
- *Customer* (Primary)
- *Bank Server* (Secondary — supports the transaction)

**Pre-condition:**
$$ P(\text{Withdraw}) \;:\; (\text{Customer\_Authenticated} = \text{TRUE}) \;\land\; (\text{Balance} \geq \text{Amount}) \;\land\; (\text{Card\_Valid} = \text{TRUE}) $$

**Post-condition (on success):**
$$ Q(\text{Withdraw}) \;:\; (\text{Balance}' = \text{Balance} - \text{Amount}) \;\land\; (\text{Cash\_Dispensed} = \text{Amount}) \;\land\; (\text{Transaction\_Logged} = \text{TRUE}) $$

**Function — Main Flow (Step-by-Step Dialogue):**

| Step | Actor | Action | System Response |
|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Customer | Inserts debit card into ATM slot | ATM reads card, requests PIN |
| 2 | Customer | Enters 4-digit PIN | System validates PIN via Bank Server |
| 3 | Customer | Selects "Withdraw Cash" option | ATM displays amount selection menu |
| 4 | Customer | Enters amount (multiples of 100) | System checks balance with Bank Server |
| 5 | Customer | Confirms transaction | System debits account, dispenses cash |
| 6 | Customer | Takes cash | ATM prints receipt (optional) |

**Alternative Flow:**
- **3a.** If customer selects "Fast Cash" → skip Step 4 (preset amounts).
- **6a.** If customer presses "Print Receipt" → extend with `Print Statement` use case.

**Exception Flow:**
- Invalid PIN → display error, return card after 3 failed attempts.
- Insufficient balance → display "Insufficient Funds", abort.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Use Case × Specification Summary Table

The following matrix is the **high-density revision artefact** for the exam. Memorize the structure; the actual values change per problem.

| Use Case | Pre-condition | Post-condition | Primary Function |
|----------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Login** | Card inserted, PIN entered | `Authenticated = TRUE`, session started | Validate PIN, start session |
| **Check Balance** | `Authenticated = TRUE` | Balance displayed on screen | Query Bank Server, render balance |
| **Withdraw Cash** | `Authenticated` ∧ `Balance ≥ Amount` | `Balance' = Balance − Amount`, cash dispensed | Debit account, dispense currency |
| **Deposit Cash** | `Authenticated` ∧ envelope valid | `Balance' = Balance + Amount`, slip printed | Credit account, log transaction |
| **Transfer Funds** | `Authenticated` ∧ valid beneficiary | Source debited, beneficiary credited | Initiate NEFT/IMPS via Bank Server |
| **Print Statement** | `Authenticated` | Mini-statement printed/displayed | Fetch last N transactions, format |
| **Change PIN** | `Authenticated` ∧ old PIN entered | `PIN' = New PIN`, old PIN invalidated | Update PIN in Bank Server |

## 3.4 Construction Logic for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`

```
Decision Logic:
    IF  use case A ALWAYS needs use case B to complete its work
            THEN A  ---- <<include>> ---->  B
    IF  use case B is OPTIONAL and only triggers under a CONDITION in A
            THEN B  ---- <<extend>> ---->  A
```

**Applied to the ATM example:**

- `Withdraw Cash` `<<include>>` `Login` → mandatory; cannot withdraw without authenticating.
- `Deposit Cash` `<<include>>` `Login` → mandatory.
- `Print Statement` `<<extend>>` `Withdraw Cash` → optional, only if customer requests a receipt.
- `Customer` *generalizes* `Privileged Customer` → inherited actor for special use cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Drawing `<<include>>` in the wrong direction. The arrow goes **from the base use case TO the included use case** (base ⇒ included). Reversing it loses 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Use Case Diagram — ATM System (Board-Exam Standard)

The diagram below mirrors what students must draw in StarUML / draw.io / Lucidchart during the lab exam. Stick figures (actors) are rendered as circular nodes, use cases as rectangles inside the system boundary, dashed arrows for `<<include>>`/`<<extend>>`, and solid lines for associations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef actorStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#5B2C0F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef usecaseStyle fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef boundaryStyle fill:#FEF9E7,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    A1((Customer))
    A2((Bank Server))

    subgraph ATMSys ["ATM System Boundary"]
        direction TB
        UC1["Login"]
        UC2["Check Balance"]
        UC3["Withdraw Cash"]
        UC4["Deposit Cash"]
        UC5["Transfer Funds"]
        UC6["Print Statement"]
        UC7["Change PIN"]
    end

    A1 === UC1
    A1 === UC2
    A1 === UC3
    A1 === UC4
    A1 === UC5
    A1 === UC6
    A1 === UC7

    A2 -.-> UC1
    A2 -.-> UC3
    A2 -.-> UC4
    A2 -.-> UC5

    UC3 -.->|includes| UC1
    UC4 -.->|includes| UC1
    UC5 -.->|includes| UC1

    UC6 -.->|extends| UC3
    UC6 -.->|extends| UC4

    class A1,A2 actorStyle
    class UC1,UC2,UC3,UC4,UC5,UC6,UC7 usecaseStyle
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU 2024 lab viva answers):**

| Element in Diagram | UML Meaning | Viva-Voce One-Liner |
|--------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Stick figure `Customer` | Primary actor | "The customer initiates transactions" |
| Stick figure `Bank Server` | Secondary actor | "Bank Server authorizes and validates" |
| Rectangle `ATM System Boundary` | Scope of the system | "Defines what is *inside* our system" |
| Solid line `Customer — Login` | Direct association | "Customer directly participates" |
| Dashed arrow `Withdraw → Login (includes)` | Mandatory reuse | "Withdrawal cannot happen without Login" |
| Dashed arrow `Print → Withdraw (extends)` | Optional extension | "Receipt prints only if requested" |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — From Requirement to Specification

This second diagram shows the **process flow** a CASE Lab student should follow when answering any "draw and specify" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stepStyle fill:#E8F8F5,stroke:#0E6251,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outStyle fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:2px

    S1["Step 1: Read Problem Statement"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify Actors using Actor-Goal List"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify Use Cases as Verb-Noun Phrases"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Identify Relationships association include extend"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Draw Diagram with System Boundary"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Write Pre-condition for each Use Case"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Write Function Main Flow Steps"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Write Post-condition on Success"]
    S8 --> OUT["Deliverable: Use Case Model Diagram plus Specifications"]

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8 stepStyle
    class OUT outStyle
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Always draw the system boundary rectangle **first**, then place use cases inside, then actors outside. Examiners check this sequence — drawing actors first often results in messy, unscoped diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]
**Define a Use Case Diagram. List any FOUR components of a use case diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Use Case Diagram is a UML behavioural diagram that depicts the functional requirements of a system by showing actors, use cases, and their relationships within a system boundary. *(1 mark)*
The four components are: *(½ mark each)*
1. **Actor** — external entity interacting with the system.
2. **Use Case** — a piece of functionality the system provides.
3. **System Boundary** — rectangle that defines the scope of the system.
4. **Relationships** — association, `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`, generalization.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in a use case diagram with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **Meaning** | Mandatory reuse of another use case | Optional addition under a condition |
| **Direction of Arrow** | Base ⇒ Included | Extender ⇒ Base |
| **Trigger** | Always executed when base runs | Only when a defined condition is met |
| **Example** | `Withdraw Cash` `<<include>>` `Login` | `Print Statement` `<<extend>>` `Withdraw Cash` (only if customer requests receipt) |

*(1 mark for each correct difference, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — 14 Marks *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]* 

**For an Online Book Shopping System, perform the following:**
**(a)** Identify all actors and draw a complete Use Case Diagram with at least 7 use cases, system boundary, and one `<<include>>` and one `<<extend>>` relationship. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Specify the **Pre-condition, Post-condition and Function** for any TWO use cases. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Actors and Diagram (7 marks)**

**Identified Actors:**
| Actor | Type | Role |
|-------|------|------|
| Customer | Primary, Human | Browses, searches, buys books |
| Admin | Primary, Human | Manages inventory, views reports |
| Payment Gateway | Secondary, Non-Human | Processes payments |
| Delivery System | Secondary, Non-Human | Ships orders |

**Identified Use Cases:** `Register`, `Login`, `Search Books`, `Add to Cart`, `Place Order`, `Make Payment`, `Track Order`, `Manage Inventory`.

**Diagram (rendered):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef actorStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#5B2C0F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecaseStyle fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px

    A1((Customer))
    A2((Admin))
    A3((Payment Gateway))
    A4((Delivery System))

    subgraph OBSys ["Online Book Store System"]
        direction TB
        UC1["Register"]
        UC2["Login"]
        UC3["Search Books"]
        UC4["Add to Cart"]
        UC5["Place Order"]
        UC6["Make Payment"]
        UC7["Track Order"]
        UC8["Manage Inventory"]
    end

    A1 === UC1
    A1 === UC2
    A1 === UC3
    A1 === UC4
    A1 === UC5
    A1 === UC6
    A1 === UC7
    A2 === UC2
    A2 === UC8
    A3 -.-> UC6
    A4 -.-> UC7

    UC5 -.->|includes| UC2
    UC6 -.->|includes| UC2
    UC5 -.->|includes| UC6
    UC7 -.->|extends| UC5

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 actorStyle
    class UC1,UC2,UC3,UC4,UC5,UC6,UC7,UC8 usecaseStyle
```

*(7 marks: 2 for actors, 3 for diagram correctness, 2 for relationships)*

#### **Part (b) — Specifications for TWO Use Cases (7 marks)**

**Use Case 1: `Place Order` (3.5 marks)**

| Field | Specification |
|-------|---------------|
| **Pre-condition** | `Customer_Authenticated = TRUE` ∧ `Cart_Not_Empty = TRUE` ∧ `Items_In_Stock = TRUE` |
| **Post-condition** | `Order_Created = TRUE` ∧ `Cart_Emptied = TRUE` ∧ `Inventory_Decremented = TRUE` |
| **Function (Main Flow)** | 1. Customer reviews cart → 2. Selects "Place Order" → 3. System displays shipping address → 4. Customer confirms → 5. System creates order (triggers `<<include>>` Make Payment) → 6. Order confirmation displayed |

**Use Case 2: `Make Payment` (3.5 marks)**

| Field | Specification |
|-------|---------------|
| **Pre-condition** | `Order_Created = TRUE` ∧ `Payment_Method_Valid = TRUE` |
| **Post-condition** | `Payment_Received = TRUE` ∧ `Order_Status = Confirmed` |
| **Function (Main Flow)** | 1. System invokes Payment Gateway → 2. Customer enters card/UPI details → 3. Gateway validates → 4. System receives success → 5. Updates order status to "Confirmed" → 6. Sends confirmation email |

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** — 14 Marks *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**For a Hospital Management System:**
**(a)** Draw the Use Case Diagram showing at least 6 use cases and 3 actors, and write the **role of each actor**. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Write the **Pre-condition, Post-condition and Function** for `Book Appointment` and `Generate Bill` use cases. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (Key Highlights)**

**Actors and Roles (7 marks):**

| Actor | Role |
|-------|------|
| **Patient** | Registers, books appointments, views medical reports, pays bills |
| **Doctor** | Views appointments, updates diagnosis, prescribes medicines |
| **Receptionist** | Manages appointments, registers patients, generates bills |
| **Insurance System** *(secondary)* | Validates insurance claims, settles payments |

**Diagram (rendered):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef actorStyle fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#7E5109,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecaseStyle fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:2px

    A1((Patient))
    A2((Doctor))
    A3((Receptionist))
    A4((Insurance System))

    subgraph HSys ["Hospital Management System"]
        direction TB
        UC1["Register Patient"]
        UC2["Login"]
        UC3["Book Appointment"]
        UC4["View Medical Report"]
        UC5["Update Diagnosis"]
        UC6["Generate Bill"]
    end

    A1 === UC1
    A1 === UC2
    A1 === UC3
    A1 === UC4
    A2 === UC2
    A2 === UC4
    A2 === UC5
    A3 === UC1
    A3 === UC3
    A3 === UC6
    A4 -.-> UC6

    UC3 -.->|includes| UC2
    UC5 -.->|includes| UC2
    UC6 -.->|extends| UC3

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 actorStyle
    class UC1,UC2,UC3,UC4,UC5,UC6 usecaseStyle
```

**Specifications (7 marks):**

`Book Appointment` — **Pre:** `Patient_Authenticated = TRUE` ∧ `Doctor_Available = TRUE`. **Post:** `Appointment_Booked = TRUE` ∧ `Slot_Reserved = TRUE`. **Function:** Patient logs in → selects specialty → chooses doctor → picks slot → confirms → receives confirmation SMS.

`Generate Bill` — **Pre:** `Treatment_Completed = TRUE` ∧ `Patient_Registered = TRUE`. **Post:** `Bill_Generated = TRUE` ∧ `Payment_Status = Pending/Paid`. **Function:** Receptionist selects patient → system aggregates charges → applies insurance → displays total → prints bill.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing System Boundary** — *−2 marks*. Always draw the enclosing rectangle.
> 2. **Writing "User clicks button" as a Pre-condition** — *−1 mark*. Pre-conditions are *system states*, not user actions.
> 3. **Reversing `<<include>>` arrow direction** — *−2 marks*. Base ⇒ Included, never the reverse.
> 4. **Confusing post-condition with output data** — *−1 mark*. Post-condition is the *new state*, not the *returned value*.
> 5. **Using nouns instead of verb–noun phrases for use cases** (e.g., "Cash Withdrawal") — *−1 mark*. Correct: "Withdraw Cash".
> 6. **Forgetting to label Secondary Actors** — *−1 mark*. Always mention non-human actors (Payment Gateway, SMTP, Database).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Use Case Diagram** = UML artefact for **functional requirements**; bridges informal SRS and formal design.
- **Four mandatory components:** Actor, Use Case, System Boundary, Relationships.
- **Actors** can be **Primary** (initiates goal) or **Secondary** (supports), **Human** or **Non-Human**.
- **Use case names** must be **verb–noun phrases in active voice** (e.g., `Withdraw Cash`, not `Cash Withdrawal`).
- **System Boundary** is a rectangle that **encloses all use cases**; actors stay **outside**.
- **Three specifications per use case** (KTU-mandatory):
  1. **Pre-condition** — state *before* the function runs.
  2. **Post-condition** — state *after* successful completion.
  3. **Function (Main Flow)** — step-by-step dialogue between actor and system.
- **`<<include>>`** = mandatory reuse; arrow **from base to included**.
- **`<<extend>>`** = optional/conditional behaviour; arrow **from extender to base**.
- **Generalization** = inheritance; solid line with hollow triangle.
- **Design by Contract rule:**
$$ P(U) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{execute } F(U) \;\Rightarrow\; Q(U) \;\; \lor \;\; \text{Exception} $$
- **Use Case Specifications** derive **test cases** (one main-flow step ⇒ one test case) — a direct CASE Lab CO mapping.
- **Tools of choice:** StarUML, Lucidchart, draw.io, Visual Paradigm, Rational Rose — any will do; examiner only checks the **final diagram**.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Missing boundary rectangle.
  - Wrong arrow direction for include/extend.
  - Pre-condition phrased as user action.
  - One use case per CRUD operation (group them).
  - Missing actor roles in textual answer.
- **Mark split to remember:** Diagram ≈ **40%**, Actor identification ≈ **25%**, Use case specifications ≈ **35%** of the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw the activity diagram for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw the activity diagram for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity Diagram** is a behavioral diagram in **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** that represents the dynamic aspects of a system by modeling the **flow of control** from one activity to another. It is essentially a **flowchart** enhanced with features that support parallel behavior, alternative paths, object flows, and swimlane-based responsibility partitioning.

According to the **Object Management Group (OMG) UML 2.5 specification** (the standard KTU follows), an activity diagram describes:

- The **sequence of activities** (atomic computations or operations).
- **Conditional and looping** control flow.
- **Concurrent (parallel) execution** using fork/join nodes.
- **Object flow** — the movement of data/objects between activities.
- **Swimlanes** — the responsibility allocation across organizational units (actors, classes, subsystems).

**Mathematical representation of control flow:**

$$CF = (A, T, D, F, J, I, F_n)$$

where:
- $A$ = Set of **Activity nodes** (rounded rectangles)
- $T$ = Set of **Transition edges** (directed arrows)
- $D$ = Set of **Decision/Merge nodes** (diamonds)
- $F$ = Set of **Fork nodes** (splitting concurrent flows)
- $J$ = Set of **Join nodes** (synchronizing concurrent flows)
- $I$ = Initial node (single filled circle)
- $F_n$ = Set of Final/Flow-final nodes (bullseye / circle with X)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Patient Journey

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Think of an **Activity Diagram** as the **patient journey map drawn on the wall of a hospital**. The patient (control) starts at the *Reception* (initial node), goes through *Doctor Consultation → Lab Tests → Diagnosis → Pharmacy* (activities), and ends at *Discharge* (final node). When two tests like *Blood Test* and *X-Ray* are done **in parallel** (fork), the patient waits at a *Wait for Both Reports* (join) before the doctor continues. The *Swimlanes* are the departments (Reception, Pathology, Radiology, Billing) — each responsible for activities in their lane.

This is exactly what an activity diagram captures for a software system:
- **Activities** = processing steps.
- **Decisions** = "Is balance sufficient?" (Yes/No).
- **Forks/Joins** = parallel tasks.
- **Swimlanes** = who does what.

---

## 1.3 Why Activity Diagrams? (Engineering Utility)

| Use Case | How Activity Diagram Helps |
|---|---|
| **Business Process Modeling** | Captures workflows independent of object-orientation. |
| **Use-Case Elaboration** | Refines the procedural logic inside a use case. |
| **Algorithm Design** | Visually traces parallel/distributed algorithms. |
| **Embedded / RTOS systems** | Models concurrent task scheduling. |
| **Workflow Software (BPM)** | Direct mapping to BPMN concepts. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When asked "What is the difference between a flowchart and an activity diagram?" — say: *"A flowchart is **program-centric**; an activity diagram is **system/business-centric** and additionally supports **swimlanes, object flow, signal handling, and parallel execution**."*

---

## 1.4 KTU-Centric Learning Outcomes (CO Mapping)

After completing this experiment, the student should be able to (mapped to PCCNL607 COs):

- **CO1:** Identify the activities, decisions, and parallel flows in a given problem statement.
- **CO2:** Apply UML 2.5 standard notation to construct a syntactically correct activity diagram.
- **CO3:** Justify the choice of control nodes (decision, fork, join, merge) for a real-world case study.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

## 2.1 Core Building Blocks of an Activity Diagram

Every activity diagram is composed of **nodes** (activities, control nodes, object nodes) and **edges** (transitions/flow arrows). The standard **UML 2.5** notation is as follows.

### 2.1.1 Activity / Action Node
- **Shape:** Rounded rectangle.
- **Symbol:** `Activity Name`
- **Purpose:** Represents one step of work (atomic or composite).
- **Example:** `Validate User Credentials`, `Deduct Balance`.

### 2.1.2 Initial Node
- **Shape:** Small **filled black circle** (●).
- **Symbol:** `●` (a single dot, no label).
- **Purpose:** Marks the **start** of the activity. Exactly **one per diagram** (best practice).

### 2.1.3 Activity Final Node
- **Shape:** Bullseye — a filled black circle inside an outer ring (⊙).
- **Symbol:** `⊙`
- **Purpose:** Marks the **end** of **all flows** in the entire activity. When reached, the entire process terminates.

### 2.1.4 Flow Final Node
- **Shape:** Circle with a **cross (X)** inside (⊗).
- **Symbol:** `⊗`
- **Purpose:** Ends a **single alternate path** (without terminating the whole activity). Used in decision branches where "one path can finish early."

### 2.1.5 Decision Node
- **Shape:** Diamond (◇).
- **Symbol:** `◇`
- **Purpose:** Single incoming, **multiple outgoing** edges with **guard conditions** like `[balance > 0]`, `[is_valid = true]`.
- **Guard Condition Format:** `[condition] / label` on the outgoing edge.

### 2.1.6 Merge Node
- **Shape:** Diamond (◇) — same notation as decision.
- **Purpose:** Multiple incoming, **single outgoing** edge. Combines alternate paths back into one.
- **Note:** Decision and merge share the same shape but are distinguished by **edge direction** (decision = 1-in, n-out; merge = n-in, 1-out).

### 2.1.7 Fork Node
- **Shape:** Thick black **horizontal or vertical bar** (▬).
- **Purpose:** One incoming edge, **multiple outgoing** parallel edges. Activities on the outgoing edges execute **concurrently**.

### 2.1.8 Join Node
- **Shape:** Thick black bar (▬) — same as fork.
- **Purpose:** Multiple incoming parallel edges, **single outgoing** edge. The outgoing edge is taken **only after ALL incoming flows arrive** (synchronization barrier).

### 2.1.9 Swimlane (Partition)
- **Shape:** Vertical or horizontal **labeled columns/rows** partitioning the diagram.
- **Purpose:** Assigns each activity to a **responsible entity** (Actor, Class, Subsystem, Department).
- **Two types:**
  - **Lane** — single partition (e.g., one role).
  - **Partition** — group of lanes (e.g., entire "Customer" group).

### 2.1.10 Object Node
- **Shape:** Rectangle (NOT rounded).
- **Symbol:** `objectName : ClassName` (can show state in brackets `[state]`).
- **Purpose:** Shows **data flow** — an object passed between activities.
- **Example:** `order : Order [approved]`.

### 2.1.11 Note
- **Shape:** Dog-eared rectangle (folded corner).
- **Purpose:** Annotations for clarification — does not affect flow.

---
