## 2.2 KTU Notation Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table. In KTU 2024 lab exams, examiners often deduct marks for **incorrect shapes** (e.g., drawing a decision as a circle).

| S.No | Element | UML Shape | Notation | Outgoing Edges | Incoming Edges |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Node | Filled black circle | `●` | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | Activity Final | Bullseye | `⊙` | 0 | $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | Flow Final | Circle with X | `⊗` | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | Activity | Rounded rectangle | `Name` | 1 (or 0 if final) | 1 (or 0 if initial) |
| 5 | Decision | Diamond | `◇` | $\geq 2$ with guards | 1 |
| 6 | Merge | Diamond | `◇` | 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| 7 | Fork | Thick bar | `▬` | $\geq 2$ | 1 |
| 8 | Join | Thick bar | `▬` | 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| 9 | Object Node | Rectangle | `obj : Class` | $\geq 1$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | Swimlane | Rectangular partition | (Lane) | — | — |
| 11 | Note | Dog-eared box | `Note text` | — | dashed connector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guard Condition Rule:** Every outgoing edge from a decision node **must** have a guard in `[ ]`. The keyword `else` may be used as a default branch.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical / Logical Foundations

### 2.3.1 Guard-Condition Completeness

For a decision node with $n$ outgoing edges carrying guards $g_1, g_2, \dots, g_n$, the KTU-recommended best practice is:

$$g_1 \lor g_2 \lor \dots \lor g_n = \text{True (mutually exclusive optional)}$$

This ensures **exactly one branch is taken** at runtime, avoiding undefined behavior.

### 2.3.2 Fork-Join Concurrency Model

For a fork node splitting into $k$ parallel flows, the join node synchronizes them. The **latency** from fork to join is:

$$T_{join} = \max(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_k)$$

where $T_i$ is the execution time of the $i^{th}$ parallel branch. This is the classic **critical-path** concept in concurrent systems.

### 2.3.3 Object Flow as a Channel

When an object $o$ is created at activity $A_1$ and consumed at activity $A_2$, the object node acts as a **buffer**:

$$B = \text{tokens in buffer} = \sum_{t} \text{productions} - \sum_{t} \text{consumptions}$$

In UML 2.5, the **upper bound** can be specified as `{upperBound = n}` to model a bounded buffer.

---

## 2.4 When to Use Each Control Node — Decision Logic

```
Problem Statement
        │
        ▼
Is there a Yes/No choice?  ──Yes──► Use DECISION node
        │ No
        ▼
Are multiple paths returning to one point?  ──Yes──► Use MERGE node
        │ No
        ▼
Do activities run simultaneously?  ──Yes──► Use FORK + JOIN
        │ No
        ▼
Is it just a sequential step?  ──Yes──► Plain ACTIVITY
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** In viva, if asked *"What if fork has no join?"* — answer: *"The system exhibits **unbounded concurrency**; the final node must wait for all un-joined threads. In UML, this is allowed but discouraged due to non-determinism."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodology & Worked Case Studies

## 3.1 The KTU-Recommended Procedure to Draw an Activity Diagram

Follow this **5-step algorithm** (this is what examiners expect to see written in your lab record):

### Step 1 — **Identify Use Case / Scenario**
- Read the problem statement carefully.
- Select **one use case** or **one business process** (not the whole system).
- Example: "Customer withdraws cash from ATM."

### Step 2 — **Enumerate Activities**
- List every step in sequence.
- Use **action verbs** in present tense.
- Example: Insert Card → Read Card → Validate PIN → Choose Operation → ...

### Step 3 — **Identify Control Flow Nodes**
- **Decisions:** Find all conditional questions.
- **Parallelism:** Find all "simultaneously" or "in parallel" statements.
- **Loops:** Find any "repeat until" patterns.

### Step 4 — **Assign Swimlanes (if applicable)**
- Identify who is responsible for each activity.
- Common lanes: Customer, System, Bank Server, Database.

### Step 5 — **Draw Using UML Notation**
- Place initial node (top-left).
- Draw top-to-bottom (best practice).
- Use proper shapes from the cheat sheet.
- Apply guards `[condition]` on decision branches.
- Place a single activity-final node (bottom).

---

## 3.2 Worked Case Study 1: ATM Cash Withdrawal

### Problem Statement
> A customer inserts a card, enters the PIN, chooses "Withdraw", enters the amount, and the ATM dispenses cash if the balance is sufficient; otherwise, it shows an error and asks the customer to retry or cancel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify Scenario**
Single use case: *Withdraw Cash*.

**Step 2: Enumerate Activities (in plain text)**

```
1. Insert Card
2. Read Card Details
3. Enter PIN
4. Validate PIN
5. Display Menu
6. Select "Withdraw"
7. Enter Amount
8. Check Balance
9. Dispense Cash
10. Print Receipt
11. Eject Card
12. End Session
```

**Step 3: Identify Control Nodes**

| Decision Point | Branch | Guard |
|---|---|---|
| Validate PIN | Correct / Incorrect | `[pin = stored]`, `[else]` |
| Check Balance | Sufficient / Insufficient | `[balance $\geq$ amount]`, `[else]` |
| After "Insufficient" | Retry / Cancel | `[retry]`, `[cancel]` |

**Step 4: Assign Swimlanes**
- **Customer Lane** — Insert Card, Enter PIN, Enter Amount, Take Cash.
- **ATM Lane** — Read Card, Display Menu, Dispense Cash, Print Receipt.
- **Bank Server Lane** — Validate PIN, Check Balance, Deduct Amount.

**Step 5: Draw the Activity Diagram**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1(["Start ●"]) --> B1["Insert Card"]
    B1 --> B2["Read Card Details"]
    B2 --> C1["Enter PIN"]
    C1 --> D1{"Valid PIN?"}
    D1 -- "[pin = stored]" --> E1["Display Menu"]
    D1 -- "[else] retry $\leq 3$" --> C1
    D1 -- "[else] retry = 3" --> F1["Retain Card & End"]
    F1 --> Z1(["End ⊙"])
    E1 --> G1["Select Withdraw"]
    G1 --> H1["Enter Amount"]
    H1 --> I1{"Balance $\geq$ Amount?"}
    I1 -- "[yes]" --> J1["Deduct Amount"]
    I1 -- "[no]" --> K1{"Retry?"}
    J1 --> L1["Dispense Cash"]
    J1 --> M1["Print Receipt"]
    M1 --> N1["Eject Card"]
    L1 --> N1
    K1 -- "[yes]" --> H1
    K1 -- "[no]" --> N1
    N1 --> Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the diagram:**
> - The **filled black circle** (`(["Start ●"])`) is the initial node.
> - `D1` and `I1` are **decision nodes** (diamonds).
> - `K1` is a **merge-decision** (it re-enters the loop on "yes" or ends on "no").
> - `F1` and `Z1` are **activity final nodes** (bullseye ⊙).
> - Guards like `[yes]`, `[no]`, `[pin = stored]` are placed on the outgoing edges.

---

## 3.3 Worked Case Study 2: Online Shopping Order Processing (with Parallelism)

### Problem Statement
> A customer places an order. The system **simultaneously** checks inventory, validates the payment, and verifies the shipping address. If all three succeed, the order is confirmed; if any fails, the order is rejected and the customer is notified. The customer's email is updated in parallel with order dispatch.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify Scenario**
Use case: *Place Order*.

**Step 2: Enumerate Activities**

```
Customer : Place Order → Receive Confirmation
System   : Check Inventory ∥ Validate Payment ∥ Verify Address
System   : Confirm Order → Dispatch
System   : Send Email  (parallel to dispatch)
```

**Step 3: Identify Control Nodes**

| Node | Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| After "Place Order" | **Fork** | 3 tasks run in parallel. |
| After 3 parallel tasks | **Join** | Must wait for all 3. |
| After join | **Decision** | All-pass / some-fail |
| "Send Email" branch | **Fork from dispatch** | Email sent in parallel with dispatch. |

**Step 4: Draw the Activity Diagram**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2(["Start ●"]) --> B2["Place Order"]
    B2 --> C2["Receive Order Details"]
    C2 --> D2{{"Fork ▬"}}
    D2 --> E2["Check Inventory"]
    D2 --> F2["Validate Payment"]
    D2 --> G2["Verify Shipping Address"]
    E2 --> H2{{"Join ▬"}}
    F2 --> H2
    G2 --> H2
    H2 --> I2{"All Checks Passed?"}
    I2 -- "[yes]" --> J2["Confirm Order"]
    I2 -- "[no]" --> K2["Reject Order"]
    J2 --> L2["Notify Customer"]
    K2 --> M2["Notify Customer with Reason"]
    L2 --> N2{{"Fork ▬"}}
    M2 --> O2(["End ⊙"])
    N2 --> P2["Dispatch Order"]
    N2 --> Q2["Send Confirmation Email"]
    P2 --> R2{{"Join ▬"}}
    Q2 --> R2
    R2 --> O2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysis of the diagram:**
> - `D2` is a **fork node** splitting into 3 parallel activities (`E2`, `F2`, `G2`).
> - `H2` is the **join node** synchronizing all 3.
> - `N2` is a **second fork** dispatching order and sending email in parallel.
> - The latency from `N2` to the end is $\max(T_{dispatch}, T_{email})$.

**Latency calculation:**

$$T_{end} = T_{place} + T_{details} + \max(T_{inv}, T_{pay}, T_{addr}) + T_{confirm} + T_{notify} + \max(T_{dispatch}, T_{email})$$

---

## 3.4 Common Drawing Pitfalls (KTU Valuation Errors)

| S.No | Mistake | Correct Way |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Multiple initial nodes | Exactly **one** initial node. |
| 2 | Decision node with no guards | Every outgoing edge **must** have `[guard]`. |
| 3 | Using `→` arrows from diamond without labels | Label each edge with `[condition]`. |
| 4 | Drawing fork/join as a regular rectangle | Use a **thick black bar**. |
| 5 | Loops without exit | Provide a clear path to a final node. |
| 6 | Activity shape: a regular rectangle | Activity is **rounded rectangle**; object node is **plain rectangle**. |

---

## 3.5 Algorithmic Sketch (Pseudocode for the Process)

```
ALGORITHM DrawActivityDiagram(problemStatement):
    1. PARSE problemStatement
    2. activities  = EXTRACT_ACTIONS(problemStatement)   // verb-noun pairs
    3. decisions   = EXTRACT_CONDITIONS(problemStatement) // "if", "whether"
    4. parallels   = EXTRACT_PARALLELISM(problemStatement) // "simultaneously"
    5. lanes       = EXTRACT_RESPONSIBILITIES(problemStatement) // "by", "system"
    6. PLOT initialNode
    7. FOR each activity IN activities IN ORDER:
            PLOT activity AS rounded_rectangle
            CONNECT with arrow FROM previous
    8. FOR each decision IN decisions:
            INSERT diamond_node
            ADD guarded branches
    9. FOR each parallel IN parallels:
            INSERT fork_bar
            INSERT join_bar
            CONNECT parallel_activities
   10. IF lanes NON-EMPTY:
            DRAW swimlane_partitions
            ASSIGN activities TO lanes
   11. PLOT finalNode
   12. RETURN activityDiagram
```

This pseudocode is the **cognitive recipe** the examiner expects you to apply in the viva.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Activity Diagram (Library Book Issue System)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A library member issues a book. The librarian checks membership validity (in parallel with stock availability). If both pass, the book is issued. Otherwise, the system rejects the request.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1(["Start ●"]) --> L1["Member Requests Book"]
    L1 --> L2["Librarian Receives Request"]
    L2 --> FK1{{"Fork ▬"}}
    FK1 --> L3["Verify Membership"]
    FK1 --> L4["Check Book Availability"]
    L3 --> JN1{{"Join ▬"}}
    L4 --> JN1
    JN1 --> D1{"Valid and Available?"}
    D1 -- "[yes]" --> L5["Issue Book"]
    D1 -- "[no]" --> L6["Reject Request"]
    L5 --> L7["Update Records"]
    L6 --> L8["Notify Member"]
    L7 --> L9["Generate Receipt"]
    L9 --> E1(["End ⊙"])
    L8 --> E1
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Activity Diagram Construction Pipeline)

This is the meta-diagram showing **how you, the student, construct** an activity diagram from a problem statement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Problem Statement"] --> P2["Textual Analysis"]
    P2 --> P3["Identify Actors & Lanes"]
    P2 --> P4["Extract Activities"]
    P2 --> P5["Detect Decisions"]
    P2 --> P6["Detect Parallelism"]
    P3 --> P7["Layout Lanes"]
    P4 --> P8["Place Activity Nodes"]
    P5 --> P9["Insert Decision Diamonds"]
    P6 --> P10["Insert Fork/Join Bars"]
    P7 --> P11["Compose Final Diagram"]
    P8 --> P11
    P9 --> P11
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12["Validate Notation"]
    P12 --> P13["Final Activity Diagram"]
```

---

## 4.3 Swimlane Architecture (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LANE_CUST["Customer Lane"]
        C1["Browse Catalog"] --> C2["Add to Cart"] --> C3["Checkout"]
    end
    subgraph LANE_SYS["System Lane"]
        S1["Validate Cart"] --> S2["Calculate Total"]
        S2 --> S3["Apply Discount"]
    end
    subgraph LANE_PAY["Payment Gateway Lane"]
        P1["Process Payment"] --> P2{"Payment OK?"}
        P2 -- "[yes]" --> P3["Confirm Payment"]
        P2 -- "[no]" --> P4["Payment Failed"]
    end
    C3 --> S1
    S3 --> P1
    P3 --> Q1["Generate Invoice"]
    P4 --> Q2["Show Error"]
    Q1 --> END1(["End ⊙"])
    Q2 --> END1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this swimlane diagram:** Each subgraph represents one swimlane. Activities inside a lane are owned by that lane's role. Cross-lane arrows represent **inter-role handoffs** (e.g., from Customer to System at "Checkout").

---

## 4.4 Decision-Tree for Control-Node Selection (Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What is the control pattern?"] --> Q2{"Is it conditional branching?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"Is there a return to a common point?"}
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q4{"Do activities run in parallel?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> A1["Use Decision + Merge"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> A2["Use Decision only"]
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> A3["Use Fork + Join"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> A4["Use Activity (sequential)"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Marks are **deducted** for using a rectangle instead of a **rounded rectangle** for activities.
> 2. Marks are **deducted** for missing **guard conditions** (`[yes]` / `[no]`) on decision branches.
> 3. Marks are **deducted** for using **multiple initial nodes** — only ONE is allowed.
> 4. Marks are **deducted** if a **fork** is drawn without a corresponding **join**.
> 5. Marks are **deducted** if the diagram is **not labeled with the use-case name** at the top.
> 6. Always use the **standard UML 2.5 shapes** — no artistic deviations.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an activity diagram. List any four notations used in an activity diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An activity diagram is a **UML behavioral diagram** that depicts the **dynamic flow** of activities in a system, including sequential, conditional, and parallel behaviors. *(1 mark)*

> Four notations: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
> 1. **Initial Node** — filled black circle (`●`).
> 2. **Activity Final Node** — bullseye (`⊙`).
> 3. **Decision Node** — diamond (`◇`).
> 4. **Fork/Join Node** — thick black bar (`▬`).

*(Full credit if any four correct notations are given with shapes.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between an activity diagram and a flowchart.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Activity Diagram | Flowchart | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Models business/system processes | Models program logic | 1 |
| **Parallelism** | Supports fork/join | Not natively supported | 1 |
| **Swimlanes** | Supported | Not supported | 0.5 |
| **Object Flow** | Supported via object nodes | Not supported | 0.5 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**A) (a)** Draw the activity diagram for the **Online Railway Reservation System** with the following requirements: *(7 marks)*

1. User logs in.
2. Searches for trains between two stations.
3. Selects a train.
4. System checks seat availability in parallel with payment gateway readiness.
5. If both succeed, ticket is confirmed; otherwise, the request is rejected.

**Model Solution:**

**Activities identified:**
- `Login` → `Search Trains` → `Select Train` → (Fork) → `Check Seat Availability` ∥ `Verify Payment Gateway` → (Join) → Decision → `Confirm Ticket` or `Reject Request` → `Notify User` → End.

**Activity Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RA(["Start ●"]) --> RB["User Logs In"]
    RB --> RC["Search Trains"]
    RC --> RD["Select Train"]
    RD --> RE{{"Fork ▬"}}
    RE --> RF["Check Seat Availability"]
    RE --> RG["Verify Payment Gateway"]
    RF --> RH{{"Join ▬"}}
    RG --> RH
    RH --> RI{"Both OK?"}
    RI -- "[yes]" --> RJ["Confirm Ticket"]
    RI -- "[no]" --> RK["Reject Request"]
    RJ --> RL["Deduct Fare & Notify"]
    RK --> RM["Notify User with Reason"]
    RL --> RN(["End ⊙"])
    RM --> RN
```

**Mark distribution for sub-part (a):**
- Correct identification of activities: **2 marks**.
- Fork and join correctly drawn: **2 marks**.
- Decision node with guards: **1.5 marks**.
- Proper start/end nodes: **1 mark**.
- Clean labeling: **0.5 marks**.

---

**(b)** Explain any **four** notations used in the above activity diagram with their UML shapes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Notation | UML Shape | Purpose in above diagram | Marks |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Initial Node** | Filled black circle `●` | Marks the start of the reservation flow at `Start ●`. | 1.5 |
| 2 | **Activity** | Rounded rectangle | Represents `User Logs In`, `Search Trains`, `Confirm Ticket`, etc. | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Decision Node** | Diamond `◇` | Used at `Both OK?` to branch into confirm or reject. | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Fork Node** | Thick black bar `▬` | Splits flow into `Check Seat Availability` and `Verify Payment Gateway` running in parallel. | 1.5 |
| 5 | **Join Node** | Thick black bar `▬` | Synchronizes both parallel paths before the decision. | 1.0 |
| | | | **Total** | **7.0** |

*(Any four well-explained notations with shapes — full marks.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — 14 Marks

**B) (a)** Draw the activity diagram for a **Hospital Patient Admission System** with the following requirements: *(7 marks)*

1. Patient arrives and registers.
2. Nurse takes initial vitals.
3. Doctor examines the patient.
4. **In parallel**, lab tests are prescribed and medicine is dispensed from pharmacy (preliminary).
5. Based on test results, the doctor decides to admit or discharge.
6. If admitted, a bed is allotted; if discharged, a bill is generated.

**Model Solution:**

**Activities identified (in order):**
- `Patient Arrives` → `Register` → `Nurse Takes Vitals` → `Doctor Examines` → (Fork) → `Lab Tests` ∥ `Dispense Preliminary Medicine` → (Join) → Decision: `Admit or Discharge?` → `Admit: Allot Bed` or `Discharge: Generate Bill` → `Update Records` → End.

**Activity Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HA(["Start ●"]) --> HB["Patient Arrives"]
    HB --> HC["Register Patient"]
    HC --> HD["Nurse Records Vitals"]
    HD --> HE["Doctor Examines Patient"]
    HE --> HF{{"Fork ▬"}}
    HF --> HG["Conduct Lab Tests"]
    HF --> HH["Dispense Preliminary Medicine"]
    HG --> HI{{"Join ▬"}}
    HH --> HI
    HI --> HJ{"Admit or Discharge?"}
    HJ -- "[admit]" --> HK["Allot Bed"]
    HJ -- "[discharge]" --> HL["Generate Bill"]
    HK --> HM["Update Medical Records"]
    HL --> HM
    HM --> HN(["End ⊙"])
```

**Mark distribution for sub-part (a):**
- Activities identified correctly: **2 marks**.
- Fork/join drawn correctly: **2 marks**.
- Decision node with guards: **1.5 marks**.
- Proper initial and final nodes: **1 mark**.
- Clean, labeled diagram: **0.5 marks**.

---

**(b)** Justify why a **fork-join** pair is used in the above hospital diagram, and explain what would happen if the join node is **missing**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Why fork-join is used:** *(3.5 marks)*

The hospital scenario involves two activities — `Conduct Lab Tests` and `Dispense Preliminary Medicine` — that can be **executed concurrently** because they are **logically independent** (one is a diagnostic activity done in the pathology lab; the other is a therapeutic activity done in the pharmacy). A **fork node** (`HF`) splits the control flow into these two parallel branches. The **join node** (`HI`) ensures that the downstream decision `Admit or Discharge?` is reached **only after both branches complete**. This models the real-world constraint that the doctor should not decide on admission until **all** parallel preliminary activities are finished. The join node enforces **synchronization** and **deterministic behavior** of the system.

**What happens if the join is missing:** *(3.5 marks)*

If the join node `HI` is removed, the two parallel branches would flow independently into the decision node `HJ` (or each into a separate decision). This causes:
1. **Race condition:** The decision `Admit or Discharge?` may be triggered twice — once after lab tests finish and again after pharmacy dispenses medicine. This leads to **duplicate processing** and inconsistent state.
2. **Non-deterministic behavior:** The doctor might make the admit/discharge decision based on **incomplete information** (e.g., lab tests not yet reported). This violates the real-world safety constraint.
3. **Violation of synchronization semantics:** In UML, parallel flows without a join exhibit **unbounded concurrency**; the diagram becomes **ill-defined** because the system has no way of knowing when all parallel work is done.
4. **Potential data inconsistency:** If the medical record is updated concurrently by both branches, there is a **write-conflict** that requires transactional resolution.

Thus, the **fork-join pair** is essential to model **safe concurrent execution** with synchronization.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (memorize for viva and exams):**

- **Definition:** Activity diagram = **UML behavioral diagram** that models the **flow of activities** (work, control, objects) in a process.
- **Initial node** = filled black circle (●), **exactly one**.
- **Activity final node** = bullseye (⊙), ends **all** flows.
- **Flow final node** = circle with X (⊗), ends **one** branch only.
- **Activity** = **rounded rectangle** (NOT plain rectangle).
- **Object node** = **plain rectangle** with `name : Class`.
- **Decision** = **diamond** with **1 incoming, ≥ 2 outgoing** edges.
- **Merge** = **diamond** with **≥ 2 incoming, 1 outgoing** edge.
- **Fork** = **thick black bar**, splits flow into parallel branches.
- **Join** = **thick black bar**, synchronizes parallel branches.
- **Swimlane** = a partition assigning responsibility to a role/actor.
- **Guard condition** must be in **square brackets** `[ ]` on every decision outgoing edge.
- **Fork must have a corresponding join** for safe synchronization.
- **Activity diagrams** can model **business processes, use-case flows, and parallel algorithms**.
- **Difference from flowchart:** supports parallelism, swimlanes, and object flow.
- **Difference from sequence diagram:** shows **flow of control**; sequence diagram shows **time-ordered message exchange**.
- **Difference from state-chart:** state-chart shows states of **one object**; activity diagram shows **process flow across many objects**.
- **Latency of parallel branches:** $T_{join} = \max(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_k)$ — the **critical-path** rule.
- **Guard completeness:** $\bigvee_{i=1}^{n} g_i = \text{True}$ for a complete decision node.
- **Always label the diagram** with the use-case name and the system name.
- **Use top-to-bottom layout** for readability (KTU preference).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identify the classes. Classify them as weak and strong classes and draw the class diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Identify the classes. Classify them as weak and strong classes and draw the class diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Class
In the context of **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme CASE Lab syllabus, a **Class** is a formal blueprint (or template) that defines the **attributes (data members)**, **operations (methods)**, and **relationships** shared by a group of objects exhibiting identical behaviour, identity, and structure. It is the fundamental building block of any UML Class Diagram.

In UML 2.5 (the **Object Management Group (OMG)** standard notation used in CASE tools such as **Rational Rose, StarUML, ArgoUML, and Visual Paradigm**), a class is represented as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**:
1. **Class Name** (mandatory, top compartment)
2. **Attributes** (middle compartment)
3. **Operations / Methods** (bottom compartment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A class is *not* an object. A class is a *plan*; an object is a *physical instance* of that plan living in memory. The class diagram depicts the *static* blueprint, while the object diagram depicts the *runtime snapshot*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Intuition
Imagine you are an **architect** designing a **residential building**:
- The **blueprint** (the paper drawing on your table) is the **Class**. It defines *how many rooms, doors, windows* the building will have.
- The **actual building** constructed from that blueprint is the **Object (Instance)**.
- The blueprint does **not occupy physical space**, but the building does. Similarly, a class does **not occupy memory**; objects do.
- You can construct **many buildings** (objects) from **one blueprint** (class).

So, when you "identify the classes" in a problem statement, you are essentially **reverse-engineering the architect's drawings** from a description of an existing system.

### 1.3 Strong Class vs. Weak Class — The Intuitive Distinction
Consider the phrase: *"The system manages **Customers**, **Orders**, **Items**, **Payments**, **Reports**, **Stock**, and **Invoices**."*

Now reflect: which of these are *genuine, independent, real-world entities* (Strong), and which are *derivative, vague, or merely descriptive labels* (Weak)?

| **Strong Class** (Independent Domain Entity) | **Weak Class** (Derivative / Ambiguous) |
|---|---|
| Has a **clear identity** (primary key) | Cannot exist meaningfully **without** another class |
| Has a **well-defined life cycle** (born → lives → dies) | Often a **descriptive label** (adjective-derived) |
| Has its **own attributes and operations** | Replicates information already present elsewhere |
| Represents a **noun** that the business truly cares about | Represents an **event, role, or transaction detail** that is a *by-product* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Expectation):** If a candidate class **cannot stand on its own** in a real-world scenario and only makes sense *in the context of* another class, it is most likely a **Weak Class**. If it has a **clear, independent identity**, it is a **Strong Class**.

### 1.4 The Class Diagram — Visual Blueprint
A **UML Class Diagram** is a **static structural diagram** that visualises:
- The classes of the system
- The **attributes** and **operations** of each class
- The **relationships** between classes (association, aggregation, composition, inheritance, dependency)
- The **multiplicity / cardinality** at each end of a relationship

It is the **most widely used** UML diagram in industry and is the **primary deliverable** of KTU CASE Lab Experiment 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Systematic Class Identification Process
Class identification is the **first analytical step** of the Booch/Rumbaugh/Jacobson (BRJ) OOAD methodology. The standard technique taught in KTU labs is the **Noun Phrase Identification (NPI)** method refined by **Abbot's Textual Analysis**.

**Step 1 — Read the Problem Statement (Requirement Specification).**  
Read the SRS / problem narrative **twice**. The first pass is for *comprehension*; the second is for *highlighting nouns*.

**Step 2 — Highlight All Nouns and Noun Phrases.**  
Every noun is a potential class candidate. Common candidates include: *Customer, Order, Product, Bill, Inventory, Employee, Account, Transaction, Department, Address*.

**Step 3 — Eliminate Non-Class Nouns (Weak Classes).**  
Apply the following elimination rules:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Filter Rules (Memorise These):**
> 1. **Redundant Classes** → If two nouns refer to the *same entity* (e.g., *Buyer* and *Customer*), keep only one.
> 2. **Adjective Nouns** → Words like *OldCustomer*, *PremiumUser*, *FastCar* are usually **roles or states**, not classes.
> 3. **Vague / Meta Classes** → Words like *System*, *Process*, *Information*, *Data*, *Details* are too generic. Refine them.
> 4. **Event / Action Nouns** → Words ending in *-tion*, *-ment* representing a happening (e.g., *Registration*, *Login*, *Cancellation*) are often **operations** on another class, not classes themselves.
> 5. **Attribute-as-Class** → A noun representing a *property* of another class (e.g., *PhoneNumber*, *Address*, *DateOfBirth*) is usually a **data type or attribute**, not a class.
> 6. **Outside-the-System Classes** → External entities like *Bank*, *PaymentGateway*, *Government* are **actors**, not internal domain classes.
> 7. **Implementation Constructs** → *File*, *Array*, *Database*, *Window*, *Menu* are technical implementation details, not domain classes (unless the system itself *is* a database manager).

**Step 4 — Validate Remaining Candidates (Strong Classes).**  
A candidate becomes a **Strong Class** only if it satisfies **all three** of the **Coad–Yourdon Rules**:
- **Retained Information:** The candidate's instances must be remembered (persisted) by the system.
- **Required Services:** The candidate must have a set of *meaningful operations* other classes need.
- **Multiple Attributes:** The candidate should have **more than one attribute** in common.

**Step 5 — Tabulate and Justify.**  
Prepare a table with columns: *Candidate | Retained? | Services? | Multiple Attributes? | Verdict (Strong / Weak / Eliminated)*.

### 2.2 High-Yield Reference Sheet — UML Class Diagram Notation

> [!NOTE]
> The following symbols and rules are the **official UML 2.5 notation** expected by KTU examiners. Memorise this entire table.

| **Symbol / Element** | **Notation in Diagram** | **Meaning** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class** | Rectangle with 3 compartments | Blueprint of an object | `Customer` |
| **Visibility `+`** | `+ name : Type` | **Public** (accessible everywhere) | `+ name : String` |
| **Visibility `-`** | `- name : Type` | **Private** (within class only) | `- balance : double` |
| **Visibility `#`** | `# name : Type` | **Protected** (subclasses too) | `# rate : float` |
| **Visibility `~`** | `~ name : Type` | **Package** (default in Java) | `~ id : int` |
| **Underlined attribute** | `+ id : int` underlined | Static / Class-level member | `+ MAX_LIMIT : int` |
| **Italic class name** | `*AbstractClass*` | Abstract class (cannot be instantiated) | `*Shape*` |
| **`«interface»` stereotype** | Rectangle with `«interface»` tag | Pure abstract contract | `«interface» Payable` |
| **Solid line with arrow (hollow)** | `──────▷` | **Generalisation (Inheritance)** | `Vehicle ──▷ Car` |
| **Solid line with arrow (filled)** | `- - - - ▷` | **Realisation (Interface impl.)** | `Payable - - - ▷ CreditCard` |
| **Plain solid line** | `──────` | **Association** (general link) | `Customer ──── Order` |
| **Solid line + filled diamond** | `◆──────` | **Composition** (strong, lifecycle-dependent) | `Order ◆──── Item` |
| **Solid line + hollow diamond** | `◇──────` | **Aggregation** (weak, shared ownership) | `Department ◇──── Employee` |
| **Dashed arrow** | `- - ▷` | **Dependency** (uses, transient) | `Controller - - ▷ Report` |
| **Multiplicity `1`** | At the end of line | Exactly one | `1` |
| **Multiplicity `*` or `0..*`** | At the end of line | Zero or many | `*` |
| **Multiplicity `1..*`** | At the end of line | At least one | `1..*` |
| **Role names** | At the end of line | Semantic role of the class | `employer / employee` |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
Class diagrams are the **lingua franca** of software engineering teams. They are used for:
- **Reverse engineering** legacy systems into modern OOP code.
- **Forward engineering** — CASE tools like **Rational Rose** and **StarUML** can auto-generate C++/Java/Python skeleton code directly from a class diagram.
- **Database schema generation** — Classes map directly to **tables**, attributes to **columns**, and associations to **foreign keys** (Object-Relational Mapping paradigm).
- **Requirements traceability** — Each class must be traceable back to a line in the SRS document.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example — Hospital Management System (HMS)

This section demonstrates the **complete end-to-end procedure** expected by the KTU examiner. Use the same workflow in your lab record.

### 3.1 Problem Statement
> *"The **CityCare Hospital** requires a software system to manage its day-to-day operations. The system should register **patients** with their personal and medical history, assign them to **doctors** based on **departments**, schedule **appointments**, generate **bills** after consultation, and maintain a **pharmacy** that dispenses **medicines**. Each patient can be admitted to a **ward** (General, ICU, or Private) and is attended by a **nurse** in shifts. Doctors write **prescriptions** that contain multiple **medicines**. The system should also handle **staff payroll** and generate **reports** for the management."*

### 3.2 Step 1 — Extract All Noun Phrases
Highlight every noun in the statement:

```
Hospital, system, patients, medical history, doctors, departments,
appointments, bills, consultation, pharmacy, medicines, ward, ICU,
nurse, shifts, prescriptions, staff, payroll, reports, management
```

### 3.3 Step 2 — Apply the Coad–Yourdon Filter

| **#** | **Candidate Noun** | **Retained Info?** | **Required Services?** | **Multiple Attributes?** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hospital | Yes (name, address, contact) | Yes (manage) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 2 | System | – | – | – | ❌ Weak (meta / outside scope) |
| 3 | Patient | Yes (ID, name, age, history) | Yes (register, admit) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 4 | MedicalHistory | Yes (diagnosis, allergies) | Yes (add, retrieve) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 5 | Doctor | Yes (ID, name, spec, fee) | Yes (consult, prescribe) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 6 | Department | Yes (name, head, location) | Yes (assign doctor) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 7 | Appointment | Yes (date, time, status) | Yes (schedule, cancel) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 8 | Bill | Yes (number, amount, date) | Yes (generate, pay) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 9 | Consultation | Yes (notes, diagnosis) | Yes (record) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 10 | Pharmacy | Yes (name, location) | Yes (dispense) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 11 | Medicine | Yes (name, price, stock) | Yes (update stock) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 12 | Ward | Yes (type, capacity) | Yes (allocate) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 13 | ICU | – | – | – | ❌ Weak (subtype of Ward) |
| 14 | Nurse | Yes (ID, name, shift) | Yes (attend patient) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 15 | Shift | Yes (time, type) | Limited (part of Nurse) | Few | ⚠️ **Weak** (attribute of Nurse or Staff) |
| 16 | Prescription | Yes (ID, date, dosage) | Yes (issue, fetch) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** |
| 17 | Staff | Yes (ID, salary, role) | Yes (manage payroll) | Yes | ✅ **Strong** (superclass) |
| 18 | Payroll | – | – | – | ❌ Weak (process / report) |
| 19 | Report | – | – | – | ❌ Weak (output artefact) |
| 20 | Management | – | – | – | ❌ Weak (external entity / actor) |

### 3.4 Step 3 — Final Strong Class Roster
After elimination, the **Strong Classes** are:

$$\text{StrongClasses} = \{ \text{Hospital, Patient, MedicalHistory, Doctor, Department, Appointment,} \}$$
$$\text{Bill, Consultation, Pharmacy, Medicine, Ward, Nurse, Prescription, Staff} \}$$

The **Weak Classes** (for documentation, often as attributes or in a separate "refined" diagram) are:

$$\text{WeakClasses} = \{ \text{System, ICU, Shift, Payroll, Report, Management} \}$$

### 3.5 Step 4 — Identify Attributes, Operations, and Relationships

**Sample attribute & operation extraction (Doctor class):**

$$\text{Doctor} = \begin{Bmatrix} \text{+ doctorID : int} \\ \text{+ name : String} \\ \text{+ specialization : String} \\ \text{+ consultationFee : double} \\ \text{+ phone : String} \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\text{Doctor.Operations} = \begin{Bmatrix} \text{+ consult(patient) : void} \\ \text{+ writePrescription() : Prescription} \\ \text{+ viewSchedule() : Appointment[]} \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Relationship identification (key associations):**

| **Source Class** | **Relationship** | **Target Class** | **Multiplicity** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hospital | composition | Department | `1` — `1..*` | A hospital *owns* its departments |
| Department | aggregation | Doctor | `1` — `1..*` | A department *has* doctors (shared) |
| Doctor | association | Appointment | `1` — `0..*` | A doctor has many appointments |
| Patient | association | Appointment | `1` — `0..*` | A patient books many appointments |
| Appointment | composition | Consultation | `1` — `1` | A consultation cannot exist without an appointment |
| Consultation | composition | Prescription | `1` — `1` | A prescription is generated by one consultation |
| Prescription | aggregation | Medicine | `1` — `1..*` | A prescription lists many medicines |
| Patient | aggregation | MedicalHistory | `1` — `1..*` | History belongs to the patient |
| Ward | composition | Patient | `1` — `0..*` | A ward accommodates patients |
| Nurse | association | Ward | `1..*` — `1` | Nurses are assigned to wards |
| Staff | inheritance | Doctor, Nurse | super → sub | Common superclass |
| Patient | association | Bill | `1` — `0..*` | Patient pays bills |
| Hospital | composition | Pharmacy | `1` — `1` | Hospital has its own pharmacy |
| Pharmacy | aggregation | Medicine | `1` — `0..*` | Pharmacy stocks medicines |

### 3.6 Step 5 — The Multiplicity & Association Class Logic
For a binary association `A ──── B` with role names `a` and `b`:

$$\text{Multiplicity}_{\text{A-end}} = \text{Number of } A \text{ instances linked to } \text{each } B \text{ instance}$$
$$\text{Multiplicity}_{\text{B-end}} = \text{Number of } B \text{ instances linked to } \text{each } A \text{ instance}$$

**Example:** `Patient ──── Bill`
- One patient can have **many bills** (over multiple visits) → multiplicity at Bill-end = `0..*`.
- One bill belongs to **exactly one patient** → multiplicity at Patient-end = `1`.

### 3.7 Step 6 — Inheritance Hierarchy
Apply **Generalisation** to remove redundancy:

```
            ┌────────────┐
            │   Staff    │  (Abstract Superclass)
            │ +staffID   │
            │ +salary    │
            └─────┬──────┘
                  │ (inheritance)
        ┌─────────┴─────────┐
        │                   │
   ┌────▼────┐        ┌─────▼────┐
   │ Doctor  │        │  Nurse   │
   └─────────┘        └──────────┘
```

Common attributes (`staffID`, `name`, `salary`) are pushed **up** to `Staff`; specialised attributes (`consultationFee` for Doctor, `shift` for Nurse) remain in the subclasses — this is the **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** principle of OOP.

## 4. Structural Diagram — Complete Class Diagram (Mermaid)

Below is the **complete, examiner-grade class diagram** for the Hospital Management System, drawn using the CASE-tool-friendly Mermaid `classDiagram` syntax.

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid `classDiagram` uses the same UML 2.5 notation as Rational Rose. The renderer is browser-compatible and embeds directly in HTML lab records.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Hospital {
        +hospitalID : int
        +name : String
        +address : String
        +phone : String
        +addDepartment() void
        +generateReport() void
    }

    class Department {
        +deptID : int
        +deptName : String
        +location : String
        +assignDoctor() void
    }

    class Staff {
        <<abstract>>
        +staffID : int
        +name : String
        +salary : double
        +phone : String
    }

    class Doctor {
        +specialization : String
        +consultationFee : double
        +consult() void
        +writePrescription() Prescription
    }

    class Nurse {
        +shift : String
        +wardAssigned : String
        +attendPatient() void
    }

    class Patient {
        +patientID : int
        +name : String
        +age : int
        +gender : String
        +bloodGroup : String
        +register() void
        +bookAppointment() void
    }

    class MedicalHistory {
        +recordID : int
        +diagnosis : String
        +allergies : String
        +date : Date
    }

    class Appointment {
        +appointmentID : int
        +date : Date
        +time : Time
        +status : String
        +schedule() void
        +cancel() void
    }

    class Consultation {
        +consultationID : int
        +notes : String
        +diagnosis : String
        +record() void
    }

    class Prescription {
        +prescriptionID : int
        +date : Date
        +dosage : String
        +duration : String
    }

    class Medicine {
        +medicineID : int
        +name : String
        +price : double
        +stock : int
        +updateStock() void
    }

    class Ward {
        +wardID : int
        +wardType : String
        +capacity : int
        +allocate() void
    }

    class Bill {
        +billNo : int
        +amount : double
        +date : Date
        +status : String
        +generate() void
        +pay() void
    }

    class Pharmacy {
        +pharmacyID : int
        +name : String
        +location : String
        +dispense() void
    }

    Hospital "1" *-- "1..*" Department : contains
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Doctor : employs
    Staff <|-- Doctor : inherits
    Staff <|-- Nurse : inherits
    Nurse "1..*" -- "1" Ward : assigned to
    Ward "1" o-- "0..*" Patient : accommodates
    Patient "1" *-- "1..*" MedicalHistory : has
    Patient "1" -- "0..*" Appointment : books
    Doctor "1" -- "0..*" Appointment : attends
    Appointment "1" *-- "1" Consultation : results in
    Consultation "1" *-- "1" Prescription : generates
    Prescription "1" o-- "1..*" Medicine : lists
    Patient "1" -- "0..*" Bill : receives
    Hospital "1" *-- "1" Pharmacy : owns
    Pharmacy "1" o-- "0..*" Medicine : stocks
```

### 4.1 Reading the Diagram
- `*--` denotes **composition** (filled diamond — strong ownership, lifecycle dependent).
- `o--` denotes **aggregation** (hollow diamond — weak ownership, shareable).
- `-->` (used as `--` in Mermaid with arrow) denotes plain **association**.
- `<|--` denotes **generalisation / inheritance** (hollow triangle).
- Multiplicities such as `1`, `1..*`, `0..*` are placed at the **end near the class** they constrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When drawing in Rational Rose, you must right-click → *Insert Attribute* / *Insert Operation* for each member. Do **not** type the full class syntax in a single field — it will be rejected.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**  
*With a suitable example, explain the difference between a **strong class** and a **weak class**. How do you decide which is which?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **strong class** represents a *genuine domain entity* that has its **own identity**, **life cycle**, and **multiple meaningful attributes and operations**. Example: `Customer` in a Banking System — it has a `customerID`, `name`, `address`, and operations like `openAccount()`, `closeAccount()`.

A **weak class** represents a *derivative, vague, or dependent entity* that cannot meaningfully exist on its own. Example: `Address` — it is merely a *property* of `Customer` and has no independent life cycle.

**Decision Rule:** A candidate is a **strong class** if and only if it satisfies all three **Coad–Yourdon conditions** — *(i)* instances are retained by the system, *(ii)* it has required services, and *(iii)* it has multiple attributes. If any condition fails, the candidate is reclassified as a **weak class** or eliminated entirely.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**  
*List and briefly explain the **three compartments** of a UML class representation. Why is the **visibility modifier** important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A UML class is drawn as a **rectangle divided into three horizontal compartments**:
1. **Class Name Compartment** (Top): Contains the class name in **bold**, centred. Abstract class names are written in *italics*. Interfaces use the `«interface»` stereotype.
2. **Attribute Compartment** (Middle): Lists the data members with the format `visibility name : Type`. Example: `- balance : double`.
3. **Operation Compartment** (Bottom): Lists the methods with the format `visibility name(params) : ReturnType`. Example: `+ deposit(amount : double) : void`.

**Importance of visibility:** Visibility controls **encapsulation**, the cornerstone of OOP. It dictates *who* can access the attribute/operation. The four standard UML visibility modifiers are `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package). Without visibility, the principle of *information hiding* cannot be enforced, and the class diagram fails to communicate the encapsulation contract.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**  
*Consider the following problem statement for an **Online Shopping System**:*
> *"An e-commerce platform allows **customers** to register, browse **products** organised into **categories**, add them to a **shopping cart**, place **orders**, make **payments** via multiple modes (Credit Card, UPI, Net Banking), and track **shipment** status. The system also maintains **sellers** who list products, **admins** who manage the catalogue, and generates **invoices** for every successful transaction."*

**(a)** Identify all candidate classes from the problem statement and present them in a filtered, tabulated form. Classify each candidate as **strong class** or **weak class** with a one-line justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw a complete **UML class diagram** for the identified strong classes. Show all **attributes, operations, relationships, multiplicities, and inheritance** where applicable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| **#** | **Candidate Noun** | **Retained?** | **Services?** | **Multi-Attr?** | **Verdict** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Core domain entity with identity & lifecycle |
| 2 | Product | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Listed, searched, ordered — central entity |
| 3 | Category | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Organises products; has name, description |
| 4 | ShoppingCart | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Has items, total, lifecycle per session |
| 5 | Order | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Central transactional entity |
| 6 | Payment | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Tracks transactions, modes, status |
| 7 | CreditCard | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ⚠️ **Weak subtype** | Can be a subclass of PaymentMode |
| 8 | UPI | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ⚠️ **Weak subtype** | Can be a subclass of PaymentMode |
| 9 | NetBanking | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ⚠️ **Weak subtype** | Can be a subclass of PaymentMode |
| 10 | Shipment | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Tracks delivery status, address, courier |
| 11 | Seller | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Lists products, has profile |
| 12 | Admin | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Manages catalogue, users |
| 13 | Invoice | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✅ **Strong** | Generated per order, has tax, total |
| 14 | e-commerce platform | – | – | – | ❌ **Eliminated** | The system itself, not a class |
| 15 | Catalogue | – | – | – | ❌ **Eliminated** | Aggregate of products |
| 16 | Transaction | – | – | – | ⚠️ **Weak** | Synonym for Payment (redundant) |

**Final Strong Class Roster (Online Shopping System):**

$$\text{Strong} = \{ \text{Customer, Product, Category, ShoppingCart, Order, Payment, Shipment,} \}$$
$$\text{Seller, Admin, Invoice} \}$$

**Final Weak Class Roster:**

$$\text{Weak} = \{ \text{CreditCard, UPI, NetBanking (subtypes of PaymentMode), Transaction, Catalogue} \}$$

**Justification for weak classification:**
- `CreditCard`, `UPI`, `NetBanking` are *modes* of payment, not standalone entities → modelled as subclasses of a `PaymentMode` abstract class.
- `Transaction` is semantically identical to `Payment` → redundant, eliminated.
- `Catalogue` is a collection of `Product` instances → not a domain entity.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

The complete class diagram (reproduced in Mermaid for clarity) is as follows:

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        +customerID : int
        +name : String
        +email : String

    }

        <<abstract>>
        +modeName : String
    }

    Customer "1" -- "0..*" Order : places
    Customer "1" -- "1" ShoppingCart : owns

    Order "1" *-- "1..*" Product : includes

    PaymentMode <|-- NetBanking : is a
    Seller "1" -- "0..*" Product : lists
    Category "1" -- "0..*" Product : groups
    Admin "1" -- "0..*" Category : manages
```

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**  
*Consider a **Library Management System (LMS)** with the following requirements:*
> *"The system manages **books**, **members**, **staff (librarian + assistant)**, and **publishers**. Each book has a unique ISBN, title, author, category, and copies. A member can **issue** up to 3 books, **return** them, and pay **fines** for late returns. The librarian issues/returns books and **reserves** them. Each book belongs to a **publisher**, and each publisher has many books. The system also tracks **categories** (e.g., Fiction, Science) and generates **reports** for the head librarian."*

**(a)** Identify all candidate classes. Classify them as **strong or weak classes** with justifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the complete **UML class diagram** showing attributes, operations, relationships, and multiplicities. Highlight any **inheritance hierarchy** (e.g., `Staff` → `Librarian`, `Assistant`). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| **#** | **Candidate** | **Verdict** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Book | ✅ **Strong** | Core entity: ISBN, title, author, copies |
| 2 | Member | ✅ **Strong** | Identity, lifecycle, borrow/return history |
| 3 | Staff | ✅ **Strong (abstract)** | Common superclass for Librarian & Assistant |
| 4 | Librarian | ✅ **Strong (subclass)** | Subclass of Staff; specific role |
| 5 | Assistant | ✅ **Strong (subclass)** | Subclass of Staff; specific role |
| 6 | Publisher | ✅ **Strong** | Independent entity with name, address |
| 7 | Category | ✅ **Strong** | Groups books; multiple attributes |
| 8 | IssueRecord | ✅ **Strong** | Transactional: issue date, due date, return date |
| 9 | Fine | ✅ **Strong** | Amount, date, reason, status |
| 10 | Reservation | ✅ **Strong** | Member, book, date, status |
| 11 | ISBN | ❌ **Weak (attribute)** | Identifier of Book, not a class |
| 12 | Report | ❌ **Weak (output)** | Generated output, not a persistent entity |
| 13 | System | ❌ **Eliminated** | Meta term |
| 14 | Head librarian | ❌ **Weak (role)** | A role of Librarian |

**Final Strong Class List:**

$$\text{Strong} = \{ \text{Book, Member, Staff, Librarian, Assistant, Publisher,} \}$$
$$\text{Category, IssueRecord, Fine, Reservation} \}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
        +ISBN : String
        +title : String
        +author : String
        +copiesAvailable : int
        +totalCopies : int
        +addCopy() void
        +isAvailable() boolean
    }

    class Member {
        +memberID : int
        +name : String
        +phone : String
        +email : String
        +maxBooks : int = 3
        +issueBook() void
        +returnBook() void
    }

    class Staff {
        <<abstract>>
        +staffID : int
        +name : String
        +salary : double
    }

    class Librarian {
        +deskNo : int
        +issueBook() void
        +returnBook() void
        +reserveBook() void
    }

    class Assistant {
        +shift : String
        +manageShelf() void
    }

    class Publisher {
        +publisherID : int
        +name : String
        +address : String
        +phone : String
    }

    class Category {
        +categoryID : int
        +name : String
        +description : String
    }

    class IssueRecord {
        +issueID : int
        +issueDate : Date
        +dueDate : Date
        +returnDate : Date
    }

    class Fine {
        +fineID : int
        +amount : double
        +reason : String
        +status : String
        +pay() void
    }

    class Reservation {
        +reservationID : int
        +reservationDate : Date
        +status : String
    }

    Book "0..*" -- "1" Publisher : published by
    Book "0..*" -- "1" Category : belongs to
    Member "1" -- "0..*" IssueRecord : has
    Book "1" -- "0..*" IssueRecord : involved in
    Member "1" -- "0..*" Fine : owes
    Member "1" -- "0..*" Reservation : makes
    Book "1" -- "0..*" Reservation : reserved by
    Staff <|-- Librarian : inherits
    Staff <|-- Assistant : inherits
    Librarian "1" -- "0..*" IssueRecord : processes
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing visibility symbols** — Forgetting `+`, `-`, `#` on attributes/operations costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per class. Always include them.
> 2. **No multiplicities** — A class diagram without `1`, `0..*`, `1..*` is considered *incomplete*. Always annotate relationship ends.
> 3. **Confusing aggregation vs. composition** — Use **composition (`◆`)** when the child *cannot exist* without the parent (e.g., `Order` → `Payment`). Use **aggregation (`◇`)** when the child *can exist independently* (e.g., `Publisher` → `Book`).
> 4. **Putting `ISBN`, `Date`, `Address` as classes** — These are **attributes or built-in types**, not classes. Examiners specifically test this trap.
> 5. **Forgetting inheritance for `Staff` → `Librarian` / `Assistant`** — Inheritance is a high-weight concept worth **1.5 to 2 marks**. Always check if "is-a" exists.
> 6. **No justifications for strong/weak classification** — Writing *just* a class list without reasoning for weak/strong costs the **Coad–Yourdon justification marks** (≈ 2 marks in Part A).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise This High-Density Summary Before the Exam:**

- ✅ A **Class** is a **static blueprint**; an **Object** is a **runtime instance**.
- ✅ The **UML class notation** has **3 compartments**: **Name | Attributes | Operations**.
- ✅ **Visibility symbols** are `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package).
- ✅ **Strong class** = satisfies **all 3 Coad–Yourdon rules** (retained info, services, multiple attributes).
- ✅ **Weak class** = fails at least one rule, or is an **attribute masquerading as a class** (e.g., ISBN, Address).
- ✅ **Class identification** = **Noun Phrase Identification (NPI)** from the problem statement.
- ✅ **Eliminate** during filtering: redundancies, adjective nouns, vague meta terms, event nouns, attribute-nouns, external actors, implementation constructs.
- ✅ **Relationships** in UML: **Association** (`──`), **Aggregation** (`◇──`), **Composition** (`◆──`), **Generalisation** (`──▷`), **Dependency** (`- -▷`), **Realisation** (`- -▷` for interface).
- ✅ **Composition** ⇒ strong ownership, lifecycle-dependent, parts die with the whole.
- ✅ **Aggregation** ⇒ weak ownership, parts can outlive the whole.
- ✅ **Multiplicity** must be placed at *both* ends of every binary relationship.
- ✅ **Inheritance** removes redundancy and follows the **"is-a"** test.
- ✅ In CASE tools (Rational Rose, StarUML), use the **Class View** → right-click → *New Class* → populate the **three compartments** manually.
- ✅ Always prepare a **filter table** showing the Coad–Yourdon rule applied to each candidate — this is what secures full marks in the lab record.
- ✅ Always **distinguish strong vs. weak** explicitly in the lab answer — it is the central learning outcome of Experiment 2.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw the sequence diagram for any two scenarios for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw the sequence diagram for any two scenarios for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence Diagram (UML 2.5 Definition)**
> A **Sequence Diagram** is a behavioral *Interaction Diagram* in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) that depicts the **time-ordered exchange of messages** between a set of *lifelines* (objects, actors, or system components) participating in a use case or scenario. Time flows **top-to-bottom** along the vertical dimension, while the horizontal dimension enumerates the participating lifelines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme CASE LAB context, drawing a sequence diagram means modelling **two distinct use-case scenarios** of a given case study (e.g., ATM System, Online Shopping, Library Management) using the standard UML 2.5 notational syntax.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Lifeline** | A vertical dashed line representing the existence of an object/actor over time. |
| **Actor** | External entity (human or external system) initiating interactions. |
| **Activation Bar** | Thin rectangle on a lifeline denoting that an object is executing a process. |
| **Message** | Communication arrow passed from one lifeline to another. |
| **Combined Fragment** | Grouping of conditional/iterative message sequences (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** Students must label every message, indicate **synchronous vs asynchronous** arrows, and use *combined fragments* when modelling alternate flows (success/failure).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **movie script** of a scene at a coffee shop:

- The **characters** in the scene = *Objects* in your diagram.
- The **vertical column of dialogue** = the *lifeline* of each character.
- The **dialogue lines spoken from top to bottom** = the *messages exchanged* in chronological order.
- A character *holding a phone (busy doing something)* = an *activation bar*.

So a sequence diagram is essentially a **"screenplay of a software system"** — it tells the story of *who* does *what* to *whom* and *in what order*. This makes it invaluable for both *design* and *debugging* distributed software.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sequence diagrams are heavily used in:

- **Requirement Analysis** (validating use cases).
- **API Design** (visualising REST/SOAP call sequences).
- **Microservices Architecture** (modelling inter-service communication).
- **Embedded Systems** (modelling hardware-software handshake protocols).
- **Reverse Engineering** of legacy code (understanding control flow).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Sequence Diagram

A sequence diagram is composed of **five primary structural elements**. Each contributes to a faithful representation of object collaboration.

#### 2.1.1 Lifelines
A lifeline begins at object creation and ends at object destruction (denoted by an `X` marker). It uses the notation:
$$
\text{objectName : ClassName}
$$
The dashed vertical line is called the *lifeline axis*, representing the lifetime of the object.

#### 2.1.2 Activation Bars
Activation bars are **thin vertical rectangles** placed on a lifeline. They indicate the time during which an object is **actively executing a method** or waiting for a response.

#### 2.1.3 Messages
Messages are the heart of a sequence diagram. They represent communication between lifelines.

#### 2.1.4 Combined Fragments
Used to model control flow constructs such as alternatives, options, loops, and parallel executions.

#### 2.1.5 State Invariants and Constraints
Conditions written inside curly braces `{}` on a lifeline representing a precondition or postcondition.

### 2.2 KTU Notation Cheat Sheet (Message Types)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the **five arrow types** below — they are tested repeatedly in KTU Board examinations.

| Message Type | Arrow Notation | Semantics | Example Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Synchronous Call** | Solid line, filled arrowhead `\to` | Caller waits for response | `login(username, pwd)` |
| **Asynchronous Call** | Solid line, open arrowhead `\to>` | Caller continues execution | Event broadcasting |
| **Return Message** | Dashed line, open arrowhead `-->` | Reply to previous call | Return value/object |
| **Self Message** | U-shaped arrow on same lifeline | Object calls its own method | Recursion, validation |
| **Found/Lost Message** | Arrow entering from/end at diagram edge | Unknown source/destination | Inter-system calls |

### 2.3 KTU Combined Fragments Cheat Sheet

| Fragment | UML Keyword | Purpose | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Alternative** | `alt` | `if-else` branching | Success vs Failure paths |
| **Option** | `opt` | `if` only (no else) | Optional behaviour |
| **Loop** | `loop` | Repeated execution | Iterative processing |
| **Parallel** | `par` | Concurrent threads | Multi-threaded flows |
| **Critical Region** | `critical` | Atomic operations | Database transactions |
| **Negative** | `neg` | Invalid interactions | Error path tracing |

### 2.4 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Methodology)

The construction of a sequence diagram follows a **deterministic algorithm** that every CASE LAB student must follow:

1. **Identify the Scenario Boundary** — Read the use-case description and define the *start* and *end* of the interaction.
2. **Enumerate Lifelines** — List all actors, system components, and external systems participating in the scenario.
3. **Establish Time Order** — Arrange messages chronologically (top-to-bottom) following the use-case flow.
4. **Classify Each Message** — Tag each arrow as synchronous, asynchronous, return, or self-message.
5. **Mark Activations** — Place activation bars on lifelines that are actively processing.
6. **Add Combined Fragments** — Use `alt` for alternate flows (success/failure), `loop` for iterations, `opt` for optional paths.
7. **Validate Completeness** — Ensure every lifeline has a clear role and no orphan arrows exist.

### 2.5 Why This Matters in CASE LAB

A sequence diagram serves as a **contract between modules**. By drawing it *before* coding, teams achieve:

- **Reduced ambiguity** in distributed team communication.
- **Early defect detection** in inter-module message flows.
- **Documentation** that remains valid through refactoring (object behaviour, not implementation detail).
- **Test case generation** — each message arrow can be mapped to a unit test stub.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Construction Methodology & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: ATM Cash Withdrawal Scenario

#### 3.1.1 Scenario Definition

> **Use Case:** *Cash Withdrawal from an Automated Teller Machine (ATM)*
> **Precondition:** User has a valid debit card and an active bank account.
> **Postcondition:** Cash is dispensed, account is debited, and a receipt is printed.

#### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify Lifelines

Based on the use-case narrative, the participating lifelines are:

$$
\text{Lifelines} = \{\text{User}, \text{ATM}, \text{BankServer}, \text{CashDispenser}, \text{ReceiptPrinter}\}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Construct the Time-Ordered Message Matrix

The complete chronological interaction matrix for the ATM scenario is constructed below:

| Step | Source | Target | Message | Type | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | User | ATM | Insert Card | Synchronous | Card reader activated |
| 2 | ATM | User | Request PIN | Async | Display prompt |
| 3 | User | ATM | Enter PIN | Synchronous | Keypad input |
| 4 | ATM | BankServer | ValidateCredentials | Synchronous | Auth API call |
| 5 | BankServer | ATM | AuthResponse(success) | Return | Token returned |
| 6 | ATM | User | ShowMenu | Async | Display options |
| 7 | User | ATM | SelectWithdrawal | Synchronous | Choice event |
| 8 | User | ATM | EnterAmount | Synchronous | Numeric input |
| 9 | ATM | BankServer | CheckBalance(accountID) | Synchronous | Balance query |
| 10 | BankServer | ATM | BalanceResult | Return | Sufficient/Insufficient |
| 11 | ATM | CashDispenser | DispenseCash(amount) | Synchronous | Hardware call |
| 12 | ATM | ReceiptPrinter | PrintReceipt | Asynchronous | Background process |
| 13 | ATM | BankServer | DebitAccount | Synchronous | Transaction commit |
| 14 | BankServer | ATM | DebitConfirmation | Return | Ledger updated |
| 15 | ATM | User | EjectCard | Async | Hardware return |

#### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Introduce Combined Fragments

Since the balance check has two outcomes, we wrap the relevant interactions in an `alt` fragment:

$$
\text{alt}\begin{cases}
\text{Sufficient Balance} \rightarrow \text{Dispense + Print + Debit} \\ \text{Insufficient Balance} \rightarrow \text{DisplayError} + \text{Eject}
\end{cases}
$$

This conditional structure is critical because it represents the **control-flow branching** that pure arrows cannot express.

#### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Convert Matrix to Visual Diagram

Refer to the rendered sequence diagram in **Section 4.1** for the final visual representation built from this matrix.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Online Shopping Order Placement

#### 3.2.1 Scenario Definition

> **Use Case:** *Customer Places an Order in an E-commerce System*
> **Precondition:** Customer is logged in and has at least one item in the cart.
> **Postcondition:** Order is confirmed, payment is processed, and a confirmation email is sent.

#### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Identify Lifelines

$$
\text{Lifelines} = \{\text{Customer}, \text{Browser}, \text{WebApp}, \text{OrderService}, \text{PaymentGateway}, \text{EmailService}\}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Construct the Time-Ordered Message Matrix

| Step | Source | Target | Message | Type | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Customer | Browser | ClickCheckout | Async | UI event |
| 2 | Browser | WebApp | GET /checkout | Synchronous | REST call |
| 3 | WebApp | OrderService | createOrder(cartID) | Synchronous | RPC call |
| 4 | OrderService | OrderService | calculateTotal() | Self | Internal computation |
| 5 | OrderService | PaymentGateway | requestPayment(amount) | Synchronous | External API |
| 6 | PaymentGateway | OrderService | paymentResult | Return | Success/Failure |
| 7 | OrderService | OrderService | updateOrderStatus() | Self | DB update |
| 8 | OrderService | EmailService | sendConfirmation(email) | Asynchronous | Fire-and-forget |
| 9 | OrderService | WebApp | orderConfirmation | Return | JSON payload |
| 10 | WebApp | Browser | RenderConfirmation | Async | UI render |

#### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Add Combined Fragments

The payment step requires an `alt` fragment for success vs failure:

$$
\text{alt}\begin{cases}
\text{Payment Success} \rightarrow \text{Send Email} + \text{Show Confirmation} \\ \text{Payment Failure} \rightarrow \text{Show Error Page}
\end{cases}
$$

#### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Convert Matrix to Visual Diagram

Refer to the rendered sequence diagram in **Section 4.2** for the final visual representation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attribute | ATM Scenario | Online Shopping Scenario |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Lifelines** | 5 | 6 |
| **External System** | Bank Server | Payment Gateway, Email Service |
| **Critical Combined Fragment** | `alt` for balance | `alt` for payment result |
| **Asynchronous Messages** | Card ejection, receipt printing | Confirmation email, UI render |
| **Hardware Interaction** | Cash dispenser, receipt printer | None (pure software) |
| **Transaction Risk** | High (money involved) | Medium (payment gateway) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sequence Diagram — ATM Cash Withdrawal Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor U as Customer
    participant ATM as ATM Machine
    participant BANK as Bank Server
    participant CASH as Cash Dispenser
    participant RECPT as Receipt Printer

    U->>ATM: Insert Card
    activate ATM
    ATM-->>U: Request PIN
    U->>ATM: Enter PIN
    ATM->>BANK: ValidateCredentials(cardID, pin)
    activate BANK
    BANK-->>ATM: AuthResponse(SUCCESS)
    deactivate BANK
    ATM-->>U: Display Menu
    U->>ATM: Select Withdrawal
    U->>ATM: Enter Amount
    ATM->>BANK: CheckBalance(accountID)
    activate BANK
    alt Sufficient Balance
        BANK-->>ATM: BalanceOK
        deactivate BANK
        ATM->>CASH: DispenseCash(amount)
        activate CASH
        CASH-->>ATM: CashDispensed
        deactivate CASH
        ATM->>RECPT: PrintReceipt
        activate RECPT
        RECPT-->>ATM: ReceiptPrinted
        deactivate RECPT
        ATM->>BANK: DebitAccount(amount)
        activate BANK
        BANK-->>ATM: DebitConfirmed
        deactivate BANK
    else Insufficient Balance
        BANK-->>ATM: InsufficientFunds
        deactivate BANK
        ATM-->>U: ShowErrorMessage
    end
    ATM-->>U: Eject Card
    deactivate ATM
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Online Shopping Order Placement Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor C as Customer
    participant BR as Web Browser
    participant APP as Web Application
    participant ORD as Order Service
    participant PAY as Payment Gateway
    participant EML as Email Service
    participant DB as Order Database

    C->>BR: Login(credentials)
    BR->>APP: POST /login
    activate APP
    APP-->>BR: 200 OK (sessionToken)
    deactivate APP
    C->>BR: Click Checkout
    BR->>APP: GET /checkout
    activate APP
    APP->>ORD: createOrder(cartID, userID)
    activate ORD
    ORD->>DB: INSERT pending_order
    activate DB
    DB-->>ORD: orderID
    deactivate DB
    ORD->>ORD: calculateTotal(items)
    ORD->>PAY: requestPayment(orderID, amount)
    activate PAY
    alt Payment Successful
        PAY-->>ORD: paymentConfirmation
        deactivate PAY
        ORD->>DB: UPDATE order SET status=CONFIRMED
        activate DB
        DB-->>ORD: updateAck
        deactivate DB
        ORD->>EML: sendConfirmation(userEmail, orderID)
        activate EML
        EML-->>C: Order Confirmation Email
        deactivate EML
        ORD-->>APP: orderConfirmationPayload
    else Payment Failed
        PAY-->>ORD: paymentDeclined
        deactivate PAY
        ORD->>DB: UPDATE order SET status=FAILED
        activate DB
        DB-->>ORD: updateAck
        deactivate DB
        ORD-->>APP: paymentErrorPayload
    end
    deactivate ORD
    APP-->>BR: Render Response Page
    deactivate APP
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reference View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Use Case Specification] --> B[Identify Actors and Objects]
    B --> C[Map Time-Ordered Messages]
    C --> D{Alternate Paths Exist?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Add alt Combined Fragment]
    D -->|No| F[Linear Flow]
    E --> G[Validate with Stakeholder]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final Sequence Diagram]
    H --> I[Generate Test Cases]
    I --> J[Implementation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Question 1
**Define a UML Sequence Diagram. List any four key components used in its construction.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Sequence Diagram* is a UML behavioral diagram that visualises the **time-ordered interaction** between objects participating in a use case. Time progresses vertically downward, and lifelines are arranged horizontally.

**Four key components:**
1. **Lifeline** — vertical dashed line representing an object's existence.
2. **Activation Bar** — rectangle showing when an object is processing.
3. **Message** — arrow representing communication between objects.
4. **Combined Fragment** — rectangular block denoting control flow (`alt`, `loop`, `opt`).

*(Self- and asynchronous messages may also be credited.)*

---

#### Question 2
**Differentiate between a synchronous message and an asynchronous message in a sequence diagram. Draw the arrow notations used for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Synchronous Message | Asynchronous Message |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Arrow Notation** | Solid line with **filled** arrowhead | Solid line with **open** arrowhead |
| **Caller Behaviour** | Waits and blocks until response | Continues execution immediately |
| **Return Path** | Implicit/explicit return expected | No return expected |
| **Typical Use** | Function/method calls | Event broadcasting, fire-and-forget |
| **Example** | `login(username, pwd)` | `sendEmail(userEmail)` |

*Diagrammatic representation:*
- Sync: `A ───────▶ B`  (filled triangle)
- Async: `A ───────▷ B` (open V)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End-Semester Evaluation Pattern]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions include internal choice. The student must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full.*

---

#### ⭐ Choice A (14 Marks)

##### **Question A:**
**(a)** Consider the case study of an **Online Railway Reservation System**. Identify the lifelines, draw a detailed sequence diagram for the scenario *"Passenger Books a Ticket"*, and explain the use of the `alt` combined fragment. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain any **four combined fragments** used in UML sequence diagrams with their syntax and one practical example each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sequence Diagram for Passenger Books a Ticket:**

**Lifelines identified:**
- Passenger (actor)
- WebPortal
- BookingService
- PaymentGateway
- IRCTC_DB (Database)
- EmailService

**Time-Ordered Message Matrix:**

| Step | From | To | Message |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Passenger | WebPortal | SearchTrain(source, dest, date) |
| 2 | WebPortal | BookingService | queryTrains(criteria) |
| 3 | BookingService | IRCTC_DB | SELECT * FROM trains |
| 4 | IRCTC_DB | BookingService | trainList |
| 5 | BookingService | WebPortal | availableTrains |
| 6 | Passenger | WebPortal | SelectTrain(trainID) |
| 7 | Passenger | WebPortal | EnterPassengerDetails |
| 8 | WebPortal | BookingService | bookTicket(trainID, pax) |
| 9 | BookingService | PaymentGateway | initiatePayment(amount) |
| 10 | PaymentGateway | BookingService | paymentStatus |
| 11 | BookingService | IRCTC_DB | INSERT INTO tickets |
| 12 | IRCTC_DB | BookingService | ticketID |
| 13 | BookingService | EmailService | sendTicket(paxEmail, ticketID) |
| 14 | EmailService | Passenger | E-Ticket Delivered |
| 15 | BookingService | WebPortal | bookingConfirmation |

**Use of `alt` fragment:** Between steps 9–15, two alternate flows exist:
- **Payment success** → ticket is generated, database updated, email sent.
- **Payment failure** → error message displayed, no ticket created.

**Visual Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor P as Passenger
    participant WP as Web Portal
    participant BS as Booking Service
    participant PG as Payment Gateway
    participant DB as IRCTC Database
    participant EM as Email Service

    P->>WP: SearchTrain(src, dest, date)
    WP->>BS: queryTrains(criteria)
    BS->>DB: SELECT trains
    DB-->>BS: trainList
    BS-->>WP: availableTrains
    WP-->>P: Display Results
    P->>WP: SelectTrain(trainID)
    P->>WP: EnterPassengerDetails
    WP->>BS: bookTicket(trainID, pax)
    activate BS
    BS->>PG: initiatePayment(amount)
    activate PG
    alt Payment Successful
        PG-->>BS: paymentConfirmed
        deactivate PG
        BS->>DB: INSERT INTO tickets
        activate DB
        DB-->>BS: ticketID
        deactivate DB
        BS->>EM: sendTicket(email, ticketID)
        activate EM
        EM-->>P: E-Ticket Email
        deactivate EM
        BS-->>WP: bookingConfirmation
    else Payment Failed
        PG-->>BS: paymentDeclined
        deactivate PG
        BS-->>WP: errorPayload
        WP-->>P: ShowPaymentError
    end
    deactivate BS
```

**(b) Four Combined Fragments Explained:**

| Fragment | Syntax | Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `alt` | `alt [condition1] ... else [condition2] ... end` | Payment success vs failure |
| `opt` | `opt [condition] ... end` | Optional SMS notification on booking |
| `loop` | `loop [count or condition] ... end` | Retry payment 3 times |
| `par` | `par ... else ... end` | Send email and SMS in parallel |

---

##### **Question B (Alternative to A):**

**(a)** Draw the sequence diagram for the scenario *"Customer Withdraws Cash"* in an **ATM System** with proper lifelines, messages, activation bars, and the `alt` fragment for balance check. Explain each step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is a **lifeline** in a sequence diagram? Explain the difference between **activation** and **deactivation** of an object. Also, explain the **return message** notation with an example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The complete sequence diagram is identical to the one rendered in **Section 4.1**. Explanation of each step:

1. *Customer → ATM: Insert Card* — initiates the use case; ATM activates.
2. *ATM → Customer: Request PIN* — asynchronous display prompt.
3. *Customer → ATM: Enter PIN* — synchronous input.
4. *ATM → BankServer: ValidateCredentials* — synchronous API call; BankServer activates.
5. *BankServer → ATM: AuthResponse* — return message; BankServer deactivates.
6. *ATM → Customer: Show Menu* — display options.
7. *Customer → ATM: Select Withdrawal, Enter Amount* — synchronous inputs.
8. *ATM → BankServer: CheckBalance* — synchronous query.
9. **`alt` fragment:** *Sufficient Balance* → dispense, print, debit. *Insufficient Balance* → error.
10. *ATM → Customer: Eject Card* — terminates interaction; ATM deactivates.

**(b) Lifeline, Activation, and Return Message:**

- **Lifeline:** A vertical dashed line representing the **lifetime of an object** from its creation to its destruction. Notation: `objectName : ClassName` at the top.
- **Activation:** A thin **rectangle** drawn on a lifeline indicating the object is *currently executing a method*.
- **Deactivation:** The **end** of the activation bar, indicating the object has *finished processing* and is idle.
- **Return Message:** A **dashed line with an open arrowhead** (`-->>`) used to depict the return value or control flow back to the caller. Example: `BankServer -->> ATM : balanceResult`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
