> 1. **Forgetting arrow types** — A solid filled arrow is *synchronous*; an open arrow is *asynchronous*. Many students draw all arrows identically and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **No activation bars** — Activation rectangles are mandatory. Omission costs 1–2 marks per diagram.
> 3. **Missing `alt` fragment labels** — The `alt` block must clearly show *guard conditions* on each branch (e.g., `[Sufficient Balance]`).
> 4. **Improper lifeline ordering** — Actors must be placed at the *leftmost* position; external systems at the *rightmost*. Wrong order loses aesthetic/structure marks.
> 5. **Return messages omitted** — KTU examiners expect either explicit return arrows or a note justifying the implicit return.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the examination hall!**

- [ ] A **Sequence Diagram** depicts **time-ordered** interactions between objects.
- [ ] Time flows **top → bottom**; lifelines are arranged **left → right**.
- [ ] Four mandatory structural elements: **Lifeline, Activation, Message, Combined Fragment**.
- [ ] **Synchronous arrow** = solid line + filled triangle head.
- [ ] **Asynchronous arrow** = solid line + open V-head.
- [ ] **Return arrow** = dashed line + open V-head (`-->>`).
- [ ] **Self message** = U-shaped arrow on the same lifeline.
- [ ] `alt` fragment models **if-else**; each branch needs a **guard condition** in square brackets.
- [ ] `opt` fragment models a **single conditional** (no else).
- [ ] `loop` fragment models **iteration**; condition placed in header.
- [ ] `par` fragment models **concurrent** execution paths.
- [ ] **Activation bars** are thin rectangles — place them on every lifeline that executes a method.
- [ ] **Actors** (human or external system) are placed at the diagram's left edge with the *stick figure* notation.
- [ ] Always include an **`alt` fragment** whenever a scenario has success/failure outcomes (KTU expects it).
- [ ] **Scenario boundaries**: define clear preconditions and postconditions before drawing.
- [ ] For CASE LAB viva: be ready to explain *why* a particular message is synchronous vs asynchronous.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and draw the collaboration diagram for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and draw the collaboration diagram for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Collaboration Diagram** (also called a **Communication Diagram** in UML 2.x) is a UML behavioral/interaction diagram that emphasizes the **structural organization of objects** that participate in a system interaction, while simultaneously showing the **flow of messages** exchanged between them. It is one of the five diagrams in the UML interaction category, alongside the Sequence Diagram, Timing Diagram, Interaction Overview Diagram, and State Machine Diagram.

In the context of the CASE LAB (Computer-Aided Software Engineering Laboratory), a collaboration diagram is the *output artifact* produced after identifying the actors, objects, classes, and message flows of a given problem statement — typically derived from a previously constructed **Use-Case diagram** and **Class diagram**.

The collaboration diagram is governed by the **OMG UML 2.5 specification** (Object Management Group) and is drawn using the **Rational Unified Process (RUP)** or **Agile Modeling** notations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Board Definition (to memorize verbatim):**
> *"A collaboration diagram is an interaction diagram that displays the interaction between objects and/or parts using sequenced messages arranged in a free-form spatial layout, with the sequence indicated by message numbering."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **group project in your B.Tech class**:
- Every team member is an **object** (a person with a specific role).
- The connections between them (WhatsApp group, shared Google Drive, in-person talks) are **links (associations/connectors)**.
- The instructions they pass to each other ("Anand, share the slides", "Anand → me → send the report") are **messages**.
- The *order* in which these messages occur is indicated by numbering them: `1`, `2`, `2.1`, `2.2`, `3`, `3.1`…

A **collaboration diagram is essentially the WhatsApp chat + contact list rolled into one picture**: it shows you *who is connected to whom* (structure) and *what they said to each other, in what order* (behavior). Unlike a sequence diagram that reads like a time-stamped timeline (top-to-bottom), a collaboration diagram reads like a *geographic map* where the numbers on the arrows give the time order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for quick recall:** **"COLLAB = Connect Objects, Link Actions, Label All By-numbering"**
> - **C**onnect → links (association lines)
> - **O**bjects → rectangles with object name
> - **L**abel → messages on the links
> - **L**ist → sequenced by number prefixes
> - **A**ctor support → stick figures allowed
> - **B**ranch numbering → `1, 2, 2.1, 2.2, 3, 4...`

---

## 1.3 Why this Diagram Exists in the CASE Lab

The CASE Lab (PCCNL607) trains students to use **CASE tools** (Computer-Aided Software Engineering — typically **Rational Rose, StarUML, Violet, Lucidchart, draw.io, or Visual Paradigm**) to produce UML artifacts. A collaboration diagram is a **mandatory deliverable** for Module Experiment 2 because it validates that the student can:

1. Identify the *participating objects* from a use case.
2. Establish the *static relationships* (links) between those objects.
3. Capture the *dynamic message flow* that realizes the use case.
4. Apply the **message numbering scheme** (`1, 1.1, 1.1.1, ...`) correctly.
5. Use a CASE tool to render the diagram with proper UML 2.5 notation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Standard Notation (must appear exactly like this in your answer):**
> - Objects → **Rectangles** with `ObjectName : ClassName` (e.g., `c1 : Customer`).
> - Anonymous objects → `: ClassName` (e.g., `: ATM`).
> - Actors → **Stick figures** (initiated from the system boundary).
> - Links → **Solid lines** between objects/actors.
> - Messages → **Solid arrowed lines** with label `msgName(args) : returnType` and a **sequence number prefix**.

---

## 1.4 UML Version History of the Diagram

| UML Version | Name | Status |
|---|---|---|
| UML 1.x | **Collaboration Diagram** | Primary name |
| UML 2.0+ | **Communication Diagram** | Official rename |
| KTU 2024 (Practical record) | **Collaboration Diagram** | Still used (legacy term accepted) |

KTU accepts **both** "Collaboration Diagram" and "Communication Diagram" as correct answers in lab records and viva-voce.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Collaboration Diagram — Six Mandatory Components

A complete collaboration diagram has exactly **six** components. Missing any one will cost you marks in the record and viva.

### Component 1: Actor
- **Notation:** Stick figure.
- **Purpose:** Represents an external entity (human user, external hardware, or another system) that initiates or participates in the use case.
- **Placement:** Usually on the **periphery** (left or top edge).
- **KTU rule:** A diagram MUST have at least one actor; otherwise it is treated as an *internal* message exchange and the examiner will deduct marks.

### Component 2: Objects (lifelines in static form)
- **Notation:** Rectangle divided into 3 stacked parts:
  - `ObjectName` (optional; the *name* of the instance)
  - `: ClassName` (the class it instantiates)
  - *Optional stereotype* like `<<actor>>, <<boundary>>, <<entity>>, <<control>>`
- **Naming convention:** object names are **lowercase** (e.g., `atm1`, `acct1`), class names are **TitleCase** (e.g., `ATM`, `Account`).
- **Three valid forms:**
  - `obj1 : Class1` — named object of a known class.
  - `obj1 :` — named object of an anonymous class.
  - `: Class1` — anonymous object of a known class.
- **Multi-object notation:** `*obj1` or stacked rectangle indicates a *collection* of objects (used for loops, e.g., iterating over a list of accounts).

### Component 3: Links (Association Lines)
- **Notation:** Solid line (no arrowhead for mere association; the arrowhead is added for the *message*).
- **Purpose:** Represents a path through which messages can flow. A link between two objects is only valid if a corresponding **association exists in the class diagram**.
- **Constraint:** Two objects MUST be connected by a link before a message can be drawn between them.

### Component 4: Messages
- **Notation:** Labeled arrowed line placed *on top of* a link.
- **Format:** `[seqNo] msgName(arg1, arg2) : returnValue`
- **Example:** `1.2.1 withdraw(amount) : boolean`
- **Arrowhead types and meanings:**

| Arrowhead | Meaning | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| ➜ **Filled triangle (solid arrow)** | Procedural call / synchronous message | Synchronous call |
| ➜ **Stick arrow (open V)** | Asynchronous message | Async call |
| ➜ **Dashed arrow (`----`)** | Return message (optional) | Reply |
| ➜ **Stick arrow with small dash** | Reply (alternative) | Reply |

### Component 5: Sequence Numbers (the *soul* of the diagram)
- **Purpose:** Encode the temporal order of message passing without using a vertical time axis.
- **Scheme:** Hierarchical dot notation, `nesting.fork` style.
- **KTU High-Yield Numbering Patterns:**

| Pattern | Meaning | Example scenario |
|---|---|---|
| `1, 2, 3, 4` | Linear, one-after-another | Login flow |
| `1, 1.1, 1.2, 2` | Nested call (1.1 happens during 1) | `1: login() → 1.1: verifyPassword() → 2: showMenu()` |
| `1, 2, 3, 2.1, 2.2` | Re-entrant / recursive | Loop iterations |
| `1, 1.1, 1.1.1, 1.1.2, 1.2` | Deep nesting | `1: placeOrder → 1.1: validate → 1.1.1: checkInventory → 1.1.2: reserveStock → 1.2: confirm` |
| `*, [condition]` | Iteration/guard | `1.1 *[for each item]: process()` |
| `[opt]`, `[alt]`, `[par]`, `[loop]` | UML 2.x interaction fragments | `1.1 [balance > 0]: dispense()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard numbering rule (examiner-tested):** The first digit identifies the level-1 message. The first `.` introduces a sub-message that occurs *during* the parent message. NEVER use spaces, dashes, or letters in sequence numbers.

### Component 6: Messages Sent to Self (Self-call / Self-message)
- **Notation:** A small loop arrow that leaves the object and curves back into the *same* object.
- **Sequence number:** Treated as a normal nested message.
- **Example:** `c1 : Customer → c1 : Customer` with label `1.1.1 : enterPIN()`.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology to Construct a Collaboration Diagram

This is the **canonical 7-step procedure** that examiners look for in your record and viva. Memorize it.

| Step | Action | CASE-Tool Action (e.g., Rational Rose) |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Read the problem statement; identify the *use case* to be modeled. | Create a new Collaboration Diagram in the tool. |
| **2** | Identify the **actor(s)** who initiate or participate. | Drag the *Actor* from the toolbox. |
| **3** | Identify the **boundary, control, and entity objects** (GRASP pattern: **BCE**). | Drag *Object*; name as `name : ClassName`. |
| **4** | Establish the **links** (associations) between objects using the class diagram as reference. | Draw *Link* (solid line) between objects. |
| **5** | Determine the **message flow** that realizes the use case (write pseudo-events first). | Drop *Message* on each link. |
| **6** | Apply the **sequence numbering scheme** hierarchically (`1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, ...`). | Manually number each message label. |
| **7** | Validate by **tracing** through each use-case path; add `[opt]`, `[loop]`, `[alt]` fragments as needed. | Save and export the diagram (PNG/PDF). |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula-Style)

| # | Item | KTU-Accepted Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Actor | Stick figure 🧍 | Initiator of use case |
| 2 | Object | Rect. `obj : Class` | Lowercase obj, TitleCase class |
| 3 | Multi-object | Rect. with `*` or stacked | E.g., `*item : LineItem` |
| 4 | Link | Solid line ─── | No arrowhead on plain link |
| 5 | Sync message | Filled-triangle arrow ➤ | Procedural call |
| 6 | Async message | Stick arrow ⇢ | Fire-and-forget |
| 7 | Return msg | Dashed arrow ⇠ | Optional, sometimes omitted |
| 8 | Self-message | Loop arrow ↺ | Same object |
| 9 | Seq number | `n.m.k...` | Dot-hierarchy |
| 10 | Iteration | `*[for each x]` | UML 2.x loop fragment |
| 11 | Optional | `[opt guard]` | Conditional |
| 12 | Alternative | `[alt cond1 / cond2]` | If-else |
| 13 | Parallel | `[par]` | Concurrent branches |
| 14 | Stereotype | `<<boundary>>` | BCE pattern |
| 15 | Note | Dotted-cornered rect. with 📝 | Annotations |

---

## 2.4 Collaboration Diagram vs Sequence Diagram (Exam Favorite Question)

| Feature | Collaboration Diagram | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | Object structure + message flow | Time-ordered message flow |
| **Time axis** | Implicit (via sequence numbers) | Explicit (vertical lifeline) |
| **Object placement** | Free-form spatial layout | Fixed vertical columns |
| **Reading direction** | Center-out (any direction) | Top-to-bottom |
| **Link emphasis** | Strong (links are first-class) | Weak (links often omitted) |
| **Message ordering** | Numbered (e.g., `1.2.1`) | Vertical position |
| **Best for** | Understanding object *collaboration* network | Understanding procedural *flow* |
| **CASE tool support** | Supported in Rational Rose, StarUML, VP | Universally supported |
| **KTU preference** | Module Experiment 2 | Module Experiment 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> Both diagrams are **isomorphic** — they convey the same information; only the *emphasis* differs. A smart CASE tool (e.g., Rational Rose) can **auto-generate one from the other**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Collaboration diagrams are used in:
1. **Reverse engineering** legacy systems (object discovery from logs).
2. **Distributed system design** (mapping RPC / REST calls between microservices).
3. **Embedded systems** (signal/message flow between software components in an ECU).
4. **Robotics control software** (sensor-actuator message choreography).
5. **Microservices choreography** in modern cloud-native architectures.
6. **API contract design** (sequence numbers map to the OpenAPI operation order).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Case Study Library Management System

We will work through **one full KTU-style problem** end-to-end so that you can replicate the methodology in your record.

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Sample)

> *"A student requests a book from the Library Management System. The system checks the student's membership. If membership is valid and the book is available, the system issues the book and updates the inventory. A confirmation is sent back to the student."*

## 3.2 Identifying the Use Case

**Use Case:** `Issue Book` (or `Borrow Book`)

## 3.3 Step 1 — Identify the Actor

- **Primary actor:** `Student` (initiates the use case).
- *No secondary actors in this simple case.*

## 3.4 Step 2 — Identify Objects (BCE Pattern)

| Stereotype | Object | Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<<actor>>` | `student1` | `Student` | External initiator |
| `<<boundary>>` | `win1` | `IssueBookWindow` | GUI / interface |
| `<<control>>` | `ctrl1` | `IssueBookController` | Coordinates business logic |
| `<<entity>>` | `mem1` | `Membership` | Student membership record |
| `<<entity>>` | `book1` | `Book` | Book being requested |
| `<<entity>>` | `inv1` | `Inventory` | Stock/inventory record |

## 3.5 Step 3 — Draw the Spatial Layout

A KTU-accepted layout places the **actor on the far left**, the **boundary next to the actor**, the **control in the middle**, and the **entity objects on the right** (forming the classic "data cluster"):

```
[Student] --- [IssueBookWindow] --- [IssueBookController] --- [Membership, Book, Inventory]
```

## 3.6 Step 4 — Identify Links (from the class diagram)

- `Student ↔ IssueBookWindow` (association: "uses")
- `IssueBookWindow ↔ IssueBookController` (association: "calls")
- `IssueBookController ↔ Membership` (association: "validates")
- `IssueBookController ↔ Book` (association: "queries")
- `IssueBookController ↔ Inventory` (association: "updates")
- `Membership ↔ Student` (implicit; the controller already knows)

## 3.7 Step 5 — Identify the Messages (with arguments and returns)

| # | From → To | Message | Returns | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Student → IssueBookWindow | `requestBook(bookID)` | — | Initial trigger |
| 1.1 | IssueBookWindow → IssueBookController | `issueBook(bookID, studentID)` | `boolean` | Forwarded to control |
| 1.1.1 | IssueBookController → Membership | `isValid(studentID)` | `true` | Validity check |
| 1.1.2 | IssueBookController → Book | `isAvailable(bookID)` | `true` | Availability check |
| 1.1.3 | IssueBookController → Inventory | `decrementStock(bookID)` | `void` | Update inventory |
| 1.1.4 | IssueBookController → Book | `setBorrower(studentID)` | — | Mark book as issued |
| 1.2 | IssueBookController → IssueBookWindow | `confirmIssue(receipt)` | — | Return result |
| 2 | IssueBookWindow → Student | `displayConfirmation()` | — | Final UI update |

## 3.8 Step 6 — Apply the Numbering Scheme

The numbering follows strict rules:
- Top-level messages (initiated by the actor) get a single integer: `1`, `2`, `3`.
- Messages sent *during* a parent message are nested: `1.1`, `1.2`, etc.
- Sub-sub-messages are `1.1.1`, `1.1.2`, ...

For the case above the numbering has been done in the table.

## 3.9 Step 7 — Add Interaction Fragments (KTU Bonus Marks)

A more rigorous version would wrap the validation in a `[opt]` fragment and the loop in a `[loop]`. For example:

```
1.1.1 [opt membership is valid]: isValid(studentID) : true
1.1.2 [opt book exists and is available]: isAvailable(bookID) : true
1.1.3 *[guard: stock > 0]: decrementStock(bookID)
```

> [!NOTE]
> Adding `[opt]`, `[alt]`, `[par]`, `[loop]` fragments is a **bonus mark technique** that examiners actively reward in PCCNL607.

## 3.10 The Final Collaboration Diagram (Conceptual Snapshot)

```
            ┌──────────┐                                  ┌─────────────┐
            │  inv1    │                                  │   book1     │
            │ :Inventory│ ◄────1.1.3───────────── ─── ──► │   : Book    │
            └──────────┘                                  └─────────────┘
                   ▲                                              ▲
                   │1.1.3 decrementStock                          │1.1.4 setBorrower
                   │                                              │
   ┌───────────────┴──────────┐                          ┌────────┴────────┐
   │       ctrl1             │                          │      book1     │
   │  :IssueBookController   │                          │     : Book     │
   └──────────┬───────────────┘                          └────────────────┘
              ▲1.1 issueBook
              │
   ┌──────────┴───────────────┐
   │       win1              │
   │  :IssueBookWindow       │
   └──────────┬───────────────┘
              ▲1 requestBook
              │
   🧍 student1
   (Student)
              ▼2 displayConfirmation
```

(Spatial placement; arrows omitted for ASCII legibility — see **Mermaid diagram in Section 4**.)

## 3.11 The Complete Algorithm (Pseudocode of the Use Case)

This pseudocode is what you'd put in the *Observation* section of your record alongside the diagram:

```
ALGORITHM IssueBook(bookID, studentID)
BEGIN
    // Step 1: Receive request from student
    win1.requestBook(bookID)
    
    // Step 2: Forward to controller
    win1.issueBook(bookID, studentID)  → ctrl1
    
    // Step 3: Validate membership
    IF ctrl1.isValid(studentID) = true THEN   // message 1.1.1
        // Step 4: Check book availability
        IF ctrl1.isAvailable(bookID) = true THEN  // message 1.1.2
            // Step 5: Update inventory
            ctrl1.decrementStock(bookID)  // message 1.1.3
            // Step 6: Mark book as issued
            ctrl1.setBorrower(bookID, studentID)  // message 1.1.4
            // Step 7: Confirm
            ctrl1.confirmIssue(receipt)  // message 1.2
        ELSE
            ctrl1.showError("Book not available")
        END IF
    ELSE
        ctrl1.showError("Invalid membership")
    END IF
    
    // Step 8: Display to student
    win1.displayConfirmation()  // message 2
END
```

The pseudocode is **isomorphic** to the message sequence numbers in the diagram. Each numbered message corresponds to one line in the algorithm — this 1:1 mapping is the examiner's quality check.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Below are two KTU-grade diagrams rendered in Mermaid (one sequence for cross-reference, one collaboration). Both are CASE-tool-equivalent and can be re-created in Rational Rose, StarUML, draw.io, or Visual Paradigm.

## 4.1 Collaboration Diagram — Library Management System (Issue Book Use Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actor1(["🧍 student1 : Student <<actor>>"])
    win1["win1 : IssueBookWindow <<boundary>>"]
    ctrl1["ctrl1 : IssueBookController <<control>>"]
    mem1["mem1 : Membership <<entity>>"]
    book1["book1 : Book <<entity>>"]
    inv1["inv1 : Inventory <<entity>>"]

    actor1 -- "1 : requestBook(bookID)" --> win1
    win1 -- "1.1 : issueBook(bookID, studentID) : boolean" --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 -- "1.1.1 : isValid(studentID) : true" --> mem1
    ctrl1 -- "1.1.2 : isAvailable(bookID) : true" --> book1
    ctrl1 -- "1.1.3 : decrementStock(bookID)" --> inv1
    ctrl1 -- "1.1.4 : setBorrower(studentID)" --> book1
    ctrl1 -- "1.2 : confirmIssue(receipt)" --> win1
    win1 -- "2 : displayConfirmation()" --> actor1
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **direction of arrows** shows who sends a message to whom.
- The **prefix number** (`1`, `1.1`, `1.1.1`, `1.1.2`, …) shows the **temporal order** of execution.
- The **text after the colon** is the *message name* with its *arguments* and (optionally) its *return type*.
- The **stereotypes** (`<<boundary>>`, `<<control>>`, `<<entity>>`) implement the GRASP BCE pattern, which KTU examiners look for.

## 4.2 Equivalent Sequence Diagram (For Cross-Verification in Lab)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor S as 🧍 student1 : Student
    participant W as win1 : IssueBookWindow
    participant C as ctrl1 : IssueBookController
    participant M as mem1 : Membership
    participant B as book1 : Book
    participant I as inv1 : Inventory

    S->>W: 1: requestBook(bookID)
    W->>C: 1.1: issueBook(bookID, studentID) : boolean
    C->>M: 1.1.1: isValid(studentID) : true
    C->>B: 1.1.2: isAvailable(bookID) : true
    C->>I: 1.1.3: decrementStock(bookID)
    C->>B: 1.1.4: setBorrower(studentID)
    C-->>W: 1.2: confirmIssue(receipt)
    W->>S: 2: displayConfirmation()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **numbers in the sequence diagram match exactly the numbers in the collaboration diagram** (e.g., `1.1.3 : decrementStock`). This is a non-negotiable rule in the CASE Lab: the two diagrams MUST be semantically equivalent. Always re-verify.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (How the Diagram Fits in the Software Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Statement"] --> B["Use-Case Diagram<br/>Identify actors and use cases"]
    B --> C["Class Diagram<br/>Identify classes and associations"]
    C --> D["Sequence Diagram<br/>Identify message flow with time axis"]
    D --> E["Collaboration Diagram<br/>Reorganize on object structure<br/>Add sequence numbers"]
    E --> F["Activity / State Diagrams<br/>Optional: process and lifecycle"]
    F --> G["Implementation in CASE Tool<br/>Rational Rose / StarUML / draw.io"]
    G --> H["Code Generation<br/>Skeleton classes with method stubs"]

    style A fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#d6ffe9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffd6f0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffe0d6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#d6ffff,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

This flow shows **where the collaboration diagram sits in the full UML deliverable pipeline**. The collaboration diagram in **Module Experiment 2** assumes that the **Use-Case diagram (Module 1)**, **Class diagram (Module 2)**, and **Sequence diagram (Module 3)** have already been produced earlier in the lab.

## 4.4 Message Numbering Decision Tree (Examiner's Mental Checklist)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the message initiated<br/>by the actor?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> N1["Use plain integer: 1, 2, 3, …"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Does it occur *during*<br/>a parent message?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> N2["Append a .n suffix<br/>e.g., parent 1 → child 1.1, 1.2"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is it a *return* message?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> N3["Use dashed arrow<br/>number = parent number + .r<br/>or simply omit"]
    Q3 -- No --> N4["Re-evaluate: it should be<br/>a top-level message"]
    N1 --> V["Validate using<br/>the algorithm pseudocode"]
    N2 --> V
    N3 --> V
    N4 --> V
```

Use this decision tree whenever you are unsure how to number a message. **Always cross-check against your algorithm pseudocode** — if the pseudocode line is nested inside an `IF` block, the message should be nested in the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCNL607]`
**Define a collaboration diagram. List any four components of a collaboration diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A collaboration diagram is a UML 2.x interaction diagram that depicts the **structural organization of objects** participating in a system interaction and the **messages they exchange**, with the temporal order of messages indicated by a **hierarchical sequence-numbering scheme** (e.g., `1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, ...`).

Four components (1/2 mark each, 2 marks total):
1. **Actor** — external initiator (stick figure).
2. **Objects** — instances of classes (rectangles `obj : Class`).
3. **Links** — solid lines connecting objects/actors.
4. **Messages** — arrowed lines with sequence-numbered labels.

*(For 3 marks, the definition is 1 mark and the four components are 1/2 mark each.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCCNL607]`
**Differentiate between a sequence diagram and a collaboration diagram. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| # | Sequence Diagram | Collaboration Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time is the primary axis (vertical lifeline). | Object structure is the primary axis (spatial layout). |
| 2 | Message order is shown by vertical position. | Message order is shown by **sequence numbers** (e.g., `1.1.1`). |
| 3 | Reads **top-to-bottom** strictly. | Reads **in any direction** (center-out). |
| 4 | Links between objects are often implicit. | Links are first-class citizens and explicitly drawn. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 rule:** Module-level internal choice means you will be given *either* Question A *or* Question B (each carrying 14 marks, split as 7+7).

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Design and draw a collaboration diagram for the following case study:**
*"A customer withdraws cash from an ATM. The ATM authenticates the customer using a PIN, contacts the bank server to verify the balance, dispenses cash if the balance is sufficient, deducts the amount, prints a receipt, and ejects the card."*

**Sub-parts (a) and (b):**

#### (a) (7 marks) Identify the actor, objects (with BCE stereotypes), and links. Write the message sequence with sequence numbers.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Actor (1 mark):**
- `customer1 : Customer` (`<<actor>>`)

**2. Objects with BCE stereotypes (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Object | Stereotype | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `screen1` | `<<boundary>>` | `ATMScreen` |
| 2 | `cardSlot1` | `<<boundary>>` | `CardReader` |
| 3 | `cash1` | `<<boundary>>` | `CashDispenser` |
| 4 | `printer1` | `<<boundary>>` | `ReceiptPrinter` |
| 5 | `atc1` | `<<control>>` | `ATMController` |
| 6 | `bank1` | `<<entity>>` | `BankServer` |
| 7 | `acct1` | `<<entity>>` | `Account` |
| 8 | `txn1` | `<<entity>>` | `Transaction` |

**3. Links (1 mark):**
- `customer1 ↔ screen1`, `customer1 ↔ cardSlot1`, `customer1 ↔ cash1`, `customer1 ↔ printer1`
- `screen1 ↔ atc1`, `cardSlot1 ↔ atc1`, `cash1 ↔ atc1`, `printer1 ↔ atc1`
- `atc1 ↔ bank1`, `atc1 ↔ acct1`, `atc1 ↔ txn1`, `bank1 ↔ acct1`

**4. Message sequence with numbers (2 marks):**

| # | From → To | Message | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | customer1 → cardSlot1 | `insertCard(cardNo)` | — |
| 1.1 | cardSlot1 → atc1 | `readCard(cardNo)` | `cardData` |
| 2 | customer1 → screen1 | `enterPIN(pin)` | — |
| 2.1 | screen1 → atc1 | `authenticate(pin, cardData)` | `boolean` |
| 2.1.1 | atc1 → bank1 | `verifyPIN(pin, cardNo)` | `true` |
| 3 | customer1 → screen1 | `selectWithdraw()` | — |
| 3.1 | screen1 → atc1 | `withdraw(amount)` | `dispenseResult` |
| 3.1.1 | atc1 → bank1 | `checkBalance(acctNo)` | `balance` |
| 3.1.2 | atc1 → acct1 | `debit(amount)` | `newBalance` |
| 3.1.3 | atc1 → txn1 | `logTransaction(amount)` | — |
| 3.1.4 | atc1 → bank1 | `updateBalance(newBalance)` | `ack` |
| 3.1.5 | atc1 → cash1 | `dispenseCash(amount)` | `success` |
| 3.1.6 | atc1 → printer1 | `printReceipt(txn)` | — |
| 4 | atc1 → cardSlot1 | `ejectCard()` | — |

#### (b) (7 marks) Draw the collaboration diagram for the above scenario using proper UML 2.5 notation and explain the role of sequence numbering.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Diagram (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cust(["🧍 customer1 : Customer <<actor>>"])
    screen["screen1 : ATMScreen <<boundary>>"]
    card["cardSlot1 : CardReader <<boundary>>"]
    cash["cash1 : CashDispenser <<boundary>>"]
    print["printer1 : ReceiptPrinter <<boundary>>"]
    ctrl["atc1 : ATMController <<control>>"]
    bank["bank1 : BankServer <<entity>>"]
    acct["acct1 : Account <<entity>>"]
    txn["txn1 : Transaction <<entity>>"]

    cust -- "1: insertCard(cardNo)" --> card
    card -- "1.1: readCard(cardNo)" --> ctrl
    cust -- "2: enterPIN(pin)" --> screen
    screen -- "2.1: authenticate(pin, cardData) : boolean" --> ctrl
    ctrl -- "2.1.1: verifyPIN(pin, cardNo) : true" --> bank
    cust -- "3: selectWithdraw()" --> screen
    screen -- "3.1: withdraw(amount) : dispenseResult" --> ctrl
    ctrl -- "3.1.1: checkBalance(acctNo) : balance" --> bank
    ctrl -- "3.1.2: debit(amount) : newBalance" --> acct
    ctrl -- "3.1.3: logTransaction(amount)" --> txn
    ctrl -- "3.1.4: updateBalance(newBalance) : ack" --> bank
    ctrl -- "3.1.5: dispenseCash(amount) : success" --> cash
    ctrl -- "3.1.6: printReceipt(txn)" --> print
    ctrl -- "4: ejectCard()" --> card
```

**Explanation of sequence numbering (2 marks):**

Sequence numbers in a collaboration diagram use a **hierarchical dot-notation** to convey *temporal* order without a time axis. The pattern works as follows:

- **Top-level integers** (`1`, `2`, `3`, `4`) mark messages **initiated by the actor** or top-of-flow events.
- **First-level nesting** (`1.1`, `1.2`, …) marks messages that occur **during** the parent message — i.e., while the parent message is being processed.
- **Second-level nesting** (`1.1.1`, `1.1.2`, …) marks messages that occur **during** `1.1`.

In the ATM example:
- `1: insertCard` is the top-level action.
- `1.1: readCard` happens *during* `1: insertCard` (the card reader hands the data to the controller).
- `3.1.5: dispenseCash` happens deep inside the `3.1: withdraw` call, after balance verification, debit, and transaction logging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Missing the actor** → −1 mark. Every collaboration diagram MUST show the actor(s).
> 2. **Missing BCE stereotypes** (`<<boundary>>`, `<<control>>`, `<<entity>>`) → −1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong sequence-number scheme** (e.g., using letters, dashes, or wrong nesting) → −2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing messages that are not on a valid link** (no association in the class diagram) → −1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the return type** when it is important for the use case (e.g., `: boolean` for `authenticate`) → −1/2 mark.
> 6. **Inconsistency with the sequence diagram** (numbers don't match the sequence diagram) → −2 marks.
> 7. **Using a sequence diagram and labeling it as a collaboration diagram** → 0 marks (full credit lost).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Design and draw a collaboration diagram for the following case study:**
*"A passenger books a flight ticket online. The user logs in, searches for flights, selects a flight, provides passenger details, makes payment through a payment gateway, and receives a confirmation e-ticket on e-mail."*

#### (a) (7 marks) Identify the use case, actor(s), objects (with BCE stereotypes), and the message sequence with sequence numbers.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Use Case (1/2 mark):** `Book Flight Ticket`
**2. Actor (1/2 mark):** `passenger1 : Passenger` (`<<actor>>`)

**3. Objects (3 marks — 0.5 each × 6):**

| # | Object | Stereotype | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `web1` | `<<boundary>>` | `BookingPortal` |
| 2 | `loginWin1` | `<<boundary>>` | `LoginWindow` |
| 3 | `bookCtrl1` | `<<control>>` | `BookingController` |
| 4 | `search1` | `<<entity>>` | `FlightSearch` |
| 5 | `flight1` | `<<entity>>` | `Flight` |
| 6 | `pay1` | `<<boundary>>` | `PaymentGateway` |
| 7 | `ticket1` | `<<entity>>` | `ETicket` |
| 8 | `mail1` | `<<boundary>>` | `MailService` |

**4. Links (1 mark):** Passenger↔LoginWindow↔BookingController; BookingController↔FlightSearch, Flight, PaymentGateway, ETicket, MailService.

**5. Message sequence (2 marks):**

| # | From → To | Message | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | passenger1 → loginWin1 | `login(userID, password)` | — |
| 1.1 | loginWin1 → bookCtrl1 | `authenticate(userID, password)` | `boolean` |
| 2 | passenger1 → web1 | `searchFlights(src, dest, date)` | — |
| 2.1 | web1 → bookCtrl1 | `findFlights(src, dest, date)` | `List<Flight>` |
| 2.1.1 | bookCtrl1 → search1 | `query(src, dest, date)` | `flightsList` |
| 3 | passenger1 → web1 | `selectFlight(flightID)` | — |
| 3.1 | web1 → bookCtrl1 | `bookFlight(flightID, passengerData)` | `Ticket` |
| 3.1.1 | bookCtrl1 → flight1 | `reserveSeat(flightID)` | `seatNo` |
| 3.1.2 | bookCtrl1 → pay1 | `processPayment(amount, cardInfo)` | `paymentStatus` |
| 3.1.3 | bookCtrl1 → ticket1 | `generateETicket(flightID, seatNo)` | `ticket` |
| 3.1.4 | bookCtrl1 → mail1 | `sendETicket(email, ticket)` | `sentStatus` |
| 4 | web1 → passenger1 | `displayConfirmation(ticket)` | — |

#### (b) (7 marks) Draw the collaboration diagram and justify why a collaboration diagram is preferred over a sequence diagram for this scenario.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pax(["🧍 passenger1 : Passenger <<actor>>"])
    lw["loginWin1 : LoginWindow <<boundary>>"]
    web["web1 : BookingPortal <<boundary>>"]
    bc["bookCtrl1 : BookingController <<control>>"]
    fs["search1 : FlightSearch <<entity>>"]
    fl["flight1 : Flight <<entity>>"]
    pay["pay1 : PaymentGateway <<boundary>>"]
    tk["ticket1 : ETicket <<entity>>"]
    mail["mail1 : MailService <<boundary>>"]

    pax -- "1: login(userID, password)" --> lw
    lw -- "1.1: authenticate(userID, password) : boolean" --> bc
    pax -- "2: searchFlights(src, dest, date)" --> web
    web -- "2.1: findFlights(src, dest, date) : List Flight" --> bc
    bc -- "2.1.1: query(src, dest, date) : flightsList" --> fs
    pax -- "3: selectFlight(flightID)" --> web
    web -- "3.1: bookFlight(flightID, passengerData) : Ticket" --> bc
    bc -- "3.1.1: reserveSeat(flightID) : seatNo" --> fl
    bc -- "3.1.2: processPayment(amount, cardInfo) : paymentStatus" --> pay
    bc -- "3.1.3: generateETicket(flightID, seatNo) : ticket" --> tk
    bc -- "3.1.4: sendETicket(email, ticket) : sentStatus" --> mail
    web -- "4: displayConfirmation(ticket)" --> pax
```

**Justification — why collaboration diagram is preferred here (2 marks):**

A collaboration diagram is **preferred for the flight-booking scenario** for two reasons:

1. **Many boundary objects interact with one controller.** The scenario involves at least **four** boundary classes (`LoginWindow`, `BookingPortal`, `PaymentGateway`, `MailService`) all coordinated by a single `BookingController`. A collaboration diagram *visually emphasizes this hub-and-spoke structural pattern* of the controller, which a sequence diagram would obscure by stretching everything into a long vertical timeline.

2. **Deep message nesting.** Messages like `3.1.2 : processPayment` and `3.1.4 : sendETicket` are nested four levels deep (`3 → 3.1 → 3.1.2`). Sequence diagrams handle this with a *very long* vertical lifeline, which becomes hard to read. A collaboration diagram expresses the same nesting **compactly** using dot-notation in the message label.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Print this section and stick it in your record's last page.**

✅ **Definition:** A collaboration/communication diagram models object structure + message flow, with time order shown via hierarchical sequence numbers (`n.n.n`).

✅ **Six mandatory components:** Actor, Object, Link, Message, Sequence Number, Self-message (loop arrow).

✅ **Three valid object forms:** `obj : Class`, `obj :` (anonymous class), `: Class` (anonymous object).

✅ **BCE Pattern (Examiner's favorite):** Every object MUST be stereotyped as `<<boundary>>`, `<<control>>`, or `<<entity>>`.

✅ **Sequence-number rules:**
- Top-level: `1, 2, 3, 4` (typically actor-initiated).
- Nesting: `1.1` happens *during* `1`; `1.1.1` happens *during* `1.1`.
- Use `[opt]`, `[alt]`, `[loop]`, `[par]` fragments for conditions and iterations.

✅ **Notation rules:**
- Solid line = link (no arrow).
- Filled triangle = synchronous message.
- Stick arrow = asynchronous message.
- Dashed arrow = return message (optional).
- Object rectangle = `name : ClassName`.

✅ **7-step methodology:** (1) Read problem → (2) Identify actor → (3) BCE objects → (4) Draw spatial layout → (5) Add links → (6) Number messages → (7) Validate using pseudocode.

✅ **Isomorphism with Sequence Diagram:** The two diagrams MUST have the *same messages and the same numbers*. Always cross-verify.

✅ **CASE Tools used in KTU lab:** Rational Rose, StarUML, Violet UML, Lucidchart, draw.io, Visual Paradigm, PlantUML.

✅ **Common mistakes to avoid:** Missing actor, missing BCE stereotypes, wrong sequence numbers, no link for a message, using a sequence diagram in place of a collaboration diagram, inconsistent numbering between the two diagrams.

✅ **Examiner's mantra:** *"If the message order is wrong, the entire diagram is wrong — sequence numbering is the soul of the collaboration diagram."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and draw the state chart diagram for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and draw the state chart diagram for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Chart Diagram (SCD)** — A *UML 2.5 behavioral diagram* (formerly known as a *state machine diagram*) that models the **dynamic lifecycle of a single object or system** as it responds to a stream of **events** by transitioning from one **state** to another. It captures the *reactive behavior* of an entity, where the entity's past influences its current reaction, and the diagram explicitly enumerates every *discrete condition* (state) the entity can occupy.

In a CASE Lab context (using tools like **StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, Enterprise Architect, ArgoUML, or Lucidchart**), a State Chart Diagram is the artifact produced under **Experiment 2** to document event-driven behavior of objects identified during Requirement Analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Activity / Flowchart Diagrams:**
> - **State Chart** = *what an object is doing* (its condition at a moment) → driven by **events**.
> - **Activity Diagram** = *what a process is doing* (its flow of work) → driven by **completion of actions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **traffic signal controller** at a junction:

- It is always in exactly one of the *colors* — **RED, YELLOW, or GREEN** — these are its **states**.
- A **timer expiry** event moves it from GREEN → YELLOW → RED.
- The system does not *flow* through the colors; it **snaps** from one to the next when a trigger fires.
- There is an **initial state** (when the signal first powers on) and often a **flashing-yellow / standby** state for failure.

A State Chart Diagram is exactly this — a *color-coded map* of the object's existence. The object **sits** in a state, **waits** for an event, **decides** (via a guard) whether to react, and **jumps** to the next state while performing an action.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** **S-T-E-A-G-A**
> **S**tate — **T**rigger — **E**vent — **A**ction — **G**uard — **A**rrow
> Every transition arrow carries: *Event [Guard] / Action*.

### 1.3 Standard Notation Metrics (UML 2.5 Compliant)

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **● (filled circle)** | Initial Pseudostate (one per diagram) |
| **◎ (bullseye)** | Final State (object lifecycle ends) |
| **Rounded Rectangle** | State |
| **Arrow →** | Transition (directed) |
| **H inside a circle** | History Pseudostate (resume last sub-state) |
| **Bar with two transitions** | Fork / Join (concurrent regions) |
| **Diamond** | Choice Pseudostate (dynamic branching) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a State Chart and not just a Table?**
> A transition table can list all (state, event) pairs, but a diagram **reveals unreachable states, dead-ends, and missing events visually** — these are the three classic defects examiners test for in KTU viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

### 2.1 The Five First-Class Citizens of a State Chart

A State Chart Diagram is constructed from exactly **five orthogonal components**. Each must be explicitly identifiable in your KTU answer to fetch full marks.

1. **State (S):** A *named condition* in which the object waits for events. The object remains in a state for a *non-instantaneous* interval.
2. **Event (E):** A *discrete occurrence* in time (e.g., `insertCard`, `timeout`, `buttonPress`). Events are the *only* triggers that can fire a transition.
3. **Guard (G):** A *Boolean predicate* evaluated *at the moment of the event*. The transition fires **only if** `G = true`. Guards do not consume time.
4. **Action (A):** An *atomic, instantaneous computation* (e.g., `/debit`, `/logError`). Actions **do not block** and are not interruptible.
5. **Activity (within a state):** A *long-running computation* that begins on `entry` and ends on `exit`. Modeled with `do:` keyword.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trick Question Examiners Ask:** "Can a state have neither activity nor action?" — Yes. Such a state is a **passive wait state** (e.g., `Idle`, `Waiting`).

### 2.2 Internal Structure of a State

A state can be expanded into **compartments** (regions inside the rounded rectangle):

```
+------------------------------+
| StateName                    |  ← Name compartment
+------------------------------+
| entry / initialiseDisplay    |  ← Entry action
| do / processBackgroundJob    |  ← Ongoing activity
| exit / cleanupResources      |  ← Exit action
+------------------------------+
| Internal Transition 1        |  ← Handled locally
+------------------------------+
```

### 2.3 Transition Syntax (KTU 14-Mark Marking Key)

Every transition arrow in your diagram **must** follow this canonical label format:

$$\text{Transition Label} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Event}}_{\text{Trigger}} \;[\, \underbrace{\text{Guard}}_{\text{Boolean}} \,] \;/\; \underbrace{\text{Action}}_{\text{Atomic}}$$

> Every part is **optional**, but a KTU top-band answer always shows all three explicitly.

### 2.4 High-Yield Notation Sheet (Memorize for ESE)

| # | Notation | UML Meaning | Where It Appears |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `●` | Initial pseudostate | Start of the diagram |
| 2 | `◎` | Final state | End of lifecycle |
| 3 | `S1 --> S2 : e` | Transition triggered by event `e` | Arrow label |
| 4 | `S1 --> S2 : e [x>0]` | Guard condition `x>0` required | Arrow label in brackets |
| 5 | `S1 --> S2 : e / a` | Action `a` executed on transition | Arrow label after `/` |
| 6 | `S1 : entry / A` | Entry action `A` | Inside state name compartment |
| 7 | `S1 : do / A` | Activity `A` while in state | Inside state name compartment |
| 8 | `S1 : exit / A` | Exit action `A` | Inside state name compartment |
| 9 | `(H)` | History pseudostate | Inside composite state |
| 10 | `||` | Concurrent regions separator | Between parallel state compartments |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

State Chart Diagrams are mandated in **safety-critical** and **event-driven** system specifications:

- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Modeling ECU (Engine Control Unit) modes — Ignition-Off, Cranking, Running, Limp-Home.
- **Aviation (DO-178C):** Modeling flight-control surface actuators — Stowed, Deploying, Deployed, Locked.
- **Embedded IoT:** Modeling Wi-Fi module states — Disconnected, Scanning, Authenticating, Connected, Sleep.
- **Banking (PCI-DSS):** Modeling ATM transaction lifecycle (the case study used in this experiment).
- **Telecommunications (3GPP):** Modeling call state machines — Idle, Paging, Ringing, Active, Released.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this in CASE Lab:** The diagram is the *only* UML artifact that is **directly translatable into finite state machine code** for PLC, VHDL, or embedded C targets. A well-designed state chart can be *mechanically* converted to a switch-case implementation — a fact examiners exploit in viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Design Methodology & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 14-mark design question always follows this exact 7-step recipe.** Memorize and reproduce the steps in order — examiners award partial marks for *process* even when the final diagram has a minor error.

### Canonical Case Study: **ATM (Automated Teller Machine) Withdrawal**

**Problem Statement:** *Design a State Chart Diagram for the withdrawal use case of an ATM. The system shall accept a card, validate the PIN (max 3 attempts), allow withdrawal of cash, dispense cash, and print a receipt. The system shall be available only when cash is present and the network is online.*

---

### Step 1 — Identify the *Subject Object*

The SCD models **one object's lifecycle**, not the entire system. Choose the object that exhibits *interesting* state changes.

$$\text{Subject} = \text{ATM Machine} \quad (\text{or its controller class } \texttt{ATMController})$$

> **Justification (write in exam):** The ATM transitions through a finite, well-defined set of conditions (Card-In, Card-Out, Authenticated, Dispensing, etc.) in response to user and network events. It is the canonical reactive object in this problem.

---

### Step 2 — Identify All *States* (S)

A state is **a condition where the object waits**. List every such condition exhaustively.

| ID | State | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S_1$ | **OutOfService** | No cash OR network down OR maintenance mode |
| $S_2$ | **Idle** | Awaiting card insertion |
| $S_3$ | **CardInserted** | Card in slot, not yet validated |
| $S_4$ | **PINEntry** | Awaiting PIN input from customer |
| $S_5$ | **Authenticating** | Verifying PIN with bank |
| $S_6$ | **Blocked** | 3 failed PIN attempts — card retained |
| $S_7$ | **Authenticated** | PIN valid, ready for transaction |
| $S_8$ | **TransactionSelection** | Awaiting choice (Withdraw/Balance) |
| $S_9$ | **EnterAmount** | Awaiting amount input |
| $S_{10}$ | **ValidatingAmount** | Checking balance & ATM cash |
| $S_{11}$ | **Dispensing** | Cash being dispensed |
| $S_{12}$ | **PrintingReceipt** | Receipt being printed |
| $S_{13}$ | **BalanceEnquiry** | Showing balance (sub-transaction) |
| $\bullet$ | **(Final)** | Only reached on graceful shutdown |

That gives **13 named states** plus **1 initial pseudostate** and **1 final state**.

---

### Step 3 — Identify All *Events* (E)

Events are **stimuli** that may trigger a transition. They are *instantaneous* in time.

| ID | Event | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_1$ | `serviceOK` | Self-diagnostic passes |
| $E_2$ | `insertCard` | Customer |
| $E_3$ | `cardAccepted` | Card reader |
| $E_4$ | `invalidCard` | Card reader |
| $E_5$ | `enterPIN(p)` | Customer |
| $E_6$ | `validPIN` | Bank server |
| $E_7$ | `invalidPIN` | Bank server |
| $E_8$ | `adminReset` | Bank administrator |
| $E_9$ | `selectTransaction(t)` | Customer |
| $E_{10}$ | `timeout` | Internal timer |
| $E_{11}$ | `cancel` | Customer |
| $E_{12}$ | `chooseAmount(a)` | Customer |
| $E_{13}$ | `confirmAmount` | Customer |
| $E_{14}$ | `sufficientFunds` | Bank server |
| $E_{15}$ | `insufficientFunds` | Bank server |
| $E_{16}$ | `cashDispensed` | Cash dispenser |
| $E_{17}$ | `receiptPrinted` | Printer |
| $E_{18}$ | `ejectCard` | System (auto) or Customer |
| $E_{19}$ | `networkDown` | Self-monitor |
| $E_{20}$ | `cashEmpty` | Self-monitor |

---

### Step 4 — Construct the Transition Table (Analytical Matrix)

This is the **core deliverable** for a 14-mark KTU question. Every row must specify: *from-state*, *event*, *guard*, *action*, *to-state*. Leave no cell ambiguous.

| # | From State | Event | Guard | Action | To State |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | OutOfService | `serviceOK` | cash $>$ 0 $\wedge$ net OK | `entry: initialise` | Idle |
| 2 | Idle | `insertCard` | none | none | CardInserted |
| 3 | CardInserted | `cardAccepted` | none | `readCard` | PINEntry |
| 4 | CardInserted | `invalidCard` | none | `ejectCard`, `logAttempt` | Idle |
| 5 | PINEntry | `enterPIN(p)` | none | `sendToBank(p)` | Authenticating |
| 6 | PINEntry | `cancel` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 7 | PINEntry | `timeout` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 8 | Authenticating | `validPIN` | none | `resetAttempts=0` | Authenticated |
| 9 | Authenticating | `invalidPIN` | attempts $<$ 3 | `ejectCard`, `logFail` | Idle |
| 10 | Authenticating | `invalidPIN` | attempts $\geq 3$ | `retainCard`, `alertBank` | Blocked |
| 11 | Blocked | `adminReset` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 12 | Authenticated | `selectTransaction` | none | `showMenu` | TransactionSelection |
| 13 | Authenticated | `timeout` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 14 | TransactionSelection | `selectWithdraw` | none | none | EnterAmount |
| 15 | TransactionSelection | `selectBalance` | none | `fetchBalance` | BalanceEnquiry |
| 16 | TransactionSelection | `cancel` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 17 | EnterAmount | `chooseAmount(a)` | a mod 100 $=$ 0 | none | ValidatingAmount |
| 18 | EnterAmount | `cancel` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 19 | ValidatingAmount | `sufficientFunds` | balance $\geq$ a $\wedge$ cash $\geq$ a | `debit(a)` | Dispensing |
| 20 | ValidatingAmount | `insufficientFunds` | balance $<$ a | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 21 | ValidatingAmount | `cashEmpty` | ATMcash $<$ a | `ejectCard`, `displayMsg` | Idle |
| 22 | Dispensing | `cashDispensed` | none | `updateATMBalance` | PrintingReceipt |
| 23 | PrintingReceipt | `receiptPrinted` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 24 | BalanceEnquiry | `ejectCard` | none | `ejectCard` | Idle |
| 25 | Idle | `cashEmpty` | none | `disableWithdraw` | OutOfService |
| 26 | Idle | `networkDown` | none | `goOffline` | OutOfService |

This is a **26-row transition table** — a complete and unambiguous specification. In a CASE Lab record, this is the *input* to your UML tool.

---

### Step 5 — Identify the Initial and Final Pseudostates

> **Initial Pseudostate (●):** A single filled circle pointing to $S_1$ (`OutOfService`).
> **Final State (◎):** A bullseye reached only if the ATM is permanently decommissioned (not a withdrawal-flow terminal — `Idle` is the practical *quiescent* state).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Drawing the final state as the *Idle* state. **Idle is a normal state, not a final state.** Final state means *lifecycle termination*, not *inactive*. The ATM never reaches the final state in a withdrawal use case.

---

### Step 6 — Validate the Design (Mandatory Self-Check)

Run these three checks **before** submitting your answer:

1. **Reachability:** Every state must be reachable from the initial state via some path. (Verify all 13 states have $\geq 1$ incoming transition except initial.)
2. **Liveness:** Every state (except final) must have at least one outgoing transition for at least one event.
3. **Determinism:** For any (state, event) pair, at most **one** transition can be taken (use guards to disambiguate if needed).

---

### Step 7 — Render the Diagram

Proceed to **SECTION 4** for the visual rendering using standard CASE tool conventions.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Insight — Why the *Guard* is the Most-Marked Concept

The guard is the only **dynamic** element in the SCD that requires **runtime evaluation**. In KTU scripts, marks are split roughly as:

$$\text{SCD Marks} \;=\; 0.20\cdot(\text{States}) + 0.15\cdot(\text{Events}) + 0.20\cdot(\text{Arrows}) + 0.20\cdot(\text{Guards}) + 0.15\cdot(\text{Actions}) + 0.10\cdot(\text{Notation})$$

> **Implication:** A student who writes the *states and events* perfectly but omits the *guards* loses **20% of marks** on a 14-mark question — i.e., **2.8 marks** — for a single oversight.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used as standalone node names, and node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text separated by spaces — no inline markdown formatting.

### 4.1 Master Diagram — ATM Withdrawal State Chart (Rendered in Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OutOfService
    OutOfService --> Idle : serviceOK [cash greater than zero and netOK] / initialise
    Idle --> OutOfService : cashEmpty or networkDown / disableWithdraw
    Idle --> CardInserted : insertCard / readSlot
    CardInserted --> PINEntry : cardAccepted / readCard
    CardInserted --> Idle : invalidCard / ejectCard and logAttempt
    PINEntry --> Authenticating : enterPIN / sendToBank
    PINEntry --> Idle : cancel or timeout / ejectCard
    Authenticating --> Authenticated : validPIN / resetAttempts
    Authenticating --> Idle : invalidPIN [attempts less than three] / ejectCard and logFail
    Authenticating --> Blocked : invalidPIN [attempts equal to three] / retainCard and alertBank
    Blocked --> Idle : adminReset / ejectCard
    Authenticated --> TransactionSelection : selectTransaction / showMenu
    Authenticated --> Idle : timeout / ejectCard
    TransactionSelection --> EnterAmount : selectWithdraw / promptAmount
    TransactionSelection --> BalanceEnquiry : selectBalance / fetchBalance
    TransactionSelection --> Idle : cancel / ejectCard
    EnterAmount --> ValidatingAmount : chooseAmount [amount mod 100 equals 0] / sendForCheck
    EnterAmount --> Idle : cancel / ejectCard
    ValidatingAmount --> Dispensing : sufficientFunds [balance greater or equal amount and ATMcash greater or equal amount] / debit
    ValidatingAmount --> Idle : insufficientFunds or cashEmpty / ejectCard and displayMsg
    Dispensing --> PrintingReceipt : cashDispensed / updateATMBalance
    PrintingReceipt --> Idle : receiptPrinted / ejectCard
    BalanceEnquiry --> Idle : ejectCard / ejectCard
    Idle --> [*] : decommission
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each arrow carries a label of the form `Event [Guard] / Action`. The `[Guard]` part is in square brackets and `/Action` follows a forward slash — both are optional in UML but are **strongly recommended** in KTU answers.

---

### 4.2 Composite State — Expanding the `Authenticating` Region (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

To demonstrate **composite state modeling**, the `TransactionSelection` super-state is decomposed into sub-states:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TransactionSelection
    state TransactionSelection {
        [*] --> AwaitingChoice
        AwaitingChoice --> Withdraw : selectWithdraw
        AwaitingChoice --> Balance : selectBalance
        AwaitingChoice --> Transfer : selectTransfer
        Withdraw --> AwaitingChoice : cancel or back
        Balance --> AwaitingChoice : cancel or back
        Transfer --> AwaitingChoice : cancel or back
    }
    TransactionSelection --> Idle : ejectCard
```

---

### 4.3 Second Worked Example — User Login Session State Chart (Simpler Case for Practice)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LoggedOut
    LoggedOut --> EnteringCredentials : openLoginForm
    EnteringCredentials --> Validating : submitForm
    EnteringCredentials --> LoggedOut : cancel
    Validating --> LoggedIn : credentialsValid / createSession
    Validating --> EnteringCredentials : credentialsInvalid [attempts less than three] / incrementAttempts
    Validating --> Locked : credentialsInvalid [attempts equal to three] / lockAccount
    Locked --> LoggedOut : unlockRequest [approved by admin] / clearAttempts
    LoggedIn --> Idle : sessionEstablished
    Idle --> LoggedIn : userActivity [within timeout] / resetTimer
    Idle --> LoggedOut : timeout / destroySession
    LoggedIn --> LoggedOut : explicitLogout / destroySession
    LoggedOut --> [*] : terminateApp
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (For Topics Requiring Physical Drawings)

When a Mermaid state diagram is not sufficient (e.g., for an *elevator control system* spanning multiple objects), use this text-based **functional architecture mapping**:

```
+---------------------------+        events         +-----------------------+
|    EXTERNAL ENTITIES      | =====================>|      CONTROLLER       |
|                           |                       |     (Subject Object)  |
|  - Customer Buttons       |   pressFloor(N)       |                       |
|  - Floor Sensors          |   doorObstructed       |   +---------------+   |
|  - Door Sensors           |   overload             |   |   STATE       |   |
|  - Weight Sensor          |                       |   |   MACHINE     |   |
+---------------------------+                       |   +---------------+   |
                                                   |                       |
                                                   |   States:             |
                                                   |   - Idle              |
                                                   |   - MovingUp          |
                                                   |   - MovingDown        |
                                                   |   - DoorsOpening      |
                                                   |   - DoorsClosing      |
                                                   |   - Maintenance       |
                                                   |   - EmergencyStop     |
                                                   |                       |
                                                   |   Guards:             |
                                                   |   - overCapacity?     |
                                                   |   - doorObstructed?   |
                                                   |   - floorReached?     |
                                                   |                       |
                                                   +-----------+-----------+
                                                               |
                                                       actions |
                                                               v
                                                   +-----------------------+
                                                   |  ACTUATORS            |
                                                   |  - Motor              |
                                                   |  - Door Drive         |
                                                   |  - Alarm              |
                                                   |  - Display            |
                                                   +-----------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PCCNL607]**

**Q1.** Define a **State Chart Diagram**. List any **four** components used to construct it.
*(CO1, Remember-Level, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for components):**

> A **State Chart Diagram** is a UML 2.5 behavioral diagram that models the **dynamic lifecycle of a single object** by representing the **states** it occupies, the **events** that trigger transitions between them, the **actions** performed during transitions, and the **guard conditions** that must be satisfied for a transition to occur.
>
> Four essential components are:
> 1. **State** — a condition in which the object waits for events.
> 2. **Transition** — a directed arrow from one state to another, labeled with an event.
> 3. **Event / Trigger** — a stimulus that may cause a state change.
> 4. **Initial Pseudostate** — a filled circle marking the starting point of the object's lifecycle.
> *(Acceptable substitutes: Final State, Guard, Action, Activity, History State.)*

---

**Q2.** Differentiate between a **State Chart Diagram** and an **Activity Diagram** with respect to *focus, control flow, and trigger mechanism*. *(CO2, Understand-Level, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per row):**

| Aspect | State Chart Diagram | Activity Diagram |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Focus** | Object's internal *condition* (state) over time | Flow of *activities* / work steps in a process |
| **Control Flow Trigger** | External **events** (e.g., `insertCard`) | **Completion of previous action** |
| **Concurrent Behaviour** | Modeled via parallel regions inside a state | Modeled via fork/join nodes in the activity flow |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam — December 2024, PCCNL607]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — ATM System**

**(a)** *Identify all states, events, and guard conditions required to model the withdrawal use case of an ATM with PIN validation (max 3 attempts), balance check, cash dispensing, and receipt printing.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

States (list any **6** correctly — 1 mark per 2 states, max 3 marks):
1. **Idle** — awaiting card
2. **CardInserted** — card in slot, not yet validated
3. **PINEntry** — awaiting PIN
4. **Authenticating** — validating PIN with bank
5. **Blocked** — 3 failed attempts
6. **Authenticated** — PIN accepted
7. **EnterAmount** — awaiting amount
8. **ValidatingAmount** — balance check
9. **Dispensing** — cash out
10. **PrintingReceipt** — printing
11. **OutOfService** — cash empty or network down

Events (list any **6** — max 2 marks):
- `insertCard`, `enterPIN`, `validPIN`, `invalidPIN`, `chooseAmount`, `cashDispensed`, `receiptPrinted`, `ejectCard`, `adminReset`, `serviceOK`, `timeout`, `cancel`, `networkDown`, `cashEmpty`.

Guards (list any **2** — max 2 marks):
- `attempts < 3` (allow retry of PIN)
- `balance >= amount AND ATMcash >= amount` (allow dispensing)
- `amount mod 100 == 0` (denomination check)

**(b)** *Draw a complete State Chart Diagram for the above system. Label all transitions with `Event [Guard] / Action` format and clearly mark the initial and final states.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks — break-up: 2 for structure, 3 for transitions, 1 for guards, 1 for initial/final):**

> Render the diagram **as in SECTION 4.1** of these notes. The examiner awards marks for:
> - **+2 marks:** Correct placement of *initial* (filled circle) and *final* (bullseye) pseudostates; rounded rectangles for every state.
> - **+3 marks:** At least 8 correctly drawn directed transitions with event labels. (Use SECTION 3 transition table as the gold standard.)
> - **+1 mark:** At least 2 transition labels include **guard conditions in square brackets**.
> - **+1 mark:** At least 2 transition labels include **actions after a forward slash**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Online Order Processing System**

**(a)** *Identify all states and events required to model the lifecycle of an online order from placement to delivery. The system must handle payment failure, cancellation, and returns.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

States (list any **6** — max 3 marks):
1. **New** — order just placed
2. **PendingPayment** — awaiting payment confirmation
3. **PaymentFailed** — payment declined
4. **Paid** — payment confirmed
5. **Processing** — warehouse picking/packing
6. **Shipped** — handed to courier
7. **OutForDelivery** — courier en route
8. **Delivered** — package received
9. **Cancelled** — order cancelled before shipping
10. **ReturnRequested** — buyer initiated return
11. **Returned** — package received back at warehouse
12. **Refunded** — money returned to buyer

Events (list any **6** — max 2 marks):
- `placeOrder`, `paymentInitiated`, `paymentSuccess`, `paymentFailed`, `retryPayment`, `startProcessing`, `shipOrder`, `dispatchForDelivery`, `confirmDelivery`, `cancelOrder`, `returnRequest`, `returnReceived`, `refundProcessed`, `timeout`.

Guards (list any **2** — max 2 marks):
- `stockAvailable` (allow transition to Processing)
- `withinReturnWindow` (allow ReturnRequested)
- `paymentRetryCount < 3` (allow re-attempt)

**(b)** *Draw the complete State Chart Diagram. Mark all transitions, guards, and the initial/final states.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> PendingPayment : placeOrder / generateOrderID
    PendingPayment --> Paid : paymentSuccess / sendConfirmation
    PendingPayment --> PaymentFailed : paymentFailed / notifyCustomer
    PaymentFailed --> PendingPayment : retryPayment [retries less than three] / reattempt
    PaymentFailed --> Cancelled : paymentFailed [retries equal to three] / notifyCustomer
    Paid --> Processing : startProcessing [stockAvailable] / allocateStock
    Paid --> Cancelled : cancelOrder / initiateRefund
    Processing --> Shipped : shipOrder / generateTracking
    Shipped --> OutForDelivery : dispatchForDelivery / updateTracking
    OutForDelivery --> Delivered : confirmDelivery / closeOrder
    Delivered --> ReturnRequested : returnRequest [withinReturnWindow] / approveReturn
    Delivered --> [*] : afterReturnWindow / archiveOrder
    ReturnRequested --> Returned : returnReceived / inspectItem
    Returned --> Refunded : refundProcessed / creditAccount
    Refunded --> [*] : refundComplete / archiveOrder
    Cancelled --> Refunded : refundProcessed / creditAccount
    Refunded --> [*] : archiveOrder
```

**Mark Allocation for (b) — 7 marks:**
- **+2** for initial + final pseudostates and all 12 state rectangles drawn.
- **+3** for ≥ 10 correct directed transitions with event labels.
- **+1** for ≥ 2 transitions labelled with a **guard in square brackets**.
- **+1** for ≥ 2 transitions labelled with **actions after `/`**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `Idle` with the Final State** — *Idle is a state, not a final state.* Final state means the object is *destroyed/decommissioned* — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting the Guard Condition syntax** — writing just `validPIN` instead of `validPIN [attempts<3] / resetAttempts` — losing up to 1 mark per guard missing.
> 3. **Drawing the diagram in Paint/MS-Word without UML notation** — rounded rectangles, filled circles, and bullseye are *mandatory* — losing 0.5–1 mark for sloppy notation.
> 4. **Missing the `OutOfService` failure path** — every reactive system *must* show a degradation/failure state for KTU 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the `event` on the transition arrow** — every arrow without an event label is treated as an *unconditional transition* and loses 0.5 mark per arrow.
> 6. **Drawing concurrent regions without using `||` separator** — concurrent states must show the **fork-bar / join-bar** explicitly — losing 1 mark if absent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this box and stick it on your lab record cover page.**

- A **State Chart Diagram** models *one object's* lifecycle — not a whole system.
- The **5 elements** to remember forever: **State, Event, Guard, Action, Transition** (acronym: **SEGA T**).
- **Initial state** = filled circle (●). **Final state** = bullseye (◎). **These are NOT regular states.**
- Transition label format: `Event [Guard] / Action` — *all three are independent and optional*.
- **Guards** are Boolean expressions evaluated *instantaneously* when the event fires; they **do not consume time**.
- **Actions** are atomic and non-interruptible; **Activities** (`do:`) are long-running and may be interrupted.
- **Composite states** contain nested sub-states — use for grouping (e.g., `TransactionSelection` containing Withdraw / Balance / Transfer).
- **Concurrent regions** are separated by `||` inside a state — use when two orthogonal activities must run in parallel.
- **History state (H)** allows re-entry into the *last active* sub-state of a composite state on re-entry.
- Always include the **OutOfService / Failure / Cancellation** path — KTU 14-mark answers that omit it lose at least 1 mark.
- Always include the **Initial and Final pseudostates** — every SCD must have *exactly one* initial state.
- **Determinism rule:** for any (state, event) pair, at most one transition can fire — use guards to disambiguate.
- Common CASE tools that render SCD: **StarUML (free), Visual Paradigm (community), Enterprise Architect, Rational Rose, Lucidchart, draw.io (free)**.
- The KTU 14-mark answer **must include** the transition table (Step 4) *before* the diagram — this is what examiners use to cross-verify your diagram.
- **Reachability, Liveness, and Determinism** — the three correctness checks you must perform before submission.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw the component diagram for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw the component diagram for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Official UML 2.5 / OMG Definition:**
> A **Component Diagram** is a type of **UML Structure Diagram** that depicts the **organization, physical implementation, and software architecture** of a system. It models the **dependencies** between software components (such as files, libraries, executables, documents, databases) and the **interfaces** through which they communicate.

In the context of the **KTU PCCNL607 – CASE Lab (2024 Scheme)**, a Component Diagram is treated as the **third logical step** in the forward-engineering pipeline of an OOAD project:

1. **Use Case Diagram** → *Functional intent*
2. **Class Diagram** → *Static structural blueprint*
3. **Component Diagram** → *Implementation / packaging blueprint*
4. **Deployment Diagram** → *Physical hardware mapping*

It is the **bridge between the logical (Class) world and the physical (Deployment) world**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy 🍳

Imagine a **professional restaurant kitchen**:

| Kitchen Element | UML Component Equivalent | Mapping Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Head Chef (orchestrates cooking) | **Component** | Each role is a self-contained module |
| Order Ticket Slip | **Required Interface** (`socket`) | What a component *needs* to work |
| Served Dish | **Provided Interface** (`lollipop`) | What a component *delivers* |
| Waiter carrying the dish | **Connector / Assembly Link** | Solid line joining a socket → lollipop |
| Recipe Book | **Artifact (note)** | Optional implementation note |
| Order Dependency (Soup needs Stock) | **Dependency (`<<uses>>`)** | Dashed arrow |

When the head chef (component) **provides** a "Dinner" interface, the waiter (connector) **attaches** it to the customer's order request — exactly how a software **Authentication Component** provides a *login service* to the **UI Component** that *requires* it.

This intuition makes it instantly obvious why a component diagram is about **pluggable, replaceable, contract-based units**, not about runtime objects.

---

## 1.3 Purpose of a Component Diagram (Why we draw it)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Pillars of a Component Diagram — KTU Board Examiner's View:**
> 1. **Modularization** — shows the system as independently deployable units.
> 2. **Reusability** — illustrates which components can be reused across systems.
> 3. **Interface Management** — defines strict *provided* vs *required* contracts.
> 4. **Dependency Analysis** — exposes coupling; supports *refactoring* decisions.
> 5. **Build / Configuration Management** — guides the build scripts (e.g., `make`, `maven`, `ant`).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Components (KTU Standard Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **Rumbaugh, Jacobson & Booch (UML 2.0)**, components are categorized into **3 types**, which the KTU syllabus explicitly expects students to label:

| Type | Stereotype | Description | Real-World Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Component** | `<<executable>>`, `<<library>>`, `<<file>>` | The run-time artifacts that constitute an executable system | `.exe`, `.dll`, `.jar`, `.war`, `.so` |
| **Work-Product Component** | `<<document>>`, `<<source>>`, `<<script>>` | The source-level entities from which deployment components are built | `.java`, `.cpp`, `.sql`, `.html`, `.pdf` |
| **Execution Component** | `<<process>>` | A component created as a result of an executing application | Runtime process, thread, COM+ object |

---

## 1.5 Where the Component Diagram Sits in the 4+1 Architectural View Model

The **Kruchten 4+1 View Model** is a KTU-favored framework:

- **Logical View** → Class Diagram
- **Process View** → Activity / Sequence Diagram
- **Physical View** → **Component Diagram** ✅
- **Development View** → **Component Diagram** ✅
- **Use-Case View** → Use Case Diagram

Hence, the component diagram uniquely answers the question: **"How is the software physically organized into replaceable, version-able, and deployable modules?"**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Component — The 5 Notational Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every component diagram you draw for KTU evaluation **must** contain the following 5 notational ingredients. Omitting any one costs marks.

### Element 1 — The Component Node

A **rectangle** with **two small overlapping rectangles on the left edge** (resembling a "torn folder" or "filing card"). The component name is written **inside or below** the rectangle.

$$
\text{Component Name} = \text{Visible inside the main rectangle}
$$

Stereotypes such as `<<executable>>`, `<<library>>`, `<<database>>` appear in `« guillemets »` above the component name.

### Element 2 — Provided Interface (Lollipop Notation)

A **filled circle** (the "lollipop") connected to the component by a **solid line**. Semantically: *"This component offers this service to others."*

$$
\text{Provided Interface} \equiv \text{Lollipop (ball + stick)}
$$

### Element 3 — Required Interface (Socket Notation)

A **half-circle / cup** (the "socket") connected to the component by a **solid line**. Semantically: *"This component depends on this service from others."*

$$
\text{Required Interface} \equiv \text{Socket (cup + stick)}
$$

### Element 4 — Dependency (`<<uses>>`)

A **dashed arrow** (open arrowhead) from the *requiring* component to the *providing* component. Represents compile-time or run-time dependency.

$$
A \xrightarrow{<<uses>>} B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{“Component } A \text{ needs Component } B\text{”}
$$

### Element 5 — Assembly Connector (Realization Link)

A **solid line** between a **socket (required)** of one component and a **lollipop (provided)** of another. This is the **runtime binding** — the "wiring diagram" of the system.

$$
\text{RequiredInterface}_A \;\longrightarrow\!\!-\!\!\longrightarrow\; \text{ProvidedInterface}_B
$$

---

## 2.2 Complete Relationship Palette (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List and draw the relationships in a component diagram."* Memorize this table verbatim.

| # | Relationship | Notation (UML 2.5) | Direction | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dependency** | Dashed arrow `-->` | $A \rightarrow B$ | $A$ uses $B$ at compile/run time | One component calls methods of another |
| 2 | **Association** | Solid line with optional arrow | $A \leftrightarrow B$ | Structural relationship | A component holds a reference to another |
| 3 | **Realization / Implementation** | Dashed line + hollow triangle | $A \dashv\!\!\triangleright B$ | $A$ implements the interface $B$ | A class implements an interface |
| 4 | **Generalization** | Solid line + hollow triangle | $A \longrightarrow\!\!\triangleright B$ | $A$ inherits from $B$ | A specialized component inherits a base |
| 5 | **Assembly Connector** | Solid line socket→lollipop | Socket $\rightarrow$ Lollipop | Runtime wire-up | Linking required ↔ provided |
| 6 | **Delegation Connector** | Arrow from external → port | External $\rightarrow$ Port | Port delegates to internal part | Component has sub-components |

---

## 2.3 Ports — The Optional 6th Element

A **port** is a small **square** on the boundary of a component. It represents a *typed interaction point* through which the component exchanges messages with the outside world. Ports are **strongly recommended** in KTU 2024 evaluations because they show **encapsulation** — what flows in and out without exposing the internals.

$$
\text{Port} = \text{Square on boundary} = \text{typed interaction gate}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Although component diagrams are diagrammatic, KTU exams sometimes ask for *process metrics* derived from them. The table below lists the most common formulas.

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Form | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Components** | $N_C = \vert C \vert$ | Total count of components in diagram | Count |
