| 2 | **Number of Interfaces** | $N_I = N_{PI} + N_{RI}$ | Provided + Required Interfaces | Count |
| 3 | **Coupling (afferent)** | $Ca = \vert \text{components depending on } C \vert$ | Incoming dependencies (fan-in) | Count |
| 4 | **Coupling (efferent)** | $Ce = \vert \text{components } C \text{ depends on} \vert$ | Outgoing dependencies (fan-out) | Count |
| 5 | **Instability Metric (Martin)** | $I = \dfrac{Ce}{Ca + Ce}$ | Range $[0, 1]$; $1$ = highly unstable | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Cohesion (avg per component)** | $Cohesion = \dfrac{\text{Intra-links}}{\vert C \vert}$ | Higher = more focused component | Links/component |
| 7 | **Reusability Score** | $R = \dfrac{N_{PI}}{N_{PI} + N_{RI}}$ | Ratio of provided interfaces | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Dependency Density** | $\rho_d = \dfrac{N_{dep}}{N_C \times (N_C - 1)}$ | Edges per possible edge | Fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **|Pipe Safeguard Active|** — All absolute values are written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Algorithm to Draw Any Component Diagram (KTU-mandated process)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 CASE Lab manual prescribes this **7-step procedure**. Examiners award marks for *methodology* (3 marks) in addition to the *final diagram* (5 marks).

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify the system boundary** | Name the system (e.g., Online Shopping) |
| 2 | **Identify major software modules** from the SRS / Use Cases | List 6–10 components |
| 3 | **Classify each component** (Deployment / Work-product / Execution) | Apply `<<stereotype>>` |
| 4 | **Identify Interfaces** for each component (What does it provide? What does it need?) | Populate lollipops & sockets |
| 5 | **Identify Relationships** — dependency, generalization, realization, assembly | Draw connectors |
| 6 | **Add Ports** (if interaction is complex) | Place small squares on component edges |
| 7 | **Refine with Notes/Constraints** | Add `<<note>>` for clarification |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Component Diagram Application |
|---|---|
| **Enterprise Java (J2EE)** | Mapping `EJB-JAR`, `WAR`, `EAR` packaging |
| **Microservices / Cloud-Native** | Depicting Docker containers, Kubernetes pods |
| **Embedded Systems** | Showing firmware modules, HAL layers |
| **Mobile App Development** | Android `.apk` internal module structure |
| **DevOps / CI-CD** | Identifying build artifacts and their dependencies |
| **Game Development (Unity/Unreal)** | Script-component architecture |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Case Study — Online Shopping System

> [!NOTE]
> We will use the **Online Shopping System** as the canonical example. The same methodology applies to *Library Management*, *Hospital Management*, *Banking System*, *Student Registration*, etc. — all standard KTU experiments.

### Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary

**System Name:** *Online Shopping System (OSS)*
**Actors identified from Use Cases:** Customer, Admin, Payment Gateway, Inventory Database

### Step 2 — Identify the Major Software Components

From the SRS document, the following **6 deployment components** are derived:

| # | Component | Stereotype | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | `UserInterface` | `<<executable>>` | Web/Mobile UI |
| C2 | `AuthenticationService` | `<<library>>` | Login, JWT, OAuth |
| C3 | `OrderProcessing` | `<<library>>` | Cart, checkout logic |
| C4 | `PaymentGateway` | `<<external>>` | 3rd-party payment API |
| C5 | `InventoryDB` | `<<database>>` | Product stock |
| C6 | `NotificationService` | `<<library>>` | Email/SMS sender |

### Step 3 — Classify Each Component (Stereotype Assignment)

Each component is tagged with the appropriate stereotype as per UML 2.5:

- `UserInterface` → `<<executable>>` (runnable artifact)
- `AuthenticationService`, `OrderProcessing`, `NotificationService` → `<<library>>` (DLL/JAR)
- `PaymentGateway` → `<<external>>` (3rd-party, outside our control)
- `InventoryDB` → `<<database>>` (persistence store)

### Step 4 — Identify Interfaces (Provided vs Required)

| Component | Provided Interface (Lollipop) | Required Interface (Socket) |
|---|---|---|
| `UserInterface` | `BrowseProducts`, `PlaceOrder` | `LoginService`, `PaymentService` |
| `AuthenticationService` | `LoginService`, `RegisterService` | `UserDB` |
| `OrderProcessing` | `OrderService`, `CartService` | `InventoryService`, `PaymentService` |
| `PaymentGateway` | `PaymentService` | *(none — self-contained)* |
| `InventoryDB` | `InventoryService` | *(none)* |
| `NotificationService` | `EmailService`, `SMSService` | *(none)* |

### Step 5 — Identify Relationships

| From | To | Relationship | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `UserInterface` | `AuthenticationService` | Dependency `<<uses>>` | UI calls login |
| `UserInterface` | `OrderProcessing` | Dependency `<<uses>>` | UI calls order |
| `OrderProcessing` | `InventoryDB` | Dependency `<<uses>>` | Needs stock check |
| `OrderProcessing` | `PaymentGateway` | Dependency `<<uses>>` | Initiates payment |
| `OrderProcessing` | `NotificationService` | Dependency `<<uses>>` | Sends order confirmations |
| `AuthenticationService` | `UserDB` | (Implicit external storage) | Persistence dependency |

### Step 6 — Add Ports (Optional but recommended)

Add a port named `«HTTP»` on `UserInterface` and a port named `«REST»` on `OrderProcessing` to indicate protocol-level access.

### Step 7 — Add Notes/Constraints

Attach a note: *"PaymentGateway is an external component; SLA: 99.9% uptime."*

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Analysis — Coupling & Instability Computation

After drawing the diagram, KTU expects students to compute a few derived metrics. We illustrate the calculations for the OSS example.

### 3.2.1 Number of Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_C &= \vert C \vert \\
&= \vert \{\text{UserInterface}, \text{AuthenticationService}, \text{OrderProcessing}, \\
&\quad\;\; \text{PaymentGateway}, \text{InventoryDB}, \text{NotificationService}\} \vert \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Afferent Coupling ($C_a$) for `OrderProcessing`

Incoming dependencies (components that depend on `OrderProcessing`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a(\text{OrderProcessing}) &= \vert \{\text{UserInterface}\} \vert \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Efferent Coupling ($C_e$) for `OrderProcessing`

Outgoing dependencies (components that `OrderProcessing` depends on):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_e(\text{OrderProcessing}) &= \vert \{\text{InventoryDB}, \text{PaymentGateway}, \text{NotificationService}\} \vert \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Instability Metric ($I$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(\text{OrderProcessing}) &= \frac{C_e}{C_a + C_e} \\
&= \frac{3}{1 + 3} \\
&= \frac{3}{4} \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $I = 0.75$ means `OrderProcessing` is *moderately unstable* — it has many outgoing dependencies, so changes to `PaymentGateway` or `InventoryDB` will likely affect it. KTU suggests refactoring toward $I \rightarrow 0$ for **stable core components** and $I \rightarrow 1$ for **leaf/replaceable components**.

### 3.2.5 Dependency Density ($\rho_d$)

Total dependencies in the system = 5 (from Section 3.1, Step 5). Total possible edges = $N_C \times (N_C - 1) = 6 \times 5 = 30$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d &= \frac{N_{dep}}{N_C \times (N_C - 1)} \\
&= \frac{5}{30} \\
&= \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only $\approx 16.67\%$ of possible connections exist — the system is **loosely coupled**, which is desirable for maintainability.

### 3.2.6 Reusability Score for `AuthenticationService`

Provided Interfaces = 2 (`LoginService`, `RegisterService`); Required Interfaces = 1 (`UserDB`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(\text{AuthService}) &= \frac{N_{PI}}{N_{PI} + N_{RI}} \\
&= \frac{2}{2 + 1} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\approx 66.7\%$ reusability — fairly good; this component can plug into any system needing authentication.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Component Diagram vs. Other UML Diagrams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU question: *"How is a Component Diagram different from a Class Diagram / Deployment Diagram?"* The following matrix is a *guaranteed* scoring answer.

| Dimension | Class Diagram | Component Diagram | Deployment Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Static *logical* structure | Physical *packaging* of code | Physical *hardware* mapping |
| **Node** | Class | Component | Node (Device) |
| **Edge** | Association, Aggregation, etc. | Dependency, Interface | Communication Path |
| **Granularity** | Fine (per class) | Medium (per module/JAR) | Coarse (per server/process) |
| **Used in Phase** | Analysis & Design | Implementation & Build | Deployment & Operations |
| **Time of drawing** | Early SDLC | Mid-to-late SDLC | Late SDLC |
| **Audience** | Developers | Architects, DevOps | System Admins, Network Eng. |
| **Example Node** | `class Order` | `<<library>> OrderProcessing.jar` | `OrderServer: Tomcat Node` |

---

## 3.4 Logical Derivation — Why Lollipop for "Provided" and Socket for "Required"?

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often award a 1-mark bonus for the *mnemonic reasoning* behind the notation.

- **Lollipop (ball on a stick)**: The ball is **closed** → symbolizes that the interface is *closed* to modification (OOP **Open/Closed Principle**). It **provides** a fixed, complete contract outward.
- **Socket (cup)**: The cup is **open** → symbolizes that the component is *open* and *waiting* to receive an external implementation. It **requires** something to be plugged in.

Mnemonic: **"Ball gives, Cup takes."** 🎯

---

## 3.5 Validation Checklist (Self-Verification Before Submission)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by missing one of the items below.

- [ ] Component name is **inside** the rectangle, not outside.
- [ ] Stereotype is in **guillemets** `« »` (not double quotes).
- [ ] Lollipop is **filled** (●); socket is a **half-circle** (◗).
- [ ] Dependency arrows are **dashed** with an **open** arrowhead.
- [ ] Assembly connectors are **solid** lines, not dashed.
- [ ] Notes are attached with a **dashed line** (not solid).
- [ ] At least **one provided** and **one required** interface exist.
- [ ] All arrowheads are **open triangles**, not filled.
- [ ] No **runtime objects** appear — only **components and interfaces**.
- [ ] Legend is provided for non-standard stereotypes.

Each component is a node; interfaces and relationships are edge labels.

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define a UML Component Diagram. List any four notations used to draw it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Component Diagram is a UML structure diagram that depicts the **organization and dependencies** among a set of software components, focusing on the **physical packaging** of the system. *[1 mark]*

**Four notations:** *[2 marks — ½ mark each]*
1. **Component** — rectangle with two small overlapping rectangles on the left edge.
2. **Provided Interface (Lollipop)** — filled circle on a solid line, representing what a component offers.
3. **Required Interface (Socket)** — half-circle (cup) on a solid line, representing what a component needs.
4. **Dependency** — dashed arrow with open arrowhead, indicating `<<uses>>`.
5. *(Bonus)* **Port** — small square on the component boundary for typed interactions.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Provided Interface (Lollipop) and Required Interface (Socket) in UML Component Diagrams. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Provided Interface (Lollipop) | Required Interface (Socket) |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | Filled circle `((O))` on a solid line | Half-circle (cup) `(( ))` on a solid line |
| **Semantic** | Service *offered* by the component | Service *consumed* by the component |
| **Direction** | Outward-facing | Inward-facing |
| **Example** | `LoginService` *provided* by `AuthenticationService` | `LoginService` *required* by `UserInterface` |

*[1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for the example]*
