### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — Online Examination System

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Supposed Model]**

> **a)** Identify the **major software components** for an *Online Examination System* and classify them using appropriate UML stereotypes. *(7 marks)*
>
> **b)** Draw the **UML Component Diagram** showing all provided/required interfaces and dependencies. Compute the **Instability Metric** of the `ExamEngine` component. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Component Identification & Classification [7 marks]**

| # | Component | Stereotype | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `WebPortal` | `<<executable>>` | Student's browser interface |
| 2 | `AuthService` | `<<library>>` | Login / SSO |
| 3 | `ExamEngine` | `<<library>>` | Timer, question rendering, auto-save |
| 4 | `QuestionBank` | `<<database>>` | Stores MCQ/descriptive Q's |
| 5 | `ResultProcessor` | `<<library>>` | Auto-grading |
| 6 | `EmailService` | `<<library>>` | Result notification |
| 7 | `PaymentGateway` | `<<external>>` | Exam fee collection |

*(3 marks for identification; 2 marks for stereotypes; 2 marks for responsibilities)*

**(b) Component Diagram + Instability Computation [7 marks]**

**Diagram (Mermaid block-render):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    WP[<<executable>> WebPortal]
    AS[<<library>> AuthService]
    EE[<<library>> ExamEngine]
    QB[<<database>> QuestionBank]
    RP[<<library>> ResultProcessor]
    EM[<<library>> EmailService]
    PG[<<external>> PaymentGateway]

    WP -- "<<uses>> Login" --> AS
    WP -- "<<uses>> ExamSession" --> EE
    WP -- "<<uses>> Payment" --> PG
    EE -- "<<uses>> QuestionSvc" --> QB
    EE -- "<<uses>> GradeSvc" --> RP
    RP -- "<<uses>> EmailSvc" --> EM

    style WP fill:#ffe6cc
    style AS fill:#e6ccff
    style EE fill:#cce6ff
    style QB fill:#ccffcc
    style RP fill:#cce6ff
    style EM fill:#cce6ff
    style PG fill:#ffcccc
```

*(3 marks for the diagram — 1 mark for each of: components, interfaces/dependencies, legend)*

**Instability of `ExamEngine`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a(\text{ExamEngine}) &= \vert \{\text{WebPortal}\} \vert = 1 \\
C_e(\text{ExamEngine}) &= \vert \{\text{QuestionBank}, \text{ResultProcessor}\} \vert = 2 \\
I(\text{ExamEngine}) &= \frac{C_e}{C_a + C_e} = \frac{2}{1 + 2} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks — 1 mark for $C_a, C_e$ values, 1 mark for the formula and final value)*

**Interpretation:** $I = 0.667$ — `ExamEngine` is moderately unstable; tightly coupled to data and grading layers. Refactoring suggestion: introduce an *abstraction layer* between `ExamEngine` and `QuestionBank` to lower coupling.

*(2 marks for the interpretation and refactoring suggestion)*

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Library Management System

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> **a)** Draw the **UML Component Diagram** for a *Library Management System* with at least 6 components. Show **one example each** of a provided interface, a required interface, a dependency, and an assembly connector. *(7 marks)*
>
> **b)** Compare **Component Diagram** with **Deployment Diagram** on **any four** parameters. Why is the Component Diagram called the *bridge* between the logical and physical worlds? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Component Diagram for Library Management System [7 marks]**

**Components identified:**

| # | Component | Stereotype |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LibPortal` | `<<executable>>` |
| 2 | `CatalogService` | `<<library>>` |
| 3 | `IssueReturnService` | `<<library>>` |
| 4 | `MembershipService` | `<<library>>` |
| 5 | `BookDB` | `<<database>>` |
| 6 | `FineCalculator` | `<<library>>` |
| 7 | `Notification` | `<<library>>` |

**Interfaces & relationships:**

- **Provided Interface:** `CatalogService` *provides* `SearchBooks` (lollipop)
- **Required Interface:** `IssueReturnService` *requires* `MemberValidator` (socket from `MembershipService`)
- **Dependency:** `IssueReturnService` `--<<uses>>-->` `BookDB` (dashed arrow)
- **Assembly Connector:** `MemberValidator` (socket on `IssueReturnService`) → `ValidateMember` (lollipop on `MembershipService`)

*(2 marks for components; 2 marks for interfaces; 3 marks for the four relationship examples with correct notation)*

**ASCII Schematic:**

```
  LibPortal --<<uses>>--> CatalogService  --<<provides>>-- SearchBooks
        |                    |
        |                    | <<uses>>
        v                    v
  IssueReturnService --<<requires>>-- MemberValidator ===<<assembly>>=== ValidateMember (from MembershipService)
        |                                                                       ^
        | <<uses>>                                                              |
        v                                                                       |
       BookDB                                                                    |
                                                                                  |
  IssueReturnService --<<uses>>--> FineCalculator --<<uses>>--> Notification    |
```

*(Hand-drawn version on paper should clearly show the lollipop and socket symbols.)*

**(b) Comparison: Component Diagram vs. Deployment Diagram [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Component Diagram | Deployment Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Shows software *packaging* and module dependencies | Shows *physical hardware* and software-to-hardware mapping |
| **Primary Node** | Component | Node (Device/Server) |
| **Edges** | Interfaces, dependencies | Communication paths (TCP/IP, USB, etc.) |
| **Audience** | Architects, DevOps | System administrators, network engineers |
| **SDLC Phase** | Implementation & Build | Operations & Deployment |
| **Granularity** | Module/Library level | Server/Process level |

*(4 marks — 1 mark per row, 4 rows chosen)*

**Why Component Diagram is the "bridge":**

The Component Diagram is called the bridge because it sits **between the Class Diagram (logical view)** and the **Deployment Diagram (physical view)**. It translates:

- **Class-level abstractions** → into *executable* and *library* artifacts (`.jar`, `.dll`).
- **Module dependencies** → into *build-time* and *run-time* wiring that is *finally* realized when those components are deployed onto hardware nodes.

Without the Component Diagram, there is no clear mapping from *design* to *implementation*, and from *implementation* to *infrastructure*. Thus, it is the **architectural hinge** that connects the developer's mental model to the operations team's deployment plan.

*(3 marks for the bridge explanation — 1 mark for stating it connects Class and Deployment, 1 mark for translation logic, 1 mark for engineering utility)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Component Diagram questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Notation** — Drawing lollipops as hollow circles or sockets as full circles. Examiner deducts 1 mark per wrong symbol. **Always double-check your symbol dictionary.**
> 2. **Mixing Class and Component** — Including *classes* (e.g., `class Order`) instead of *components* (e.g., `<<library>> OrderProcessing`). Component Diagrams show *packaged artifacts*, not classes.
> 3. **Missing Stereotypes** — Drawing components without `«executable»`, `«library»`, etc. Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for correct stereotype usage.
> 4. **Confusing Dependency with Realization** — A dashed line with an open arrowhead is **dependency**; a dashed line with a hollow triangle is **realization**. Mixing them is a 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **No Legend** — Always include a small legend box explaining non-standard stereotypes. Examiners reward clarity.
> 6. **Forgetting Provided/Required Pairing** — A valid component diagram must show *at least one* lollipop connected to *at least one* socket via an assembly connector. Otherwise the diagram is "static" and incomplete.
> 7. **Wrong Direction of Arrow** — Dependency arrow should point from the *requirer* (dependent) to the *provider* (dependee). Reversing it loses 1 mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision bullet sheet — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Component Diagram = Implementation View** = "How is the system packaged?"
- ✅ Component symbol = **rectangle with two small rectangles on the left** (the "filing card").
- ✅ Three component types: **Deployment, Work-Product, Execution** components.
- ✅ Stereotypes in **guillemets** `«executable»`, `«library»`, `«database»`, `«external»`, `«document»`, `«file»`, `«process»`, `«source»`, `«script»`.
- ✅ **Lollipop `((O))` = Provided Interface** (ball gives); **Socket `(( ))` = Required Interface** (cup takes).
- ✅ **Dependency** = dashed arrow with **open arrowhead** (`--->`).
- ✅ **Realization** = dashed line with **hollow triangle** (`--▷`).
- ✅ **Generalization** = solid line with **hollow triangle** (`—▷`).
- ✅ **Assembly Connector** = solid line socket → lollipop.
- ✅ **Port** = small square on boundary; represents a typed interaction point.
- ✅ Mnemonic: **"Ball gives, Cup takes."** 🎯
- ✅ Drawing methodology = **7 steps** (Boundary → Components → Stereotypes → Interfaces → Relationships → Ports → Notes).
- ✅ **Instability metric** $I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e}$; range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Dependency density** $\rho_d = \dfrac{N_{dep}}{N_C \times (N_C - 1)}$.
- ✅ **Reusability score** $R = \dfrac{N_{PI}}{N_{PI} + N_{RI}}$.
- ✅ Component Diagram is the **bridge between Class Diagram and Deployment Diagram**.
- ✅ Always include a **Legend** for non-standard stereotypes.
- ✅ Always include **at least one assembly connector** (socket + lollipop pair) for a valid diagram.
- ✅ Examiner's pet-peeve: do **not** mix class diagram notations (private/public visibility, attributes) with component diagram notation.
- ✅ Common KTU case studies: *Online Shopping, Library Management, Hospital Management, Banking, Student Registration, Online Examination, E-Commerce, Payroll*.

---

> 🎓 **End of Notes — KTU PCCNL607 CASE LAB | Module Experiment 2 | Component Diagram**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstrate forward engineering in Java (Model to code conversion)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstrate forward engineering in Java (Model to code conversion)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Forward Engineering** in the context of **Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE)** is the process of transforming a higher-level abstract representation of a software system (typically a **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** model) into a lower-level, executable, and platform-specific implementation (such as **Java source code**). It is the *traditional* software development workflow that proceeds from **design → implementation**, in contrast to **Reverse Engineering** which moves from code → model.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCNL607 – CASE LAB**, forward engineering is demonstrated using CASE tools such as **StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, ArgoUML, or Umbrello**, where class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and state charts are converted into compilable Java code skeletons (and sometimes full CRUD implementations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize this):**
> *“Forward engineering is the process of generating source code from a design model using a CASE tool, thereby maintaining traceability and consistency between the design (UML) and the implementation (code) artifacts of a software system.”*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are an **architect** who draws a detailed **blueprint** of a house — showing rooms, doors, walls, and dimensions. Now imagine handing that blueprint to a **construction robot**. The robot reads your blueprint and **physically lays the bricks, installs the doors, and pours the concrete** exactly as drawn.

In this analogy:
- The **blueprint** = the **UML Class Diagram** (design model)
- The **construction robot** = the **CASE tool** (e.g., StarUML, VP)
- The **built house** = the **Java source code files** (`.java` files)
- The act of reading the blueprint and building = **Forward Engineering**

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Exam Tip:**
> Forward Engineering = *Model-to-Code* conversion. The word "Forward" indicates we are moving **forward** in the development lifecycle (from design to implementation).

### Standard CASE Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested)
- **Supported Diagrams for Code Generation:** Class Diagram, State Machine Diagram, Sequence Diagram (for interaction skeletons), Object Diagram.
- **Default Languages:** Java, C++, C#, PHP, Python, IDL, CORBA.
- **Generation Accuracy Metric:** Typically **95–100%** of structural elements (classes, attributes, methods, visibility, types) are generated; **method bodies** are usually left empty or with TODO comments for the developer to fill.
- **Round-trip Engineering:** A process combining forward + reverse engineering, ensuring the code and model stay synchronized.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Forward Engineering Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The end-to-end forward engineering workflow in a CASE tool consists of the following structured stages:

1. **Requirement Capture & Analysis**
   - Functional and non-functional requirements are gathered and documented.
   - Output: SRS document, use-case descriptions.

2. **Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)**
   - Identify candidate classes, responsibilities, and collaborations (CRC cards).
   - Identify relationships: *association, aggregation, composition, generalization, dependency, realization*.

3. **Object-Oriented Design (OOD) — UML Modeling**
   - Build a **Class Diagram**: classes, attributes (with types and visibility), operations (with parameters and return types).
   - Add **multiplicity** to associations, **stereotypes** (`<<interface>>`, `<<abstract>>`, `<<enumeration>>`).
   - Build **Sequence / State / Activity** diagrams for dynamic behavior.

4. **Model Validation**
   - Verify the model is syntactically and semantically correct in the CASE tool.
   - Check for orphan classes, missing multiplicities, unresolved generalizations.

5. **Code Generation (Forward Engineering Core Step)**
   - The CASE tool traverses the XMI (XML Metadata Interchange) model representation.
   - It applies a **template/codegen engine** (e.g., Acceleo, MOFScript, JET) to emit code.
   - For Java, the tool emits `.java` files for each class, including:
     - Package declaration
     - Imports
     - Class header (with visibility, abstract/final modifier, inheritance, interfaces)
     - Attribute declarations (with visibility, type, static/final)
     - Method signatures (with visibility, return type, parameters, throws)
     - Empty method bodies (or default `// TODO` stubs)
     - `getXxx()` and `setXxx()` accessors if requested

6. **Code Compilation & Refinement**
   - Generated skeleton is filled with business logic by developers.
   - Compilation yields a runnable artifact (`.class` / `.jar`).

7. **Testing & Deployment**
   - Unit, integration, and system testing are performed on the implemented system.

### Why Forward Engineering Matters (The 'Why' Behind the 'How')

| Benefit | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Traceability** | Every code line maps back to a model element — required for safety-critical systems (DO-178C, ISO 26262). |
| **Consistency** | Eliminates the *model–code drift* problem. |
| **Productivity** | Automates 30–60% of boilerplate code generation. |
| **Reusability** | One model can be code-generated in multiple target languages (Java, C#, Python). |
| **Maintainability** | Refactoring at the model level regenerates the code; no manual edits needed. |

### The "Why and How" of UML → Java Translation Rules

| UML Element | Java Translation |
|---|---|
| `+ publicAttribute: int` | `public int publicAttribute;` |
| `- privateAttribute: String` | `private String privateAttribute;` |
| `# protectedAttribute: double` | `protected double protectedAttribute;` |
| `+ methodName(arg1: Type): ReturnType` | `public ReturnType methodName(Type arg1) { }` |
| Class A **inherits from** Class B (generalization) | `public class A extends B { }` |
| Class A **implements** Interface I (realization) | `public class A implements I { }` |
| **Aggregation** (hollow diamond) | Field reference, no ownership: `private B b;` |
| **Composition** (filled diamond) | Field reference with ownership, usually initialized in constructor: `private B b = new B();` |
| **Association** (1..* multiplicity) | `private List<B> bList = new ArrayList<>();` or single `B b;` |
| `<<interface>>` stereotype | `public interface InterfaceName { }` |
| `<<abstract>>` stereotype | `public abstract class ClassName { }` |
| `<<enum>>` stereotype | `public enum EnumName { VALUE1, VALUE2; }` |
| **Static** attribute (underlined in UML) | `public static final int CONSTANT = 10;` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The examiner often asks *"How is a UML composition represented in the generated Java code?"* — The expected answer is: *a strong lifecycle dependency where the child object is instantiated inside the parent's constructor and is destroyed when the parent is destroyed.*

### Formula Sheet / Mapping Cheat-Table (KTU Board Pattern)

| UML Notation | Java Code Generated | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Class Name (UML) | `public class ClassName` | 2 |
| Attribute with visibility | `visibility type name;` | 2 |
| Method signature | `visibility returnType name(params) {}` | 2 |
| Generalization (extends) | `extends ParentClass` | 1 |
| Realization (implements) | `implements InterfaceName` | 1 |
| 1-to-1 Association | Single object reference | 1 |
| 1-to-many Association | `ArrayList<>` or `List<>` collection | 1 |
| Aggregation | Weak reference, no constructor injection | 1 |
| Composition | Strong reference, constructor injection | 1 |
| Abstract class | `abstract class` keyword | 1 |
| Interface | `interface` keyword (no class) | 1 |
| Static member (underlined) | `static` keyword | 1 |
| Final class (italicized in VP) | `final` keyword | 1 |
| `<<enumeration>>` | `enum` keyword | 1 |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — UML Class Diagram → Java Code (Full Derivation)

Consider the following **UML Class Diagram** for a simple *Library Management System*:

```
+---------------------+          +-----------------------+
|      <<class>>      |          |     <<interface>>     |
|       Library       |          |     IBorrowable       |
+---------------------+          +-----------------------+
| - name: String      |          | + borrow(): void      |
| - books: List<Book> |◇---------| + returnBook(): void  |
+---------------------+ 1    *   +-----------------------+
| + Library(name)     |                  △
| + addBook(b: Book)  |                  |
| + getName(): String |                  |
+---------------------+                  |
                |                       |
                | 1                      |
                |                       |
                | composes               |
                ▼                       |
+---------------------+        +-----------------------+
|     <<class>>       |        |     <<abstract>>      |
|        Book         |◇------ |     LibraryItem       |
+---------------------+        +-----------------------+
| - title: String     |        | - itemId: int         |
| - author: String    |        | + getItemId(): int    |
| - isbn: String      |        | + displayInfo(): void |
+---------------------+        +-----------------------+
| + Book(t, a, i)     |                  △
| + displayInfo()     |                  |
+---------------------+                  |
                                        |
                          +-------------+
                          |
                  +-----------------------+
                  |     <<class>>         |
                  |       Journal         |
                  +-----------------------+
                  | - issueNumber: int    |
                  | - publisher: String   |
                  +-----------------------+
                  | + Journal(i, p)       |
                  | + displayInfo()       |
                  +-----------------------+
```

### Step-by-Step Java Code Generation

Now we derive the **Java code** for each class, applying the UML-to-Java mapping rules systematically.

**Step 1 — `IBorrowable` Interface (from `<<interface>>` stereotype)**
Since the interface is realized (implemented) by `Library`, the tool generates:

```java
public interface IBorrowable {
    void borrow();
    void returnBook();
}
```

**Derivation Logic:** The `<<interface>>` keyword in UML maps to the `interface` keyword in Java. Methods of an interface are implicitly `public abstract`, so the tool omits these modifiers per Java coding standards.

**Step 2 — `LibraryItem` Abstract Class (from `<<abstract>>` stereotype)**

```java
public abstract class LibraryItem {
    private int itemId;

    public int getItemId() {
        return itemId;
    }

    public abstract void displayInfo();
}
```

**Derivation Logic:** The `<<abstract>>` stereotype produces the `abstract` modifier. The abstract method `displayInfo()` is generated without a body. The `private` visibility on `itemId` produces a private field plus a public getter (since private fields cannot be accessed directly from outside).

**Step 3 — `Book` Class (concrete subclass of `LibraryItem`, with `implements IBorrowable` realization)**

```java
public class Book extends LibraryItem implements IBorrowable {
    private String title;
    private String author;
    private String isbn;

    public Book(String title, String author, String isbn) {
        this.title = title;
        this.author = author;
        this.isbn = isbn;
    }

    @Override
    public void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Book: " + title + " by " + author + " (ISBN: " + isbn + ")");
    }

    @Override
    public void borrow() {
        System.out.println("Book '" + title + "' has been borrowed.");
    }

    @Override
    public void returnBook() {
        System.out.println("Book '" + title + "' has been returned.");
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — `Journal` Class (concrete subclass of `LibraryItem`)**

```java
public class Journal extends LibraryItem {
    private int issueNumber;
    private String publisher;

    public Journal(int issueNumber, String publisher) {
        this.issueNumber = issueNumber;
        this.publisher = publisher;
    }

    @Override
    public void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Journal Issue: " + issueNumber + ", Publisher: " + publisher);
    }
}
```

**Step 5 — `Library` Class (composition relationship with `Book`, realizes `IBorrowable`)**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class Library implements IBorrowable {
    private String name;
    private List<Book> books;

    public Library(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        this.books = new ArrayList<>();
    }

    public void addBook(Book book) {
        books.add(book);
    }

    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }

    @Override
    public void borrow() {
        System.out.println("Borrowing from library: " + name);
    }

    @Override
    public void returnBook() {
        System.out.println("Returning to library: " + name);
    }
}
```

**Step 6 — Driver Class (typically hand-written, not generated)**

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Library lib = new Library("City Library");
        Book b1 = new Book("Effective Java", "Joshua Bloch", "978-0134685991");
        Journal j1 = new Journal(42, "ACM");

        lib.addBook(b1);
        b1.displayInfo();
        j1.displayInfo();
        b1.borrow();
        b1.returnBook();
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — UML Notation → Java Code

Let us formally express the mapping equation. For a UML class element $C$ with:

$$
C = \langle \text{name}, \text{visibility}, \text{stereotypes}, \text{attributes}, \text{operations}, \text{relations} \rangle
$$

The CASE tool's code generator $G$ transforms it into a Java compilation unit $J$:

$$
G(C) = J = \text{package} \cup \text{imports} \cup \text{classHeader}(C) \cup \text{fieldDecl}(C.\text{attributes}) \cup \text{methodDecl}(C.\text{operations})
$$

Where:

$$
\text{classHeader}(C) = \begin{cases}
\texttt{public class name extends Parent} & \text{if simple inheritance} \\
\texttt{public abstract class name extends Parent} & \text{if } \texttt{<<abstract>>} \\
\texttt{public class name extends Parent implements I1, I2} & \text{if realization present} \\
\texttt{public interface name} & \text{if } \texttt{<<interface>>} \\
\texttt{public final class name} & \text{if } \texttt{<<final>>}
\end{cases}
$$

And the **multiplicity-to-collection** transformation:

$$
\text{fieldType}(rel) = \begin{cases}
\texttt{T} & \text{if } multiplicity = 1 \text{ or } 0..1 \\
\texttt{List<T>} & \text{if } multiplicity = * \text{ or } 0..* \text{ or } 1..*
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Step:** The generated code must compile. A common KTU practical exam mistake is forgetting the `import java.util.List;` and `import java.util.ArrayList;` statements when collections are used — the CASE tool usually adds these automatically, but students editing the generated code often delete them.

### 3.3 StarUML — Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Standard)

The following is the **canonical procedure** for demonstrating forward engineering using **StarUML** (the most commonly mandated CASE tool in KTU labs):

1. **Launch StarUML** → Click **File → New Project** → Select **Empty Project**.
2. **Add a Class Diagram**: In the Model Explorer, right-click *Model* → **Add Diagram → Class Diagram**.
3. **Add Classes**: Drag the **Class** icon from the toolbox onto the canvas. Rename to `Book`, `Library`, `Journal`, etc.
4. **Add Attributes**: Select the class → in the Properties panel, click *Attribute ( + )* → set `Visibility = private`, `Type = String`, `Name = title`.
5. **Add Operations**: Click *Operation ( + )* → set `Visibility = public`, `Name = displayInfo`, `ReturnType = void`.
6. **Add Relationships**: Use the **Directed Association**, **Aggregation**, **Composition**, **Generalization**, or **Realization** arrows from the toolbox.
7. **Set Stereotypes**: Right-click the class → *Stereotype* → select from `<<abstract>>`, `<<interface>>`, `<<enumeration>>`, etc.
8. **Validate the Model**: Tools → **Check Model** (or `Ctrl + Shift + V`). Fix any errors.
9. **Generate Java Code**:
   - Menu → **Tools → Java → Generate Code…**
   - Select output folder (e.g., `C:\KtuLab\LibrarySystem`).
   - StarUML creates a `package-info.java` and one `.java` file per class.
10. **Inspect Generated Files** in File Explorer / IDE (Eclipse / IntelliJ / NetBeans).
11. **Compile**:
    ```bash
    javac *.java
    java Main
    ```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Viva Favorite:** *"What is the file extension of the StarUML project file?"* — Answer: **`.uml`** (it is XML-based XMI internally).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Forward Engineering Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **end-to-end forward engineering pipeline** from requirements to executable code:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Software Requirements SRS] --> B[Object Oriented Analysis OOA]
    B --> C[Identify Classes Attributes Operations]
    C --> D[Object Oriented Design OOD]
    D --> E[Build UML Class Diagram]
    E --> F[Add Relationships Generalization Aggregation Composition]
    F --> G[Apply Stereotypes Abstract Interface Enumeration]
    G --> H[Validate Model in CASE Tool]
    H --> I{Model Valid?}
    I -- No --> J[Fix Errors and Re validate]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Forward Engineering Code Generation]
    K --> L[Generate Java Source Files dot java]
    L --> M[Developer Fills Method Bodies]
    M --> N[Compile with javac]
    N --> O[Run and Test Application]
    O --> P[End Deliverable dot jar]
```

### 4.2 UML-to-Java Translation Architecture Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a CASE tool's code generation engine and how it maps UML model elements to Java constructs:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[UML Class Diagram] --> A2[XMI Model XML Tree]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Engine]
        A2 --> B1[Model Parser]
        B1 --> B2[Semantic Analyzer]
        B2 --> B3[Template Engine Acceleo JET MOFScript]
        B3 --> B4[Java Code Emitter]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Artifacts]
        B4 --> C1[dot java file per class]
        B4 --> C2[Package info dot java]
        B4 --> C3[Import Statements]
        B4 --> C4[Method Stubs]
    end

    subgraph POSTGEN[Post Generation]
        C1 --> D1[Developer Adds Business Logic]
        D1 --> D2[Compile and Test]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mapping Logic Schematic — UML Element vs Java Construct

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[UML Class with plus public] --> J1[public class]
    U2[UML Class with abstract stereotype] --> J2[abstract class]
    U3[UML Interface with interface stereotype] --> J3[interface]
    U4[UML Attribute with minus private] --> J4[private field with getter setter]
    U5[UML Operation with plus public] --> J5[public method with empty body]
    U6[UML Generalization arrow] --> J6[extends keyword]
    U7[UML Realization dashed arrow] --> J7[implements keyword]
    U8[UML Association 1 to star] --> J8[List of referenced type]
    U9[UML Composition filled diamond] --> J9[Constructor injected strong reference]
    U10[UML Aggregation hollow diamond] --> J10[Weak reference not owned]
    U11[UML Enumeration stereotype] --> J11[enum keyword]
    U12[UML Static Attribute underlined] --> J12[static keyword]
```

### 4.4 Forward Engineering vs Reverse Engineering — Comparative Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FE[Forward Engineering]
        F1[UML Design Model] --> F2[CASE Tool]
        F2 --> F3[Java Source Code]
        F3 --> F4[Executable Application]
    end

    subgraph RE[Reverse Engineering]
        R1[Existing Source Code] --> R2[CASE Tool Scanner]
        R2 --> R3[Recovered UML Model]
    end

    subgraph RT[Round Trip Engineering]
        RT1[Model] <--> RT2[Code Continuous Sync]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** When you take a printout of the generated Java code for your lab record, always **underline the elements that were auto-generated** (e.g., class header, fields, method signatures) and **circle the elements you hand-coded** (method bodies, business logic). Examiners award bonus marks for this kind of evidence.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **forward engineering** in the context of CASE tools. Mention any two CASE tools that support forward engineering to Java.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Forward engineering is the process of automatically generating source code (such as Java) from a higher-level design model (typically a UML class diagram) using a Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE) tool. The tool traverses the model and emits compilable code skeletons that the developer later refines with business logic.

Two CASE tools that support forward engineering to Java are:
1. **StarUML** (open-source, widely used in academic labs).
2. **Rational Rose** (IBM, enterprise-grade).

*(Valid alternatives accepted: Visual Paradigm, ArgoUML, Umbrello, Eclipse UML2 + Acceleo, Enterprise Architect.)*

*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark per tool name, 1 mark for any one correct supporting detail.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List the **main UML elements** that are translated into Java code during forward engineering. Briefly explain the translation of **aggregation** vs **composition**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Main UML elements translated: classes, attributes, operations, generalizations, realizations, associations, dependencies, multiplicities, and stereotypes.

- **Aggregation (hollow diamond)** → translated as a *weak reference*; the contained object can exist independently of the container. In Java: `private Department dept;` — the `Department` object is created elsewhere and passed in.
- **Composition (filled diamond)** → translated as a *strong reference with ownership*; the contained object's lifecycle is tied to the container. In Java: `private Department dept = new Department();` — typically initialized in the constructor of the containing class.

*(Award 1 mark for UML elements list, 1 mark for aggregation translation, 1 mark for composition translation.)*

---

### **PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Draw the UML class diagram for an **Online Course Management System** with the following classes: `Course`, `Instructor`, `Student`, and `Module`. Include appropriate attributes, methods, and relationships (generalization, association, aggregation).
**(b)** Demonstrate the **forward engineering process** by showing the Java code generated for the `Course` and `Module` classes. State the CASE tool used and explain the steps followed.

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — UML Class Diagram (7 marks):**

```
+---------------------------+         +----------------------------+
|       <<class>>           |         |       <<class>>            |
|       Instructor          |         |         Module             |
+---------------------------+         +----------------------------+
| - instructorId: int       |         | - moduleId: int            |
| - name: String            |         | - title: String            |
| - email: String           |         | - durationHours: int       |
+---------------------------+         +----------------------------+
| + Instructor(id, n, e)    |         | + Module(id, t, d)         |
| + assignGrade(s, g): void |         | + getDuration(): int       |
| + getName(): String       |         | + displayModule(): void    |
+---------------------------+         +----------------------------+
        | 1                                       ▲
        | teaches                                 | contains
        |                                         |
        | 1..*                          +-------------------------+
        ▼                               |       <<class>>         |
+---------------------------+   *  ◇    |         Course          |
|       <<class>>           |◇---------+-------------------------+
|         Course            |          | - courseId: int          |
+---------------------------+          | - courseName: String     |
| - courseId: int           |          | - credits: int           |
| - courseName: String      |          | - modules: List<Module>  |
| - credits: int            |          | - instructor: Instructor|
+---------------------------+          +-------------------------+
| + Course(id, n, c)        |          | + Course(id, n, c)       |
| + addModule(m: Module)    |          | + addModule(m: Module)   |
| + assignInstructor(i): void          | + assignInstructor(i): void
| + displayCourse(): void   |          | + displayCourse(): void  |
+---------------------------+          +-------------------------+
              ▲
              | enrolls
              |
              | 0..*
+---------------------------+
|       <<class>>           |
|        Student            |
+---------------------------+
| - studentId: int          |
| - name: String            |
| - enrolledCourses: List<Course>
+---------------------------+
| + Student(id, n)          |
| + enroll(c: Course): void |
| + listCourses(): void     |
+---------------------------+
```

*Key Relationships:*
- `Course` **composes** `Module` (filled diamond, 1 to *).
- `Course` **aggregates** `Instructor` (hollow diamond, 1 to 1, here shown 1..* for multi-instructor flexibility).
- `Student` **associates** with `Course` (many-to-many, simplified as 0..*).

*(Award 1 mark for each class header with attributes, 1 mark for methods, 1 mark for relationships, 1 mark for multiplicities, 1 mark for proper notation, 1 mark for composition, 1 mark for aggregation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Forward Engineering Java Code (7 marks):**

**CASE Tool Used:** StarUML (open-source, KTU-recommended).

**Steps Followed:**
1. Created an empty StarUML project, added a Class Diagram.
2. Dragged four Class shapes; named them `Course`, `Module`, `Instructor`, `Student`.
3. Added private attributes and public operations via the property inspector.
4. Drew a Composition arrow from `Course` → `Module` and an Aggregation arrow from `Course` → `Instructor`.
5. Added an Association from `Student` → `Course`.
6. Validated the model: *Tools → Check Model* (Ctrl+Shift+V).
7. Generated code: *Tools → Java → Generate Code* → selected output folder.

**Generated Java Code for `Module.java`:**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Module {
    private int moduleId;
    private String title;
    private int durationHours;

    public Module(int moduleId, String title, int durationHours) {
        this.moduleId = moduleId;
        this.title = title;
        this.durationHours = durationHours;
    }

    public int getDuration() {
        return durationHours;
    }

    public void displayModule() {
        System.out.println("Module ID: " + moduleId + ", Title: " + title);
    }
}
```

**Generated Java Code for `Course.java`:**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Course {
    private int courseId;
    private String courseName;
    private int credits;
    private List<Module> modules;
    private Instructor instructor;

    public Course(int courseId, String courseName, int credits) {
        this.courseId = courseId;
        this.courseName = courseName;
        this.credits = credits;
        this.modules = new ArrayList<>();
    }

    public void addModule(Module module) {
        modules.add(module);
    }

    public void assignInstructor(Instructor instructor) {
        this.instructor = instructor;
    }

    public void displayCourse() {
        System.out.println("Course: " + courseName + " (" + credits + " credits)");
        for (Module m : modules) {
            m.displayModule();
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Generation Logic:**
- The 1-to-* multiplicity between `Course` and `Module` produced `private List<Module> modules;` plus an `import java.util.*;` directive.
- The composition relationship (filled diamond) caused the `modules` list to be **initialized inside the constructor** (`this.modules = new ArrayList<>();`), reflecting the strong lifecycle dependency.
- The aggregation relationship (hollow diamond) produced a `private Instructor instructor;` reference that is **not auto-initialized**; it is set via the `assignInstructor()` method.
- Private visibility produced private fields with public getters; the constructor and methods were generated with empty method bodies (later filled with business logic).

*(Award 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for Module.java code, 2 marks for Course.java code, 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**(a)** Explain the **difference between forward engineering and reverse engineering** with a neat diagram. List any four CASE tools used for forward engineering.
**(b)** Consider a **Bank Account Management System** with classes `Bank`, `Account` (abstract), `SavingsAccount`, and `CurrentAccount`. Draw the UML class diagram and demonstrate forward engineering by generating the Java code for `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount`.

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Forward vs Reverse Engineering (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Engineering | Reverse Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Model → Code | Code → Model |
| Purpose | Implementation of design | Recovery of design from legacy code |
| Input | UML Class Diagram | Existing source code |
| Output | Compilable source code | Recovered UML model |
| Tools | StarUML, Rational Rose, VP | Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, SourceInsight |
| Use case | New development | Maintenance, documentation, modernization |

**Diagram:**

```
   FORWARD ENGINEERING (Top-Down)         REVERSE ENGINEERING (Bottom-Up)
  ┌─────────────────────┐                ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  UML Design Model   │                │  Existing Source Code│
  └──────────┬──────────┘                └───────────┬──────────┘
             │  G(C) = J (Code Gen)                    │  Parse + Recover
             ▼                                         ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐                ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  Java Source Code   │                │  Recovered UML Model │
  └─────────────────────┘                └──────────────────────┘
```

**Four CASE tools for forward engineering:**
1. **StarUML**
2. **Rational Rose** (IBM)
3. **Visual Paradigm**
4. **ArgoUML** / **Enterprise Architect** (any one)

*(Award 2 marks for tabular comparison, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for tools, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Bank Account System (7 marks):**

**UML Class Diagram (in textual form):**

```
        +-------------------------+
        |    <<abstract>>         |
        |       Account           |
        +-------------------------+
        | - accountNo: int        |
        | - holderName: String    |
        | - balance: double       |
        +-------------------------+
        | + Account(no, n, b)     |
        | + deposit(amt): void    |
        | + withdraw(amt): void   |
        | + display(): void       |
        +-------------------------+
                  △
            ┌─────┴─────┐
            │           │
   +------------------+  +----------------------+
   |    <<class>>     |  |     <<class>>        |
   | SavingsAccount   |  |  CurrentAccount      |
   +------------------+  +----------------------+
   | - interestRate: double  | - overdraftLimit: double |
   +------------------+  +----------------------+
   | + SavingsAccount(...)  | + CurrentAccount(...)     |
   | + calculateInterest():double | + withdraw(amt): void |
   | + display()            | + display()                |
   +------------------+  +----------------------+

   +-------------------------+
   |     <<class>>           |
   |        Bank             |
   +-------------------------+
   | - bankName: String      |
   | - accounts: List<Account> ◇-- (composition 1 to *)
   +-------------------------+
   | + Bank(name)            |
   | + openAccount(a): void  |
   | + listAccounts(): void  |
   +-------------------------+
```

**Generated Java Code for `SavingsAccount.java`:**

```java
public class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate;

    public SavingsAccount(int accountNo, String holderName, double balance, double interestRate) {
        super(accountNo, holderName, balance);
        this.interestRate = interestRate;
    }

    public double calculateInterest() {
        return getBalance() * interestRate / 100.0;
    }

    @Override
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Savings Account #" + getAccountNo() + ", Holder: " + getHolderName()
                           + ", Balance: " + getBalance() + ", Interest Rate: " + interestRate + "%");
    }
}
```

**Generated Java Code for `CurrentAccount.java`:**

```java
public class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    private double overdraftLimit;

    public CurrentAccount(int accountNo, String holderName, double balance, double overdraftLimit) {
        super(accountNo, holderName, balance);
        this.overdraftLimit = overdraftLimit;
    }

    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (getBalance() + overdraftLimit >= amount) {
            // perform withdrawal logic
        } else {
            System.out.println("Overdraft limit exceeded.");
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Current Account #" + getAccountNo() + ", Holder: " + getHolderName()
                           + ", Balance: " + getBalance() + ", Overdraft Limit: " + overdraftLimit);
    }
}
```

**Derivation Highlights:**
- `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` extend `Account` (generalization) → tool emits `extends Account`.
- `Account` has the `<<abstract>>` stereotype → tool emits `public abstract class Account`.
- Private fields in `Account` are accessed via getters (`getAccountNo()`, `getHolderName()`, `getBalance()`) generated by the tool.
- The `@Override` annotation is added by the tool to indicate polymorphic overriding of `display()` and `withdraw()`.

*(Award 2 marks for UML diagram, 2 marks for SavingsAccount code, 2 marks for CurrentAccount code, 1 mark for derivation explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `<<stereotype>>` markers** (especially `<<abstract>>` and `<<interface>>`) in the UML diagram — loses 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing aggregation and composition** in the diagram: *hollow diamond = aggregation (weak), filled diamond = composition (strong with constructor injection)*. Reversing these loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Not specifying visibility** (`+`, `-`, `#`) on attributes/operations in the UML class diagram — examiners deduct 1 mark per unspecified visibility.
> 4. **Forgetting imports** (`java.util.List`, `java.util.ArrayList`) in the generated Java code when collections are used.
> 5. **Showing only the class header and not the method signatures** in the model-to-code mapping — KTU expects at least one full method body in the answer.
> 6. **Writing Python/C++ code** when the question specifically asks for **Java** — automatic 0 for the code portion. Always match the target language stated in the question.
> 7. **Omitting multiplicities** on association ends (e.g., writing `Student ——— Course` without `1` or `*`) — loses 1 mark.
> 8. **Not mentioning the CASE tool name** explicitly in the procedure — examiners expect StarUML / Rational Rose / Visual Paradigm by name.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Forward Engineering = Model-to-Code** (Design → Implementation). It is the *top-down* approach.
- ✅ The **CASE tool** is the engine: StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, ArgoUML, Enterprise Architect.
- ✅ The **input** is a UML Class Diagram (and optionally Sequence/State diagrams for behavior).
- ✅ The **output** is one `.java` file per class, containing package, imports, class header, fields, constructors, getters/setters, and method stubs.
- ✅ **Generalization** → `extends`; **Realization** → `implements`; **Interface** → `interface` keyword.
- ✅ **Aggregation** → weak reference, no constructor injection (hollow diamond).
- ✅ **Composition** → strong reference, **constructor-injected** (filled diamond) — the contained object's lifecycle is tied to the container.
- ✅ **1-to-many association** → `List<T>` (with `import java.util.*;` or `import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.List;`).
- ✅ **Private attributes** → generated with `private` modifier and public `getXxx()` / `setXxx()` accessors.
- ✅ **Method bodies** are *left empty* by the generator; the developer fills them with business logic.
- ✅ **Abstract classes** → `abstract class` keyword; methods with no body → abstract methods.
- ✅ **Static members** (underlined in UML) → `static` keyword; final classes → `final` keyword.
- ✅ **Round-trip Engineering** = Forward + Reverse, keeping model and code in sync.
- ✅ **The procedure** in KTU labs: Model → Validate → Tools → Java → Generate Code → Compile → Run.
- ✅ **Always mention** the CASE tool name, the output folder, and the *sequence* of steps followed.
- ✅ **StarUML project files** have the `.uml` extension (XML/XMI-based internally).
- ✅ Forward engineering ensures **traceability**, **consistency**, and **reduced boilerplate** coding effort.
- ✅ For the **lab record**, include: (1) UML diagram screenshot, (2) generated code listing, (3) hand-edited business logic, (4) output screenshot of running program.
- ✅ The **mapping equation** $G(C) = J$ (where $C$ is a UML class, $J$ is a Java compilation unit) is a high-yield theoretical concept.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstrate reverse engineering in Java (Code to Model conversion)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstrate reverse engineering in Java (Code to Model conversion)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Reverse Engineering in Java (Code to Model Conversion)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reverse Engineering** in the context of Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE) is the analytical process of **examining an existing software system, extracting its design and architectural artifacts, and representing the discovered knowledge at a higher level of abstraction** — typically as Unified Modeling Language (UML) diagrams, Entity-Relationship (ER) schemas, or structured documentation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCNL607 (CASE Lab)*, this experiment specifically demands the demonstration of **Code-to-Model conversion**, where the source artifact is a compiled or partially documented Java program, and the target artifact is a regenerated **UML Class Diagram, Sequence Diagram, and/or Package Diagram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Reverse engineering is the process of deriving the design and specification of a software system from its source code, in order to recover the lost design information, support maintenance, and enable forward re-engineering."*

The formal notation that follows is from the **IEEE Standard 1233-1998 (Guide for Developing System Requirements Specifications)** and the **Object Management Group (OMG) UML 2.5.1 specification**.

### 1.2 The Three-Tier Abstraction Pyramid

Reverse Engineering moves information *upward* through these abstraction layers:

| Tier | Abstraction Level | Artifact | Example |
|------|------------------|----------|---------|
| **Tier 3 (Highest)** | Design / Architecture | UML Diagrams, ER Models | Class Diagram, Sequence Diagram |
| **Tier 2 (Middle)** | Source Code Structure | AST, Intermediate Code | Java `.java` source files |
| **Tier 1 (Lowest)** | Machine / Binary | Compiled Bytecode | `.class` files, JAR archives |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Archaeologist and the Ruined Palace"**
>
> Imagine an archaeologist arriving at the site of an ancient palace that has crumbled over centuries. The original **blueprints (design documents)** are long lost. The archaeologist carefully examines the surviving **stone pillars, foundation outlines, and wall remnants (the code)**, measures distances, identifies the marble types, and reconstructs an **architectural floor plan (the UML model)**. Reverse engineering is the software equivalent of this exact process.
>
> The palace stones = classes, methods, variables.
> The reconstructed floor plan = UML class diagram.
> The archaeologist's tools = CASE tools (Eclipse + ObjectAid, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, StarUML, ArgoUML, Enterprise Architect).

### 1.4 Why KTU Tests This Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance for KTU Students:**
> 1. **Maintenance Reality** — Over **70%** of a software engineer's career is spent maintaining *legacy* code, not writing new code. Reverse engineering is the first step.
> 2. **Documentation Recovery** — Agile projects often ship code with minimal documentation. Reverse engineering auto-generates visual models.
> 3. **Migration Support** — Migrating from Java to Kotlin, or from a monolithic system to microservices, requires first understanding the existing structure.
> 4. **IPO Compliance** — Industry-ready engineers must be tool-fluent, not just code-literate.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Classification: Forward vs Reverse vs Re-Engineering

KTU examiners frequently pose a 3-mark question on distinguishing these three processes. The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet**.

| Attribute | Forward Engineering | **Reverse Engineering** | Re-Engineering |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Direction** | Model → Code | **Code → Model** | Code → Improved Code |
| **Starting Artifact** | UML Diagram, SRS Document | **Existing Source Code** | Existing Source Code |
| **Ending Artifact** | Executable Program | **Design Model / Documentation** | Refactored Program + Model |
| **Purpose** | Build new software | Understand/Recover design | Improve structure & functionality |
| **KTU Tool Used** | StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm | **Eclipse + ObjectAid, EA, ArgoUML** | Eclipse Refactor, NetBeans Refactor |
| **Phase in SDLC** | Development Phase | Maintenance Phase | Modernization Phase |
| **Abstraction Change** | High → Low | **Low → High** | Low → Low (with improvement) |
| **Output Nature** | Creative | Analytical | Creative + Analytical |

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Reverse Engineering

The operational need for reverse engineering arises from three real-world pressures:

- **Documentation Decay (Bit-Rot)** — Design documents become outdated the moment code changes are committed without updating diagrams. Within 18 months, most documentation loses 60–80% of its accuracy against the running code.
- **Personnel Turnover** — Original developers leave; the institutional design knowledge vanishes with them. Reverse engineering recovers this lost knowledge.
- **Legacy System Acquisition** — When a company acquires another, the acquired codebase is often an opaque "black box." Reverse engineering opens the box.

### 2.3 Components Extractable from Java Code

A CASE tool performing Code-to-Model conversion identifies the following structural elements from Java source files:

| # | Java Source Element | UML Model Element | Extraction Method |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | `class` declaration | **Class** (Rectangle) | Lexical scan of `class` keyword |
| 2 | `interface` declaration | **Interface** (`<<interface>>` stereotype) | Lexical scan of `interface` keyword |
| 3 | `extends` keyword | **Generalization** (Hollow Triangle Arrow) | Parser detects inheritance token |
| 4 | `implements` keyword | **Realization** (Dashed Hollow Triangle) | Parser detects implementation token |
| 5 | Instance variables | **Attributes** (in class rectangle) | AST node for field declarations |
| 6 | Method signatures | **Operations** (in class rectangle) | AST node for method declarations |
| 7 | `private`/`public`/`protected` | **Visibility symbols** (`-`, `+`, `#`) | Modifier-to-symbol mapping |
| 8 | `static` modifier | **Underline** in UML | Static indicator mapping |
| 9 | Local variable types | Parameter typing | Type resolution from AST |
| 10 | Method call relationships | **Dependencies / Sequence Diagram messages** | Call graph analysis |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Card for KTU Lab Exam**

| # | Concept | Formal Description | Tool/Command Example | Output Artifact |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Reverse Engineering** | $RE : C \rightarrow M$ where $C$ = Code, $M$ = Model | Eclipse → Right-click → *Visualize* → Add to Class Diagram | Class Diagram (`.ucls`) |
| 2 | **Forward Engineering** | $FE : M \rightarrow C$ | StarUML → *Generate Code* → Java | `.java` files |
| 3 | **Round-Trip Engineering** | $RT = FE \circ RE$ | Enterprise Architect | Synchronized model + code |
| 4 | **Class Visibility Symbols** | $+ \equiv public$, $\# \equiv protected$, $- \equiv private$, $\sim \equiv package$ | Auto-mapped by parser | UML attribute prefix |
| 5 | **ObjectAid Plugin** | Eclipse UML Explorer for Java | `Help \rightarrow Eclipse Marketplace \rightarrow ObjectAid` | Class + Sequence Diagrams |
| 6 | **ArgoUML** | Open-source standalone CASE tool | `argouml.jar` execution | Class, Sequence, State, Activity, Use-Case |
| 7 | **Visual Paradigm** | Commercial CASE tool (Community Edition free) | *Instant Reverse* → Java | Class, Package, Component Diagrams |
| 8 | **Enterprise Architect (EA)** | Sparx Systems CASE suite | *Import* → *Source Directory* | Comprehensive UML 2.5 |
| 9 | **StarUML** | Lightweight UML 2.x tool | *Menu* → *Java* → *Reverse Engineer* | Class, Package Diagrams |
| 10 | **PlantUML** | Text-to-UML (declarative reverse) | `@startuml class Foo {} @enduml` | Class, Sequence, Activity, etc. |

### 2.5 UML Multiplicity Notation Reference

When the CASE tool reverse-engineers association relationships, it represents **multiplicity** using the following standard UML 2.5.1 notation:

| Notation | Meaning | Example Scenario |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| `1` | Exactly one | Each `Student` has exactly **1** `StudentID` |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) | An `Employee` may have **0..1** `CompanyCar` |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many (unbounded) | A `Department` has `0..*` `Employee` instances |
| `1..*` | At least one | A `Team` must have `1..*` `Player` instances |
| `n..m` | Bounded range | A `Semester` enrolls `30..60` `Students` |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Use of Reverse Engineering |
|-----------------|---------------------------|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Understanding 30-year-old COBOL legacy cores before migration |
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Generating design artifacts for FAA certification audits |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | Recovering data flow diagrams for compliance documentation |
| **Cybersecurity** | Analyzing malware bytecode (`.class` decompilation → design recovery) |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Migrating ECU software architectures between vendor platforms |
| **Telecom (5G/LTE)** | Understanding vendor-locked baseband code for interoperability |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Experiment Aim

> **Aim:** To demonstrate *reverse engineering* in Java by converting an existing multi-class Java source program into a **UML Class Diagram** using the **Eclipse IDE with the ObjectAid UML Explorer plugin** (the most commonly adopted KTU lab setup). Secondary objective: produce a **Sequence Diagram** from method-call relationships.

## 3.2 Prerequisites (Lab Setup Checklist)

1. **JDK 17 or later** installed and `JAVA_HOME` configured.
2. **Eclipse IDE for Java Developers (2023-09 or later)** installed.
3. **ObjectAid UML Explorer** plugin installed via *Eclipse Marketplace*:
   - `Help` → `Eclipse Marketplace` → Search `ObjectAid` → Install → Restart.
4. Sample Java project with **at least 3 classes**, exhibiting:
   - Inheritance (`extends`)
   - Interface implementation (`implements`)
   - Association (one-to-many)
   - Aggregation / Composition (preferred)

## 3.3 Java Source Code for Reverse Engineering

Create a project named `ReverseEngDemo` and add the following five Java files:

### File 1: `Person.java` (Abstract Superclass)

```java
public abstract class Person {
    protected String name;
    protected int age;
    protected String address;
    
    public Person(String name, int age, String address) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
        this.address = address;
    }
    
    public abstract void displayDetails();
    
    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }
}
```

### File 2: `Student.java` (Subclass demonstrating Inheritance)

```java
public class Student extends Person {
    private int rollNumber;
    private double cgpa;
    private Course[] enrolledCourses;
    private int courseCount;
    
    public Student(String name, int age, String address, int rollNumber, double cgpa) {
        super(name, age, address);
        this.rollNumber = rollNumber;
        this.cgpa = cgpa;
        this.enrolledCourses = new Course[5];
        this.courseCount = 0;
    }
    
    public void enrollCourse(Course course) {
        if (courseCount < enrolledCourses.length) {
            enrolledCourses[courseCount++] = course;
        }
    }
    
    @Override
    public void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Student: " + name + ", Roll: " + rollNumber + ", CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }
}
```

### File 3: `Professor.java` (Subclass with Interface Implementation)

```java
public class Professor extends Person implements IResearcher {
    private String employeeId;
    private String department;
    private int publications;
    
    public Professor(String name, int age, String address, String employeeId, String department) {
        super(name, age, address);
        this.employeeId = employeeId;
        this.department = department;
        this.publications = 0;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Professor: " + name + ", Dept: " + department);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void publishPaper(String title) {
        publications++;
        System.out.println("Published: " + title);
    }
}
```

### File 4: `Course.java` (Associated Class)

```java
public class Course {
    private String courseCode;
    private String courseName;
    private int credits;
    private Professor instructor;
    
    public Course(String courseCode, String courseName, int credits, Professor instructor) {
        this.courseCode = courseCode;
        this.courseName = courseName;
        this.credits = credits;
        this.instructor = instructor;
    }
    
    public void showCourseInfo() {
        System.out.println(courseCode + ": " + courseName + " (" + credits + " credits)");
    }
}
```

### File 5: `IResearcher.java` (Interface)

```java
public interface IResearcher {
    void publishPaper(String title);
    int getPublicationCount();
}
```

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure (Using Eclipse + ObjectAid)

**Step 1 — Create a new Java Project**
- Open Eclipse → `File` → `New` → `Java Project` → Name: `ReverseEngDemo` → Finish.

**Step 2 — Add the five `.java` files**
- Copy-paste the five source files into the `src` folder of the project.

**Step 3 — Verify the project compiles**
- `Project` → `Build Project` (or `Ctrl+B`). Resolve any syntax errors.

**Step 4 — Create a new UML Class Diagram file**
- `File` → `New` → `Other` → `ObjectAid UML Diagram` → `Class Diagram` → File name: `ReverseEngDiagram.ucls` → Finish.

**Step 5 — Drag classes from Package Explorer onto the diagram canvas**
- Drag `Person.java`, `Student.java`, `Professor.java`, `Course.java`, `IResearcher.java` from *Package Explorer* onto the diagram.

**Step 6 — ObjectAid auto-derives the relationships**
- The plugin parses the bytecode / source and automatically draws:
  - `Student` → `Person` with a hollow triangle (Generalization / Inheritance).
  - `Professor` → `Person` (Generalization).
  - `Professor` → `IResearcher` with a dashed hollow triangle (Realization).
  - `Student` → `Course` (Association with multiplicity `*` to `1`).
  - `Course` → `Professor` (Association).

**Step 7 — Configure visibility and layout**
- Right-click any class → `Properties` → Toggle *Show Attributes* / *Show Operations* / *Show Visibility*.
- Rearrange the diagram for clarity using drag-and-drop.

**Step 8 — Save the diagram**
- `Ctrl+S`. The `.ucls` file is now a persistent visual model derived from code.

**Step 9 — Generate a Sequence Diagram (optional)**
- Create another `.ucls` file named `SequenceDiagram.ucls` of type *Sequence Diagram*.
- Drag a method (e.g., `Student.enrollCourse`) onto the diagram.
- ObjectAid traces the call graph and renders the sequence of message exchanges.

**Step 10 — Export the model**
- Right-click the `.ucls` file → `Export` → `Image` (PNG/JPG) for inclusion in the lab record.

## 3.5 Expected Output (Verbal Description of Generated UML Class Diagram)

The reverse-engineered diagram contains the following five UML elements:

| UML Element | Source Code Trigger | UML Notation in Diagram |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| `Person` (abstract class, italicized) | `abstract class Person` | Rectangle with italicized name |
| `Student` (concrete class) | `class Student extends Person` | Rectangle connected to `Person` via solid line with hollow triangle pointing to `Person` |
| `Professor` (concrete class) | `class Professor extends Person implements IResearcher` | Rectangle with two outgoing relationships: solid-triangle to `Person`, dashed-triangle to `IResearcher` |
| `Course` (concrete class) | `class Course` | Independent rectangle |
| `IResearcher` (interface) | `interface IResearcher` | Rectangle with `<<interface>>` stereotype |
| Attribute `name: String` | `protected String name;` | Listed in `Person` rectangle with `#` symbol |
| Operation `displayDetails(): void` | `public abstract void displayDetails();` | Listed in `Person` rectangle in *italics* |
| Association `Student → Course` | `Course[] enrolledCourses` field | Solid line, `1` near `Student`, `*` near `Course` |
| Association `Course → Professor` | `Professor instructor` field | Solid line, `1` multiplicity on both ends |

## 3.6 Derivation of the Class Diagram from Source — Analytical Walkthrough

The CASE tool performs the following logical sequence to derive the model:

**Phase 1: Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)**
- The lexer scans `Student.java` and emits tokens: `public`, `class`, `Student`, `extends`, `Person`, `{`, `private`, `int`, `rollNumber`, `;`, …

**Phase 2: Syntax Analysis (Parsing)**
- The parser builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**:

$$
AST_{Student} = \text{ClassDecl}(\text{name}=\text{Student}, \text{extends}=\text{Person}, \text{fields}, \text{methods})
$$

- Fields node: `[ \text{rollNumber:int}, \text{cgpa:double}, \text{enrolledCourses:Course[]}, \text{courseCount:int} ]$
- Methods node: `[ \text{Student(...): constructor}, \text{enrollCourse(Course): void}, \text{displayDetails(): void}, \text{getName(): String} ]$

**Phase 3: Semantic Analysis (Relationship Resolution)**
- The semantic analyzer resolves the symbol `Person` (the superclass) to `Person.class` in the classpath.
- It identifies the type of `enrolledCourses` as `Course[]` → an aggregation/association of multiplicity `*` to `1`.

**Phase 4: Model Synthesis**
- The CASE tool synthesizes a UML model object:

$$
M_{Student} = (\text{Name}=\text{Student}, \text{Stereotype}=\emptyset, \text{Superclass}=\text{Person}, \text{Attributes}, \text{Operations})
$$

**Phase 5: Diagram Rendering**
- The renderer lays out the model using a force-directed graph algorithm and emits an SVG/PNG representation.

## 3.7 Comparison: Reverse Engineering via Decompilation vs Source Parsing

| Aspect | **Decompilation-Based RE** | **Source-Parsing RE** |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Input | `.class` bytecode (binary) | `.java` source (text) |
| Tool Examples | JD-GUI, CFR, Procyon | ObjectAid, Eclipse JDT, ArgoUML |
| Information Recovered | Class structure, method signatures (partial) | Full class structure, comments, generics, annotations |
| Accuracy | Moderate (~85–95%) | High (~98–100%) |
| Loss of Information | Variable names may be lost; generics erased | None, except stripped comments |
| KTU Lab Use Case | Rarely (security/forensics) | **Commonly used** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
