# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Short-Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q1.** Define **reverse engineering** in the context of CASE tools. Differentiate it from **forward engineering** in **two** points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Reverse engineering is the process of analyzing an existing software system's source code or executable artifacts to extract its design, architecture, and behavioral information, and reconstructing this information in the form of higher-level models such as UML diagrams, ER schemas, or structured documentation.

**Two Differences (0.75 marks each):**

| Aspect | Forward Engineering | Reverse Engineering |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Direction** | Model → Code (High → Low abstraction) | Code → Model (Low → High abstraction) |
| **Purpose** | Build a new system from a design | Recover the design of an existing system |
| **Phase** | Development | Maintenance / Modernization |

---

### Question 2: Tool-Specific Short-Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q2.** List **any three** CASE tools that support reverse engineering of Java code, and state **one distinguishing feature** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Eclipse with ObjectAid UML Explorer** (1 mark) — A lightweight Eclipse plugin that generates UML class and sequence diagrams by directly parsing Java source files in real time; updates the diagram automatically when the code is modified.

2. **Visual Paradigm (Community Edition)** (1 mark) — A professional CASE suite that provides the *Instant Reverse* feature for Java, supporting comprehensive UML 2.5 diagrams including class, package, and component diagrams.

3. **Enterprise Architect (Sparx Systems)** (1 mark) — An industry-grade CASE tool that supports round-trip engineering (forward + reverse), enabling synchronized maintenance of code and UML models; widely used in avionics, banking, and defense domains.

*(Acceptable alternatives: ArgoUML, StarUML, Rational Rose, PlantUML, NetBeans UML plugin.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations for Lab courses (PCCNL607) typically feature *practical/procedural* Part B questions worth 14 marks with internal choice. The structure below mirrors that pattern.

---

### Choice A: Full Experiment Demonstration `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q.A (a)** Write a Java program containing at least **three classes** demonstrating **inheritance**, **interface implementation**, and **association**. (7 marks)
>
> **Q.A (b)** Demonstrate the **step-by-step procedure** to reverse engineer the above Java program into a **UML Class Diagram** using the **Eclipse + ObjectAid** tool. Include the **expected output diagram** in your description. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Java Source Code (7 marks)**

```java
// File: Shape.java
public abstract class Shape {
    protected String color;
    public Shape(String color) {
        this.color = color;
    }
    public abstract double calculateArea();
    public String getColor() {
        return color;
    }
}

// File: Circle.java
public class Circle extends Shape implements Drawable {
    private double radius;
    public Circle(String color, double radius) {
        super(color);
        this.radius = radius;
    }
    @Override
    public double calculateArea() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a " + color + " circle of radius " + radius);
    }
}

// File: Drawable.java
public interface Drawable {
    void draw();
}
```

**Mark distribution for (a):**
- Correct abstract class with `extends` capability: **2 marks**
- Correct subclass with `extends` and `implements`: **2 marks**
- Correct interface declaration: **1 mark**
- Method overriding demonstrated: **1 mark**
- Code compiles and runs: **1 mark**

**Part (b) — Reverse Engineering Procedure (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Install ObjectAid via `Help → Eclipse Marketplace → Search "ObjectAid" → Install → Restart`.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Create a new Java Project named `ReverseShapeDemo` and add the three `.java` files.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Build the project (`Ctrl+B`) to ensure no compilation errors.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Create a new Class Diagram: `File → New → Other → ObjectAid UML Diagram → Class Diagram → ReverseShapeDiagram.ucls`.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Drag `Shape.java`, `Circle.java`, and `Drawable.java` from Package Explorer to the diagram canvas.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** ObjectAid auto-detects and renders:
- `Circle → Shape` (Generalization: solid line with hollow triangle)
- `Circle → Drawable` (Realization: dashed line with hollow triangle)
- Attributes `color: String` (#) in `Shape`
- Attribute `radius: double` (-) in `Circle`
- Operations `calculateArea(): double` (italic in `Shape`), `draw(): void` in `Circle` and `Drawable`

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Save the diagram (`Ctrl+S`) and export as PNG for the lab record.

**Expected Output Diagram — Verbal Description:**
A class diagram with three rectangles. `Shape` is at the top, marked as abstract (italicized name). `Drawable` interface (with `<<interface>>` stereotype) is on the right. `Circle` is at the bottom. A solid line with a hollow triangle points from `Circle` upward to `Shape`. A dashed line with a hollow triangle points from `Circle` to the right toward `Drawable`. Inside `Shape`: attribute `#color: String` and abstract operation `calculateArea(): double` (italic). Inside `Circle`: attribute `-radius: double` and operation `draw(): void`. Inside `Drawable`: operation `draw(): void` (italic).

---

### Choice B: Tool-Centric Comparative Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **three-tier abstraction pyramid** in software engineering and place reverse engineering in this pyramid. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of reverse engineering. (7 marks)
>
> **Q.B (b)** Compare **ObjectAid**, **ArgoUML**, and **Enterprise Architect** as CASE tools for Java reverse engineering across **four** distinct parameters. Recommend the most suitable tool for a KTU undergraduate lab. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Abstraction Pyramid & Reverse Engineering (7 marks)**

**Three-Tier Pyramid (3 marks):**
- **Tier 1 — Implementation / Machine Level:** Compiled bytecode (`.class`), executables, machine code.
- **Tier 2 — Source Code Level:** Java source (`.java`), C++, Python files. Structural, syntactically detailed.
- **Tier 3 — Design / Architecture Level:** UML diagrams, SRS documents, ER schemas. Highest level of abstraction.

Reverse engineering moves information from **Tier 1/Tier 2 → Tier 3** (i.e., code → model), the *upward* direction.

**Two Advantages (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Documentation Recovery:** Auto-generates up-to-date design diagrams from the latest source code, eliminating stale documentation.
2. **Maintenance Efficiency:** Helps new developers quickly understand a legacy system, reducing onboarding time by up to **50%** as per industry studies.

**Two Limitations (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Loss of Design Intent:** Reverse engineering recovers *structure* but not *rationale* — i.e., *why* a design decision was made is not preserved in code.
2. **Tool/Version Dependency:** Different CASE tools may produce slightly different UML models for the same codebase, leading to inconsistency.

**Part (b) — Tool Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | ObjectAid (Eclipse Plugin) | ArgoUML (Standalone) | Enterprise Architect (Commercial) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Cost** (1 mark) | Free, open-source | Free, open-source | Commercial (~$399), free trial |
| **UML Version Support** (1 mark) | UML 2.x (class + sequence) | UML 1.4 (limited 2.0) | UML 2.5.1 (full suite) |
| **Reverse Engineering Capability** (1 mark) | Java source parsing; real-time updates | Java source via *Import* menu; no auto-sync | *Source Code Engineering*; round-trip supported |
| **Learning Curve** (1 mark) | Low (Eclipse users adapt quickly) | Medium (standalone, dated UI) | High (rich features, complex UI) |
| **Diagram Types** (1 mark) | Class, Sequence | Class, Sequence, State, Activity, Use-Case | All 14 UML 2.5.1 diagram types |
| **Platform Support** (1 mark) | Windows, macOS, Linux (via Eclipse) | Cross-platform Java | Windows (primary), others limited |
| **KTU Lab Suitability** (1 mark) | **★★★★★ (Highly Recommended)** | ★★★ (Good but dated) | ★★ (Overkill for UG) |

**Recommendation (1 mark):** For KTU undergraduate labs, **ObjectAid (Eclipse plugin)** is the most suitable tool because it is free, integrates seamlessly with the Eclipse IDE that students already use for Java programming, supports real-time reverse engineering, and covers the two most-tested UML diagrams (class and sequence).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Reverse with Forward Engineering (–2 marks):** Students often write that reverse engineering generates code from a diagram. The correct direction is **Code → Model**, not Model → Code.
>
> 2. **Omitting the Diagram in Lab Record (–3 marks):** The reverse-engineered **UML class diagram screenshot** is a *mandatory* output artifact. A procedure without a diagram is considered incomplete.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Show Visibility Symbols (–1 mark):** The auto-generated diagram must clearly show `+`, `-`, `#` symbols for `public`, `private`, `protected`. If the diagram is captured with visibility hidden, marks are deducted.
>
> 4. **Using `class` for Interface (–1 mark):** When reverse engineering `Drawable.java`, the diagram must show it as `<<interface>>`, not as a regular `class`. Students often miss this distinction.
>
> 5. **No Code Compilation Evidence (–1 mark):** The lab record must show a screenshot of the project compiling successfully (`Build Successful` in Eclipse console) *before* reverse engineering is performed.
>
> 6. **Confusing Reverse Engineering with Decompilation (–1 mark):** Decompilation converts bytecode back to source. Reverse engineering produces *models*, not source code.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**
>
> ✅ Reverse Engineering = **Code → Model** (Low abstraction → High abstraction).
>
> ✅ Forward Engineering = **Model → Code** (High → Low).
>
> ✅ Re-Engineering = **Code → Improved Code** (includes both refactoring and reverse engineering for guidance).
>
> ✅ Most common **KTU lab tool** = **Eclipse + ObjectAid UML Explorer**.
>
> ✅ ObjectAid supports **Class Diagrams** and **Sequence Diagrams** for Java.
>
> ✅ Plugin installation: `Help → Eclipse Marketplace → ObjectAid → Install → Restart Eclipse`.
>
> ✅ File format for saved diagrams: **`.ucls`** (ObjectAid Classdiagram file).
>
> ✅ UML visibility symbols: `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package).
>
> ✅ **Abstract class** in UML is shown with *italicized* name.
>
> ✅ **Interface** in UML is shown with `<<interface>>` stereotype.
>
> ✅ **Generalization** (inheritance) = solid line + hollow triangle.
>
> ✅ **Realization** (implements) = dashed line + hollow triangle.
>
> ✅ **Association** = solid line; **Aggregation** = solid line + hollow diamond; **Composition** = solid line + filled diamond.
>
> ✅ Multiplicity notations: `1`, `0..1`, `*` (or `0..*`), `1..*`, `n..m`.
>
> ✅ Three-tier abstraction pyramid: **Machine → Source → Design**.
>
> ✅ Two key advantages: documentation recovery + maintenance support.
>
> ✅ Two key limitations: loss of design intent + tool inconsistency.
>
> ✅ **Mandatory lab record artifacts**: (1) Java source code listing, (2) Console output showing successful compilation, (3) UML class diagram screenshot, (4) Brief description of the procedure.
>
> ✅ Commercial alternatives: **Enterprise Architect**, **Rational Rose** (discontinued but historically tested), **Visual Paradigm**.
>
> ✅ Open-source alternatives: **ArgoUML**, **StarUML**, **PlantUML**, **Umlet**, **Violet UML**.
>
> ✅ PlantUML uses **declarative text-based** reverse engineering (e.g., `@startuml ... @enduml`).
>
> ✅ The process flow: **Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis (AST) → Semantic Analysis → Model Synthesis → Diagram Rendering**.

---

topics.title: Develop and draw the deployment diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop and draw the deployment diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deployment Diagram** is a type of **UML 2.5 (Unified Modeling Language) Structural Diagram** that models the **physical deployment of software artifacts** (executables, libraries, JAR/WAR files, DLLs, databases) onto **hardware devices and execution environments** (servers, workstations, mobile devices, cloud nodes). It captures the **runtime topology** of a system: which software runs on which hardware, and how the hardware nodes communicate with one another across network protocols.

In the KTU 2024 *CASE Lab* context, a deployment diagram is the **output artifact of Experiment 2**, generated using a CASE (Computer-Aided Software Engineering) tool such as **Rational Rose, StarUML, Lucidchart, Visual Paradigm, Draw.io, or PlantUML**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Aligned):**
> *"A deployment diagram shows the configuration of run-time processing nodes and the software artifacts, processes, and objects that live on them. It is a graph of nodes connected by communication associations, drawn as 3-D boxes (nodes) with artifacts/components placed inside."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition 🎯

Imagine you are the **chief architect of a hospital**. Before any software is installed, you must decide:

| Hospital Equipment | UML Deployment Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 🏥 Main Hospital Building | **Server Node** (e.g., `ApplicationServer`) |
| 💻 Reception Desk Computer | **Client Node** (e.g., `ReceptionPC`) |
| 🖨️ X-Ray Machine with monitor | **Device Node** (e.g., `XRayMachine`) |
| 👨‍⚕️ Doctor's Tablet | **Mobile Device Node** |
| 🔌 LAN cables, Wi-Fi routers | **Communication Association Paths** |
| 🩺 Patient Record Software installed on PC | **Artifact** (e.g., `ReceptionApp.exe`) |

Just as the hospital architect draws a **floor plan** showing where equipment sits and how power/network cables connect them, a **deployment diagram** is the **"software floor plan"** of a system, showing where each piece of code physically lives and how devices talk to each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Asks This:**
> Deployment diagrams are assessed because they are the **only UML diagram that explicitly addresses non-software concerns** (hardware, network, OS, middleware). They answer the key question: *"Where will my code actually run?"* — critical in distributed/cloud-native architectures (microservices, Kubernetes pods, AWS EC2 instances).

---

## 1.3 Scope Boundaries of a Deployment Diagram

| Concern | Addressed? |
|---|---|
| Hardware topology (servers, clients) | ✅ Yes |
| Network protocols (HTTP, TCP/IP, JDBC) | ✅ Yes |
| Software artifacts on each node | ✅ Yes |
| Object interactions at runtime | ❌ No (use sequence diagram) |
| Class structure | ❌ No (use class diagram) |
| Use case flow | ❌ No (use activity/use case diagram) |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU pitfall is confusing **deployment diagrams with component diagrams**. Recall:
> - **Component Diagram** = Logical view of *what* software modules exist and *how they connect*.
> - **Deployment Diagram** = Physical view of *where* those components physically *run* and *how machines connect*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Tier-1 Elements of a Deployment Diagram

A deployment diagram is fundamentally built from **three primary elements**:

### 2.1.1 Nodes (📦)
A **Node** is a runtime physical resource on which artifacts are deployed. Drawn as a **3-D box (cube)**.

- **Device Node** → A physical hardware resource. Stereotyped `<<device>>`.
  - Examples: PC, Laptop, Mobile Phone, Router, Mainframe, IoT Sensor.
- **Execution Environment Node** → A software platform running on top of a device. Stereotyped `<<executionEnvironment>>`.
  - Examples: JVM, .NET CLR, OS, Apache Tomcat, Docker Container, Node.js runtime, Web Browser, Android OS.
- **Node Nesting** → An execution environment can be **nested inside** a device node (because software runs on hardware).

### 2.1.2 Artifacts (📄)
An **Artifact** is a physical piece of information (a file) deployed on a node. Drawn as a **document icon** (a rectangle with a folded corner) and labeled with **stereotype** `<<artifact>>`.

- Common artifacts:
  - `.exe` (Windows executable)
  - `.jar` (Java archive)
  - `.war` (Web application archive)
  - `.dll` (Dynamic Link Library)
  - `.apk` (Android Package)
  - `database file` (e.g., `PatientsDB.db`)
  - Configuration files (`config.xml`)

### 2.1.3 Communication Associations (🔗)
A **Communication Path** is a line drawn between two nodes representing the **physical communication link** (cable, wireless, network protocol). Labels often include the **protocol** used (e.g., `«HTTP»`, `«TCP/IP»`, `«JDBC»`).

---

## 2.2 Auxiliary Modeling Elements

| Element | Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Stereotype** | `<<device>>`, `<<executionEnvironment>>`, `<<artifact>>` | Classifies a node/artifact |
| **Dependency** | Dashed arrow `-->` with stereotype | Shows one artifact depends on another |
| **Manifestation** | Dashed arrow from artifact to component | Shows an artifact *implements* a component |
| **Component** | Rectangle with two small rectangles on left side | Software module deployed as artifact |
| **Package** | Folder icon | Groups related nodes |
| **Note** | Dog-eared rectangle with dashed line | Free-form annotation/clarification |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Notation Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim — questions on notation are scored as 3-mark direct items in KTU ESE.**

| Symbol | UML Name | Visual | KTU High-Yield Fact |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **3-D Cube Box** | Device Node | 🟦 (cube) | Stereotype `<<device>>` is **mandatory** |
| **3-D Box (inner)** | Execution Environment | 🟦 (cube, nested) | Stereotype `<<executionEnvironment>>` |
| **Doc-with-folded-corner** | Artifact | 📄 | Labeled `<<artifact>> name.ext` |
| **Solid line** | Communication Association | ─── | Label with protocol: `«HTTP»`, `«JDBC»` |
| **Dashed arrow** | Dependency / Manifestation | - - -> | Component → Artifact = manifestation |
| **Rectangle with tabs** | Component | ▭ with left tabs | Optional; shown inside node |
| **Folder** | Package | 📁 | Used for grouping (rare in labs) |

---

## 2.4 Why Are Deployment Diagrams Useful in Engineering?

1. **Distributed System Design** — Essential for client-server, microservices, cloud architectures.
2. **Capacity Planning** — Identifies hardware bottlenecks (e.g., single server hosting DB + App = risk).
3. **DevOps & CI/CD** — Maps directly to Docker/Kubernetes pods, AWS/Azure resources.
4. **Security Audits** — Shows network boundaries and exposed endpoints.
5. **Disaster Recovery Planning** — Identifies single points of failure.
6. **Cost Estimation** — Hardware procurement decisions based on node count.

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Notation Choices

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Why is a node drawn as a **3-D box**? | Visually distinct from a 2-D class box; emphasizes **physical/real-world entity**. |
| Why is the **execution environment** nested inside the device? | Reflects reality: an OS (JVM) runs *inside* the hardware (PC). |
| Why use **stereotypes** `<<...>>`? | UML's extension mechanism — adds semantics without changing core metamodel. |
| Why label communication paths with **protocols**? | Engineers must know *how* nodes connect, not just *that* they connect. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process to Develop a Deployment Diagram

> This section is the **exhaustive, no-skipping** methodology for Experiment 2. Follow these steps exactly during your lab record write-up.

---

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Requirements Gathering (Pre-Drawing)

### Step 1.1: Re-read the Problem Statement
Identify all **hardware devices** mentioned (PC, server, mobile, sensor, printer) and all **software components** (web app, DB, authentication service).

### Step 1.2: Identify the **Tiers** of the Architecture
| Tier | Typical Components | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Presentation Tier** | Client GUI, browser, mobile app | `WebBrowser` |
| **Logic / Application Tier** | Business logic server | `AppServer` |
| **Data Tier** | Database server, file storage | `DBServer` |

### Step 1.3: List the **Protocols** between Tiers
- Client ↔ Server → `«HTTP»` or `«HTTPS»`
- App Server ↔ DB Server → `«JDBC»` or `«ODBC»`
- Server ↔ Server → `«TCP/IP»` or `«RMI»` or `«WebService(SOAP/REST)»`

---

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Identify Nodes (Drawing Phase)

### Step 2.1: Enumerate Device Nodes
For each piece of physical hardware, create one **device node** with stereotype `<<device>>`.

```
Examples: <<device>> PC_Reception, <<device>> Server_Room, <<device>> DoctorTablet
```

### Step 2.2: Enumerate Execution Environment Nodes
For each software runtime (OS, JVM, browser, DB engine), create a node with stereotype `<<executionEnvironment>>` **nested inside the appropriate device**.

```
Examples: <<executionEnvironment>> Windows11 (inside PC_Reception)
          <<executionEnvironment>> ApacheTomcat (inside Server_Room)
          <<executionEnvironment>> MySQL (inside DBServer)
```

---

## 3.3 Phase 3 — Identify Artifacts

For each deployable software file, draw an **artifact** and place it **inside the execution environment node** where it runs.

```
Examples: <<artifact>> PatientPortal.war   (inside ApacheTomcat)
          <<artifact>> HospitalDB.sql      (inside MySQL)
          <<artifact>> ReceptionApp.exe    (inside Windows11)
```

---

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Draw Communication Associations

Draw a **solid line** between every pair of nodes that communicates. **Label the line** with the protocol used. Use a separate association per direction of communication (if asymmetric).

---

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Add Manifestation Links (Optional but KTU-Appreciated)

If you are deriving deployment from a component diagram, draw **dashed arrows** from each artifact to the **component it implements**. This shows traceability between logical (component) and physical (deployment) views.

---

## 3.6 Phase 6 — Validation Checklist

Before submission, verify:

- [x] Every device node has stereotype `<<device>>`.
- [x] Every execution environment is **nested** inside a device.
- [x] Every artifact is **inside** an execution environment.
- [x] All communication paths have a **protocol label**.
- [x] No artifact floats outside any node.
- [x] Diagram title is present.
- [x] Legend / Notation key is included.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Hospital Management System

### Step A — Read problem
*"A hospital uses a web portal for receptionists, a tablet app for doctors, and a central server hosting a Java-based application. The application stores data in a MySQL database. Receptionists access the portal via browser. Doctors access the app via tablet. The DB and App are on different servers."*

### Step B — Tier Map
| Tier | Element | Node |
|---|---|---|
| Presentation | Receptionist Browser | `<<device>> PC_Reception` → `<<executionEnvironment>> Chrome` |
| Presentation | Doctor Tablet | `<<device>> Tablet_Doctor` → `<<executionEnvironment>> Android` |
| Logic | Java Web App | `<<device>> AppServer` → `<<executionEnvironment>> ApacheTomcat` |
| Data | MySQL DB | `<<device>> DBServer` → `<<executionEnvironment>> MySQL` |

### Step C — Artifacts Map
| Artifact | Inside Node |
|---|---|
| `<<artifact>> HospitalPortal.war` | ApacheTomcat |
| `<<artifact>> DoctorApp.apk` | Android |
| `<<artifact>> HospitalDB` | MySQL |

### Step D — Associations
- `Chrome` ─── «HTTPS» ─── `ApacheTomcat`
- `Android` ─── «REST/HTTPS» ─── `ApacheTomcat`
- `ApacheTomcat` ─── «JDBC» ─── `MySQL`

The full drawing is presented in **Section 4** as a Mermaid block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML-Compliant Mermaid Block Diagram — Hospital Management System Deployment

```mermaid
graph TB

subgraph SERVER_ROOM ["SERVER ROOM"]
    subgraph AppServerDevice ["<<device>> AppServer (Linux)"]
        subgraph AppServerEE ["<<executionEnvironment>> Apache Tomcat 9"]
            ArtifactApp["<<artifact>> HospitalPortal.war"]
            ArtifactDoctorApp["<<artifact>> DoctorAppServer.war"]
        end
    end

    subgraph DBServerDevice ["<<device>> DBServer (Linux)"]
        subgraph DBServerEE ["<<executionEnvironment>> MySQL 8.0"]
            ArtifactDB["<<artifact>> HospitalDB (Schema)"]
        end
    end

    AppServerEE -- "<<JDBC>>" --> DBServerEE
end

subgraph RECEPTION ["RECEPTION DESK"]
    subgraph PCDevice ["<<device>> Reception PC (Windows 11)"]
        subgraph BrowserEE ["<<executionEnvironment>> Google Chrome"]
            ArtifactBrowser["<<artifact>> index.html (Browser Cache)"]
        end
    end
end

subgraph DOCTOR ["DOCTOR MOBILE"]
    subgraph TabletDevice ["<<device>> Doctor Tablet"]
        subgraph AndroidEE ["<<executionEnvironment>> Android 13 OS"]
            ArtifactAPK["<<artifact>> DoctorApp.apk"]
        end
    end
end

BrowserEE -- "<<HTTPS over TCP/IP>>" --> AppServerEE
AndroidEE -- "<<REST over HTTPS>>" --> AppServerEE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid diagram:**
> - **Outer rectangles** = physical device nodes (`<<device>>`).
> - **Inner rectangles nested inside** = execution environments (`<<executionEnvironment>>`).
> - **Small boxes inside the innermost rectangles** = artifacts (`<<artifact>>`).
> - **Arrows with labels** = communication associations with protocols.
> - This mirrors the exact visual layout of a UML deployment diagram drawn in StarUML / Rational Rose.

---

## 4.2 Notation Legend Block (Add to Every Lab Submission)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1["3-D Box"] -.->|represents| LN1["<<device>> Node"]
    L2["Nested 3-D Box"] -.->|represents| LN2["<<executionEnvironment>>"]
    L3["Document Icon"] -.->|represents| LN3["<<artifact>>"]
    L4["Solid Line + Label"] -.->|represents| LN4["Communication Association (with protocol)"]
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture Topology View (Client–Server–Database)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RECEPTION PC"] -->|HTTPS| B["APP SERVER"]
    C["DOCTOR TABLET"] -->|REST/HTTPS| B
    B -->|JDBC| D["DB SERVER"]
```

This simplified flow shows the **3-tier distributed architecture** at a glance — the typical KTU asked drawing.

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which UML Diagram

| If the question is... | Draw this UML Diagram |
|---|---|
| *What does the system do?* | **Use Case Diagram** |
| *How does the system behave over time?* | **Sequence / Activity Diagram** |
| *What are the software modules?* | **Component Diagram** |
| ***Where does the software physically run?*** | ***Deployment Diagram*** ✅ |
| *What is the data model?* | **Class Diagram / ER Diagram** |
| *What are the object states?* | **State Machine Diagram** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a deployment diagram. List any FOUR notations used in it.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

A **deployment diagram** is a UML structural diagram that depicts the **physical arrangement of hardware nodes**, the **execution environments** running on them, the **software artifacts** deployed on those environments, and the **communication links** (with protocols) connecting the nodes.

**Four Notations:**

1. **Node** — represented as a 3-D box, with stereotype `<<device>>` for hardware (e.g., PC, server) and `<<executionEnvironment>>` for software platforms (e.g., JVM, Tomcat).
2. **Artifact** — represented as a document icon (rectangle with a folded corner) with stereotype `<<artifact>>`, indicating a deployable file (e.g., `.war`, `.exe`).
3. **Communication Association** — a solid line connecting two nodes, labeled with a stereotype indicating the protocol (e.g., `«HTTP»`, `«JDBC»`).
4. **Component** — an optional rectangle with two small rectangles on the left side, placed inside a node to show the software module that an artifact implements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for definition and 0.5 mark each for the four notations. Spelling "stereotype" correctly earns bonus credit.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a Component Diagram and a Deployment Diagram.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Component Diagram | Deployment Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Shows logical organization of software modules | Shows physical distribution on hardware |
| **Primary Element** | Components (rectangles with tabs) | Nodes (3-D boxes) |
| **Focus** | *What* software exists and its dependencies | *Where* software runs and how hardware connects |
| **Node Type** | Logical (no device/EE distinction) | Physical (`<<device>>`, `<<executionEnvironment>>`) |
| **Connections** | Dashed dependency arrows | Solid communication associations with protocols |
| **Artifact** | Component *itself* | Artifact is a *separate* icon inside a node |
| **View** | Logical / Developer view | Physical / Network architect view |
| **Example** | `<<component>> AuthService` | `<<device>> Server → <<executionEnvironment>> Tomcat → <<artifact>> AuthService.war` |

> **Memory trick:** *Component = "What", Deployment = "Where".*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B offers internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — students answer one.

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *The Classic 3-Tier Web Application*

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Develop a deployment diagram for a 3-tier online examination system with the following specification. Draw the diagram and explain each component.** (7 Marks)

**Specification:**
- Students access the system via a **web browser** on a **Windows PC**.
- The web application runs on an **Apache Tomcat** server (Linux).
- The database is **MySQL**, hosted on a separate **DB server**.
- Authentication is handled by a separate **LDAP server**.
- Communication: Browser ↔ App Server uses HTTPS; App Server ↔ DB uses JDBC; App Server ↔ LDAP uses LDAP protocol.

**(b) Explain the role of stereotypes in UML deployment diagrams with three examples relevant to your diagram in part (a).** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Development of the Diagram (7 marks)**

Following the methodology in **Section 3**, we identify:

| Tier | Device Node | Execution Environment | Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Presentation | `<<device>> StudentPC` | `<<executionEnvironment>> Chrome Browser` | `<<artifact>> index.html` |
| Logic | `<<device>> AppServer` | `<<executionEnvironment>> ApacheTomcat` | `<<artifact>> ExamPortal.war` |
| Data | `<<device>> DBServer` | `<<executionEnvironment>> MySQL` | `<<artifact>> ExamDB` |
| Security | `<<device>> LDAPServer` | `<<executionEnvironment>> OpenLDAP` | `<<artifact>> users.ldif` |

**Communication associations (with protocol labels):**

1. `Chrome` ─── `«HTTPS»` ───→ `ApacheTomcat`
2. `ApacheTomcat` ─── `«JDBC»` ───→ `MySQL`
3. `ApacheTomcat` ─── `«LDAP»` ───→ `OpenLDAP`

**Explanation of components (each node gets a brief mention for full marks):**

- **StudentPC (<<device>>)** — A Windows PC where students log in. Hosts the Chrome browser.
- **Chrome (<<executionEnvironment>>)** — The web browser acts as the runtime that displays the UI sent by the server.
- **AppServer (<<device>>)** — A physical Linux server hosting Tomcat.
- **ApacheTomcat (<<executionEnvironment>>)** — The Java servlet container executing the WAR file.
- **ExamPortal.war (<<artifact>>)** — The compiled web application deployable.
- **DBServer (<<device>>) / MySQL (<<executionEnvironment>>)** — Hosts the relational database.
- **ExamDB (<<artifact>>)** — The actual schema storing questions, responses, marks.
- **LDAPServer (<<device>>) / OpenLDAP (<<executionEnvironment>>)** — Centralized authentication.
- **users.ldif (<<artifact>>)** — The directory file storing user credentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
> Correct device nodes (2), correct execution environments (2), correct artifacts (1), correct communication lines + protocols (2).

**Part (b) — Stereotypes in UML (7 marks)**

A **stereotype** in UML is an *extension mechanism* (denoted as `<<...>>`) that allows a modeler to introduce a new building-block category with a specific semantics, while keeping the core metamodel unchanged. Stereotypes enrich the deployment diagram with domain-specific meaning.

**Three Examples relevant to the diagram in (a):**

1. **`<<device>>` applied to AppServer, DBServer, LDAPServer, StudentPC**
   — Classifies the node as **physical hardware**. Visually, the 3-D cube shape is implied, and any engineer reading the diagram instantly knows the node is a tangible, purchasable machine (with OS, power, network port).

2. **`<<executionEnvironment>>` applied to ApacheTomcat, MySQL, OpenLDAP, Chrome**
   — Classifies the node as a **software platform** (not hardware). Without this stereotype, one would not know whether Tomcat is a server box or a process. This stereotype signals that the node is a *container* in which artifacts run.

3. **`<<artifact>>` applied to ExamPortal.war, ExamDB, users.ldif**
   — Classifies the document icon as a **physical file** to be deployed. The stereotype is critical because it tells deployment engineers, "this is a tangible, versioned, shippable file" — enabling CD/CI pipelines to track and promote it across environments.

**Bonus discussion (any one accepted for full marks):**
- Stereotype on a **communication association**, e.g., `<<HTTPS>>` on the line between Chrome and Tomcat, specifies the **protocol contract** — designers and network engineers can immediately verify firewall / TLS requirements.
- Stereotype on a **dependency**, e.g., `<<manifest>>` from an artifact to its component, shows that the artifact *implements* the component's behavior (useful for traceability in component → deployment migration).

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative: Internet of Things (IoT) System*

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Draw a deployment diagram for an IoT-based smart agriculture system. The system has soil-moisture sensors, a Raspberry Pi gateway running a Python script, a cloud-based analytics server, and a farmer's mobile app. Communication between gateway and cloud is MQTT; between cloud and mobile is HTTPS.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Explain how a deployment diagram differs from a component diagram, and justify why a deployment diagram is more suitable for IoT systems like the one in part (a).** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — IoT Deployment Diagram (7 marks)**

| Tier | Device | EE | Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edge / Sensor | `<<device>> SoilSensor1`, `SoilSensor2` | `<<executionEnvironment>> Firmware_v2` | `<<artifact>> sensor_fw.bin` |
| Gateway | `<<device>> RaspberryPi` | `<<executionEnvironment>> RaspbianOS` | `<<artifact>> gateway.py` |
| Cloud | `<<device>> CloudAnalyticsVM` | `<<executionEnvironment>> DockerContainer` | `<<artifact>> analytics_service.tar.gz` |
| Client | `<<device>> FarmerMobile` | `<<executionEnvironment>> AndroidOS` | `<<artifact>> FarmerApp.apk` |

**Communication paths:**
- `SoilSensor1` ─── `«LoRaWAN»` ───→ `RaspberryPi`
- `SoilSensor2` ─── `«LoRaWAN»` ───→ `RaspberryPi`
- `RaspberryPi` ─── `«MQTT over TLS»` ───→ `DockerContainer`
- `DockerContainer` ─── `«HTTPS/REST»` ───→ `AndroidOS`

**Explanation of nodes (for full marks):**
- *Soil Sensors* — low-power IoT devices deployed in the field, running embedded firmware.
- *Raspberry Pi* — the edge gateway aggregating sensor data.
- *Cloud Analytics VM* — high-capacity server running containerized analytics service.
- *Farmer Mobile* — Android device where the farmer views dashboards and alerts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
> Correct device nodes (2), correct EEs nested correctly (2), correct artifacts (1), communication lines + protocols (2).

**Part (b) — Deployment vs Component Diagram (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Component Diagram | Deployment Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | *What* software modules exist? | *Where* does each module physically run? |
| Abstraction level | Logical (independent of hardware) | Physical (hardware-aware) |
| Communication | Dependency between components (logical) | Protocol-bound links (e.g., MQTT, HTTPS) |
| Key element | Component (rectangle with tabs) | Node (3-D box) + Artifact (doc icon) |
| Audience | Developers | DevOps, Network architects, Hardware vendors |

**Why Deployment Diagram is more suitable for IoT (any 4 points for full marks):**

1. **Heterogeneous hardware** — IoT systems involve diverse physical devices (sensors, microcontrollers, gateways, cloud VMs, phones). Only a deployment diagram captures this physical diversity using `<<device>>` stereotypes.
2. **Protocol diversity** — IoT uses many protocols (MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN, BLE, HTTP). The deployment diagram's ability to *label* associations with protocols is critical for network planning.
3. **Edge / Fog / Cloud separation** — IoT architecture is inherently tiered. A deployment diagram visually represents this 3-tier (or n-tier) physical distribution.
4. **Resource-constrained devices** — Edge devices have limited memory/CPU. The diagram helps engineers identify *which artifact runs where* — e.g., `sensor_fw.bin` (lightweight) on a microcontroller vs. `analytics_service.tar.gz` (heavy) on a cloud VM.
5. **Scalability and physical topology** — When adding 100 more sensors, the deployment diagram clearly shows whether the gateway can handle the load — a component diagram cannot answer this.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Deployment Diagram Questions — and How They Lose Marks**

| # | Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drawing a **Component Diagram** instead of a **Deployment Diagram** | Up to **5 marks** | Always use 3-D boxes (cubes) for nodes, document icons for artifacts. |
| 2 | **Forgetting stereotypes** `<<device>>`, `<<executionEnvironment>>`, `<<artifact>>` | **2–3 marks** | Add stereotype label inside every node/artifact. |
| 3 | **Not nesting** execution environment inside device | **1–2 marks** | Always draw EE *inside* its parent device box. |
| 4 | **Placing artifacts outside** any execution environment | **1–2 marks** | Artifacts must be *inside* an EE node. |
| 5 | **Missing protocol labels** on communication lines (e.g., plain line without `«HTTP»`) | **1–2 marks** | Always label: `«HTTPS»`, `«JDBC»`, `«MQTT»`, etc. |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

### 🔁 Quick Recap Checklist

- [x] A **deployment diagram** models the **physical runtime architecture** of a system.
- [x] It uses **3-D boxes (nodes)** for hardware/software platforms.
- [x] Three node types: **`<<device>>`**, **`<<executionEnvironment>>`**, generic `Node`.
- [x] **Artifacts** (`<<artifact>>`) are deployable files shown as document icons.
- [x] Execution environments are **nested inside** device nodes.
- [x] Artifacts are **placed inside** execution environment nodes.
- [x] **Communication associations** are solid lines, labeled with **protocols** like `«HTTP»`, `«HTTPS»`, `«JDBC»`, `«MQTT»`, `«LDAP»`, `«RMI»`.
- [x] **Manifestation** = dashed arrow from artifact to component (shows artifact implements component).
- [x] Use cases: distributed apps, web apps, cloud deployments, IoT, mobile systems, microservices.
- [x] **Difference from Component Diagram**: Component = *what*; Deployment = *where*.
- [x] **Tools** (as per KTU lab): StarUML, Rational Rose, Lucidchart, Draw.io, PlantUML.
- [x] **Mandatory in lab record**: Title, all 3 node types, artifacts, labeled communication lines, legend.

### 🎯 The 3-Second Rule for KTU

> *"If your diagram does not have a 3-D box with a `<<device>>` stereotype, a nested box with `<<executionEnvironment>>` stereotype, a document icon with `<<artifact>>` stereotype, and solid lines with `<<protocol>>` labels — **it is not a deployment diagram**."*

### 🧠 Mnemonics to Remember

- **D-E-A-C** → **D**evice, **E**xecution Environment, **A**rtifact, **C**ommunication = the four pillars.
- **"Device is Hardware. EE is Software. Artifact is File. Communication is Wire."**
- **"Deployment = Where. Component = What. Sequence = When."**

### 📊 Expected Mark Weightage in KTU 2024 ESE

| Module | Topic | Avg. Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Module 2 | Deployment Diagram | **7 – 14 marks** (Part A: 3; Part B: 14) |
| Module 1 (related) | Component Diagram | Often compared → 3 marks |
| Lab Internal | Drawing using CASE tool | 10 marks (full practical) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop programme/tool to calculate effort and cost estimation using COCOMO model for any given case/problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL607 - CASE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop programme/tool to calculate effort and cost estimation using COCOMO model for any given case/problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of COCOMO

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model)** is a procedural software cost estimation model developed by **Barry W. Boehm in 1981**. It is used to estimate the **effort** (in Person-Months), **development time** (in months), and **average team size** required to develop a software product, based on the size of the software expressed in **Kilo Delivered Source Instructions (KDSI)** — also called **KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code)**.

In the context of KTU's **CASE LAB (Computer-Aided Software Engineering Laboratory)**, developing a programme/tool for COCOMO estimation means constructing an executable artifact (typically a **Python/Java/C++ script with a GUI or CLI**) that:
1. Accepts **KLOC** (size of software in thousands of lines of code) as input.
2. Accepts the **project mode** (Organic / Semi-Detached / Embedded).
3. Optionally accepts **Cost Drivers (EAF)** for Intermediate/Detailed COCOMO.
4. Computes **Effort (PM)**, **Development Time (Months)**, **Average Staff Size**, **Productivity**, and **Total Cost**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Construction Blueprint Estimator":**
> Imagine you are a civil contractor bidding on a house construction job. Before laying the first brick, you must answer:
> - How many **workers (effort)** will it take?
> - How many **months (schedule)** will it need?
> - What will be the **total wages (cost)**?
>
> You cannot answer these by guessing — you base them on the **size of the house (KLOC)** and the **terrain difficulty (project mode)**. A small cottage in a plain area (Organic) needs fewer workers than a sky-scraper on rocky, regulated land (Embedded).
>
> COCOMO works exactly like this blueprint estimator, but for software. The "house blueprint" is the **software specification**, the "workers" are **software engineers**, and the "terrain difficulty" is the **project complexity class**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in COCOMO

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **canonical Boehm COCOMO constants (1981)** that must be used in any KTU evaluation:
>
> - **Effort Unit:** Person-Months (**PM**), where 1 PM = **152 working hours**.
> - **Size Unit:** **KDSI / KLOC** — Kilo Delivered Source Instructions.
> - **Currency Unit:** Cost is computed as $\text{Cost} = \text{Effort} \times \text{Average Wage per PM}$.
> - **Staff Size Formula:** $\text{Average Staff Size} = \dfrac{\text{Effort (PM)}}{\text{Development Time (Months)}}$

### 1.4 The Three Project Modes of COCOMO (Basic Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Organic** — Small teams, familiar in-house environment, simple application. Examples: business systems, simple inventory tools.
>
> **Semi-Detached** — Medium-sized teams, mixed experience, moderate complexity. Examples: database management systems, compilers.
>
> **Embedded** — Tight hardware/software/operational constraints, very complex. Examples: real-time avionics, missile control systems, ATM switching software.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Concept — How COCOMO Thinks

COCOMO follows a layered estimation philosophy. For KTU lab purposes, you are expected to implement at least the **Basic COCOMO** model, and optionally extend to **Intermediate COCOMO** with **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)**.

The model operates on three levels (as per Boehm's original taxonomy):

1. **Basic COCOMO** — Estimates effort and time purely as a function of **size (KLOC)** and **mode (a, b, c constants)**.
2. **Intermediate COCOMO** — Multiplies the basic effort by an **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)** derived from **15 cost drivers** (product, hardware, personnel, project attributes).
3. **Detailed COCOMO** — Applies phase-sensitive multipliers for each **COCOMO phase** (Plans, Requirements, Design, Coding, Integration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU PCCNL607 lab records/viva, **Basic COCOMO is mandatory**, and **Intermediate COCOMO with EAF** earns you full marks (and examiner appreciation).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Effort (Basic) — Organic | $E = 2.4 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.05}$ | Person-Months | KLOC $> 0$ |
| 2 | Effort (Basic) — Semi-Detached | $E = 3.0 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.12}$ | Person-Months | KLOC $> 0$ |
| 3 | Effort (Basic) — Embedded | $E = 3.6 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.20}$ | Person-Months | KLOC $> 0$ |
| 4 | Development Time — Organic | $T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.38}$ | Months | $E > 0$ |
| 5 | Development Time — Semi-Detached | $T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.35}$ | Months | $E > 0$ |
| 6 | Development Time — Embedded | $T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.32}$ | Months | $E > 0$ |
| 7 | Effort (Intermediate) | $E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b} \times \text{EAF}$ | Person-Months | $0.75 \le \text{EAF} \le 1.66$ typically |
| 8 | EAF | $\text{EAF} = \prod_{i=1}^{15} \text{EM}_i$ | Dimensionless | Product of 15 Effort Multipliers |
| 9 | Average Staff Size | $SS = \dfrac{E}{T_{\text{dev}}}$ | Persons | Always $\ge 1$ |
| 10 | Productivity | $P = \dfrac{\text{KLOC}}{E}$ | KLOC / PM | Inverse of effort intensity |
| 11 | Total Cost | $C = E \times W$ | Currency | $W$ = wage per Person-Month |
| 12 | Cost per KLOC | $C_{\text{KLOC}} = \dfrac{C}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Currency / KLOC | Used for benchmarking |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PIPING RULE REMINDER:** All absolute values in formula expressions (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) use `\vert` inside table cells to prevent KTU's strict markdown table parser from breaking.

### 2.3 The 15 Cost Drivers of Intermediate COCOMO

> [!NOTE]
> These 15 attributes are grouped into **4 categories**. Each is rated on a scale of **Very Low → Very High**, and an Effort Multiplier (EM) is assigned. The product of all 15 EMs gives the **EAF**.

| Category | Cost Drivers |
|----------|--------------|
| **Product Attributes** | Required Software Reliability (RELY), Database Size (DATA), Product Complexity (CPLX) |
| **Hardware Attributes** | Execution Time Constraint (TIME), Main Storage Constraint (STOR), Virtual Machine Volatility (VIRT), Computer Turnaround Time (TURN) |
| **Personnel Attributes** | Analyst Capability (ACAP), Programmer Capability (PCAP), Applications Experience (AEXP), Virtual Machine Experience (VEXP), Programming Language Experience (LEXP) |
| **Project Attributes** | Modern Programming Practices (MODP), Use of Software Tools (TOOL), Required Development Schedule (SCED) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each EM typically ranges from **0.75 (Very Low impact — speeds up)** to **1.66 (Very High impact — slows down)**. The default for an unrated driver is **1.00 (Nominal)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of COCOMO in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Project Bidding:** Software firms use COCOMO to quote fixed-price contracts.
> - **Resource Planning:** Managers determine hiring needs months in advance.
> - **Risk Analysis:** Sensitivity to cost drivers identifies high-risk project dimensions.
> - **Benchmarking:** Compare actuals vs. estimated productivity to improve future estimates.
> - **Modern Relevance:** COCOMO II (1995/1997) extends these principles for object-oriented, distributed, and agile contexts — and is used by NASA, DOD, and Boeing.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Effort and Time from KLOC

Let us derive the COCOMO Basic Model formulae step-by-step so you can write them confidently in your KTU lab record and viva.

**Step 1 — The Fundamental Assumption (Power Law).**
Boehm empirically observed that software effort grows **non-linearly** with size. He proposed a **power law** relation:

$$
E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- $E$ is the effort in Person-Months.
- $\text{KLOC}$ is the size in thousands of delivered source instructions.
- $a$ and $b$ are empirically derived constants that depend on the project mode. As complexity (mode) increases, both $a$ and $b$ increase — reflecting that embedded systems demand disproportionately more effort per KLOC than organic systems.

**Step 2 — Substituting Mode-Specific Constants.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Organic:} \quad & E = 2.4 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.05} \\
\text{Semi-Detached:} \quad & E = 3.0 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.12} \\
\text{Embedded:} \quad & E = 3.6 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.20}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- For **Organic** projects, the exponent is nearly **linear (1.05)** — a 10% increase in size requires roughly 10.5% more effort. This reflects familiar technology and small team dynamics.
- For **Embedded** projects, the exponent is **1.20** — a 10% size increase demands 12% more effort, but the coefficient $a = 3.6$ (vs. 2.4 for organic) means the **absolute effort penalty** is severe. This is because embedded systems have tight hardware coupling, real-time constraints, and exhaustive verification.

**Step 3 — Deriving Development Time from Effort.**
Boehm's second empirical law links schedule to effort:

$$
T_{\text{dev}} = c \times (E)^{d}
$$

where $c = 2.5$ for all modes, and $d$ decreases with mode complexity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Organic:} \quad & T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.38} \\
\text{Semi-Detached:} \quad & T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.35} \\
\text{Embedded:} \quad & T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.32}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- A **higher exponent $b$** on effort means longer calendar time per unit effort for organic projects — small projects need proportionally more calendar time because overheads (planning, testing) consume a larger fraction.
- A **lower exponent $d$** for embedded systems means that even though effort is huge, the team size scales up to compress calendar time — embedded projects are scheduled aggressively.

**Step 4 — Computing Average Staff Size.**

$$
SS = \frac{E}{T_{\text{dev}}}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- Dividing total Person-Months by calendar months gives the **average number of full-time personnel** working on the project throughout its lifecycle.

**Step 5 — Adding the EAF for Intermediate COCOMO.**

$$
E_{\text{adj}} = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b} \times \text{EAF}
$$

where $\text{EAF} = \text{EM}_1 \times \text{EM}_2 \times \ldots \times \text{EM}_{15}$

**Textual Explanation:**
- Each EM (Effort Multiplier) of **$<1$** means that driver **speeds up** development (e.g., highly experienced staff).
- Each EM of **$>1$** means that driver **slows down** development (e.g., very tight reliability requirements).

**Step 6 — Computing Total Cost.**

$$
C = E_{\text{adj}} \times W
$$

where $W$ is the average wage per Person-Month (e.g., ₹1,50,000 / PM in Indian context).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full KTU Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A semi-detached software project estimated at **8 KLOC**. Average wage per PM = **₹1,00,000**. Compute Effort, Development Time, Staff Size, Productivity, and Total Cost using **Basic COCOMO**.

**Solution — Step 1: Effort.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.0 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.12} \\
  &= 3.0 \times (8)^{1.12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-step: Computing $8^{1.12}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{1.12} &= e^{1.12 \times \ln(8)} \\
         &= e^{1.12 \times 2.0794} \\
         &= e^{2.3289} \\
         &\approx 10.266
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.0 \times 10.266 \\
  &\approx 30.80 \text{ Person-Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Development Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dev}} &= 2.5 \times (E)^{0.35} \\
              &= 2.5 \times (30.80)^{0.35}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-step: Computing $30.80^{0.35}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
30.80^{0.35} &= e^{0.35 \times \ln(30.80)} \\
             &= e^{0.35 \times 3.4275} \\
             &= e^{1.1996} \\
             &\approx 3.318
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dev}} &= 2.5 \times 3.318 \\
              &\approx 8.30 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average Staff Size.**

$$
SS = \frac{E}{T_{\text{dev}}} = \frac{30.80}{8.30} \approx 3.71 \text{ Persons}
$$

**Step 4: Productivity.**

$$
P = \frac{\text{KLOC}}{E} = \frac{8}{30.80} \approx 0.260 \text{ KLOC / PM}
$$

**Step 5: Total Cost.**

$$
C = E \times W = 30.80 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,30{,}80{,}000
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Comparison for the Same KLOC

| KLOC | Mode | Effort $E$ (PM) | Time $T_{\text{dev}}$ (Months) | Staff $SS$ | Productivity (KLOC/PM) |
|------|------|-----------------|-------------------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 10 | Organic | $2.4 \times 10^{1.05} \approx 25.30$ | $2.5 \times 25.30^{0.38} \approx 7.79$ | 3.25 | 0.395 |
| 10 | Semi-Detached | $3.0 \times 10^{1.12} \approx 37.18$ | $2.5 \times 37.18^{0.35} \approx 8.94$ | 4.16 | 0.269 |
| 10 | Embedded | $3.6 \times 10^{1.20} \approx 57.54$ | $2.5 \times 57.54^{0.32} \approx 9.39$ | 6.13 | 0.174 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For the **same 10 KLOC**, embedded mode demands **~2.27× more effort** than organic mode. This is why mode selection is critical in any COCOMO-based tool.

### 3.4 Reference Implementation — Python Tool for COCOMO (Lab Program)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Python programme is a **complete, runnable reference implementation** for your KTU lab record. You may adapt it to Java, C++, or add a Tkinter/Flask GUI for bonus marks.

```python
# ============================================================
# PCCNL607 - CASE LAB | Module Experiment 2
# COCOMO Effort and Cost Estimation Tool
# ============================================================

import math

def cocomo_basic(kloc, mode):
    """Compute Basic COCOMO estimates."""
    coefficients = {
        'organic':       (2.4, 1.05, 0.38),
        'semi-detached': (3.0, 1.12, 0.35),
        'embedded':      (3.6, 1.20, 0.32),
    }
    if mode not in coefficients:
        raise ValueError("Invalid mode. Choose: organic / semi-detached / embedded")
    a, b, d = coefficients[mode]
    effort = a * (kloc ** b)
    tdev   = 2.5 * (effort ** d)
    staff  = effort / tdev
    productivity = kloc / effort
    return effort, tdev, staff, productivity


def cocomo_intermediate(kloc, mode, eaf):
    """Compute Intermediate COCOMO estimates with EAF."""
    a_table = {'organic': 2.4, 'semi-detached': 3.0, 'embedded': 3.6}
    b_table = {'organic': 1.05, 'semi-detached': 1.12, 'embedded': 1.20}
    d_table = {'organic': 0.38, 'semi-detached': 0.35, 'embedded': 0.32}
    if mode not in a_table:
        raise ValueError("Invalid mode.")
    effort = a_table[mode] * (kloc ** b_table[mode]) * eaf
    tdev   = 2.5 * (effort ** d_table[mode])
    staff  = effort / tdev
    return effort, tdev, staff


def estimate_cost(effort, wage_per_pm):
    """Compute total cost from effort and wage."""
    return effort * wage_per_pm


def main():
    print("=" * 60)
    print(" COCOMO Effort & Cost Estimation Tool | KTU PCCNL607 ")
    print("=" * 60)

    try:
        kloc = float(input("Enter software size (KLOC)            : "))
        mode = input("Enter project mode (organic/semi/embedded): ").strip().lower()

        # Basic COCOMO
        effort, tdev, staff, productivity = cocomo_basic(kloc, mode)
        wage = float(input("Enter average wage per Person-Month (₹): "))
        cost = estimate_cost(effort, wage)

        print("\n----- BASIC COCOMO RESULTS -----")
        print(f"Effort (Person-Months)   : {effort:.3f}")
        print(f"Development Time (Months): {tdev:.3f}")
        print(f"Average Staff Size       : {staff:.3f}")
        print(f"Productivity (KLOC/PM)   : {productivity:.4f}")
        print(f"Total Estimated Cost (₹) : {cost:,.2f}")

        # Optional Intermediate COCOMO
        choice = input("\nUse Intermediate COCOMO with EAF? (y/n): ").strip().lower()
        if choice == 'y':
            eaf = float(input("Enter Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF): "))
            effort_i, tdev_i, staff_i = cocomo_intermediate(kloc, mode, eaf)
            cost_i = estimate_cost(effort_i, wage)
            print("\n----- INTERMEDIATE COCOMO RESULTS -----")
            print(f"Adjusted Effort (PM)     : {effort_i:.3f}")
            print(f"Adjusted Time (Months)   : {tdev_i:.3f}")
            print(f"Adjusted Staff Size      : {staff_i:.3f}")
            print(f"Adjusted Total Cost (₹)  : {cost_i:,.2f}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[ERROR] {e}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

============================================================
Enter software size (KLOC)            : 8
Enter project mode (organic/semi/embedded): semi-detached
Enter average wage per Person-Month (₹): 100000

----- BASIC COCOMO RESULTS -----

Development Time (Months): 8.296
Average Staff Size       : 3.712

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PCCNL607 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define COCOMO. List its three basic project modes.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer:**
> COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) is a procedural software cost estimation model proposed by **Barry W. Boehm in 1981**. It estimates effort, development time, and cost as a function of software size (KLOC) and project complexity.
>
> The three project modes are:
> 1. **Organic** — small teams, familiar in-house applications.
> 2. **Semi-Detached** — medium teams, mixed experience, moderate complexity.
> 3. **Embedded** — tight hardware/real-time constraints, very complex systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PCCNL607 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. What is the role of the Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF) in Intermediate COCOMO?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)** is a multiplicative correction factor used in **Intermediate COCOMO** to refine the basic effort estimate. It is the product of **15 Effort Multipliers (EMs)** derived from four categories: product, hardware, personnel, and project attributes.
> An EAF $> 1$ indicates that the project attributes slow down development, while EAF $< 1$ indicates that they speed it up. The adjusted effort is $E = a \times \text{KLOC}^{b} \times \text{EAF}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by not writing the **mode-specific constants** ($a, b, c, d$) explicitly.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** by **forgetting the unit** (Person-Months, Months, KLOC).
> - Students **lose 2 marks** in viva if they cannot justify the **power-law rationale** of Boehm's empirical model.

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q.A (a)** Compute the **effort, development time, average staff size, and productivity** for a **semi-detached** project of size **15 KLOC** using **Basic COCOMO**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the Semi-Detached Effort Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.0 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.12} \\
  &= 3.0 \times (15)^{1.12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $15^{1.12}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
15^{1.12} &= e^{1.12 \times \ln(15)} \\
          &= e^{1.12 \times 2.7081} \\
          &= e^{3.0330} \\
          &\approx 20.764
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Effort:

$$
E = 3.0 \times 20.764 \approx 62.29 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

Step 4 — Development Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dev}} &= 2.5 \times (62.29)^{0.35} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{0.35 \times \ln(62.29)} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{0.35 \times 4.1313} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{1.4460} \\
              &\approx 2.5 \times 4.2458 \\
              &\approx 10.61 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Staff Size:

$$
SS = \frac{62.29}{10.61} \approx 5.87 \text{ Persons}
$$

Step 6 — Productivity:

$$
P = \frac{15}{62.29} \approx 0.2408 \text{ KLOC / PM}
$$

**Final Answers (Q.A-a):**
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Effort | **62.29 PM** |
| Development Time | **10.61 Months** |
| Average Staff Size | **5.87 Persons** |
| Productivity | **0.2408 KLOC/PM** |

---

**Q.A (b)** Develop the **algorithm and Python programme** for a COCOMO Basic Model tool that accepts KLOC, mode, and wage per PM, and outputs effort, time, staff, productivity, and cost. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
BEGIN COCOMO_TOOL
    DISPLAY banner
    READ KLOC, mode, wage
    IF mode = "organic"       THEN a = 2.4, b = 1.05, d = 0.38
    IF mode = "semi-detached" THEN a = 3.0, b = 1.12, d = 0.35
    IF mode = "embedded"      THEN a = 3.6, b = 1.20, d = 0.32
    effort       = a * KLOC^b
    tdev         = 2.5 * effort^d
    staff        = effort / tdev
    productivity = KLOC / effort
    cost         = effort * wage
    DISPLAY all outputs
END
```

**Python Reference Code:**

```python
def cocomo_tool(kloc, mode, wage):
    modes = {
        'organic':       (2.4, 1.05, 0.38),
        'semi-detached': (3.0, 1.12, 0.35),
        'embedded':      (3.6, 1.20, 0.32)
    }
    a, b, d = modes[mode]
    E = a * (kloc ** b)
    T = 2.5 * (E ** d)
    SS = E / T
    P = kloc / E
    C = E * wage
    return E, T, SS, P, C

# Driver
kloc = 15
mode = "semi-detached"
wage = 100000
E, T, SS, P, C = cocomo_tool(kloc, mode, wage)
print(f"Effort={E:.2f} PM, Time={T:.2f} months, "
      f"Staff={SS:.2f}, Productivity={P:.4f}, Cost=Rs.{C:,.2f}")
```

**Output (for input above):**

```
Effort=62.29 PM, Time=10.61 months, Staff=5.87, Productivity=0.2408, Cost=Rs.6,229,256.32
```

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q.B (a)** For an **embedded** software project of **25 KLOC**, compute the **effort, development time, staff size, and total cost** assuming a wage of **₹2,00,000 per Person-Month**. Use **Basic COCOMO**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Effort for Embedded Mode:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.6 \times (25)^{1.20}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $25^{1.20}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
25^{1.20} &= e^{1.20 \times \ln(25)} \\
          &= e^{1.20 \times 3.2189} \\
          &= e^{3.8627} \\
          &\approx 47.61
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Final Effort:

$$
E = 3.6 \times 47.61 \approx 171.40 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

Step 4 — Development Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dev}} &= 2.5 \times (171.40)^{0.32} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{0.32 \times \ln(171.40)} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{0.32 \times 5.1443} \\
              &= 2.5 \times e^{1.6462} \\
              &\approx 2.5 \times 5.188 \\
              &\approx 12.97 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Staff Size:

$$
SS = \frac{171.40}{12.97} \approx 13.21 \text{ Persons}
$$

Step 6 — Total Cost:

$$
C = 171.40 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,3{,}42{,}80{,}000
$$

**Final Answers (Q.B-a):**
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Effort | **171.40 PM** |
| Development Time | **12.97 Months** |
| Average Staff Size | **13.21 Persons** |
| Total Cost | **₹3,42,80,000** |

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain with a **block diagram** how **Intermediate COCOMO** extends Basic COCOMO using the **Effort Adjustment Factor**. List any **5 cost drivers** and their typical effort multiplier ranges. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation:**
Intermediate COCOMO extends the basic effort formula by introducing the **EAF**, which captures the influence of **15 cost drivers** on development effort. The extended formula is:

$$
E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b} \times \text{EAF}, \quad \text{where } \text{EAF} = \prod_{i=1}^{15} \text{EM}_i
$$

The block diagram is identical to the **Intermediate COCOMO Hierarchical Decomposition** in §4.3 of Section 4.

**Five Sample Cost Drivers with EM Ranges:**

| # | Cost Driver | Category | Nominal EM | Range |
|---|-------------|----------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Required Software Reliability (RELY) | Product | 1.00 | 0.75 – 1.39 |
| 2 | Database Size (DATA) | Product | 1.00 | 0.94 – 1.28 |
| 3 | Product Complexity (CPLX) | Product | 1.00 | 0.75 – 1.66 |
| 4 | Programmer Capability (PCAP) | Personnel | 1.00 | 0.75 – 1.34 |
| 5 | Required Development Schedule (SCED) | Project | 1.00 | 0.91 – 1.43 |

**Conclusion:** Each EM is multiplied; the cumulative EAF adjusts the basic estimate. Highly skilled personnel (PCAP $<$ 1) can reduce effort, while extreme complexity (CPLX $>$ 1) inflates it. For KTU lab records, students should tabulate **at least 5 cost drivers** with their multipliers.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PCCNL607 Module 2 Viva:**

- **COCOMO** stands for **C**onstructive **Co**st **Mo**del, by **Barry Boehm (1981)**.
- The size unit is **KLOC / KDSI** = thousands of delivered source instructions.
- The effort unit is **Person-Months (PM)** = 152 person-hours.
- The three modes are **Organic (2.4, 1.05, 0.38)**, **Semi-Detached (3.0, 1.12, 0.35)**, and **Embedded (3.6, 1.20, 0.32)**.
- All three modes use the same **schedule coefficient $c = 2.5$** in $T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times E^{d}$.
- Effort grows as a **power law** with size; exponent $b$ ranges from **1.05 (Organic)** to **1.20 (Embedded)**.
- Development time exponent $d$ **decreases** with complexity (0.38 → 0.32) because aggressive staffing compresses calendar time in embedded projects.
- **Staff Size** = Effort / Time.
- **Productivity** = KLOC / Effort.
- **Total Cost** = Effort × Wage per PM.
- **Intermediate COCOMO** multiplies effort by **EAF** = product of **15 effort multipliers**.
- Each EM is rated on a scale of **Very Low (0.75) to Extra High (1.66)**.
- The **4 categories** of cost drivers are **Product, Hardware, Personnel, Project**.
- For your **lab programme**, implement a function-based Python tool with proper **input validation** and **formatted output**.
- **Always show** intermediate calculations (e.g., $\ln$, exponentiation) in your record to secure **full marks**.
- **COCOMO II** (1995/1997) extends Boehm's model with **5 scale factors** and **17 cost drivers** for modern object-oriented and agile contexts — a **bonus viva point**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Viva Trap:** If asked *"Why does the exponent $b$ increase from Organic to Embedded?"*, answer: *"Because as project complexity rises, the marginal effort per additional KLOC grows due to integration overheads, real-time verification, and hardware-software coupling — empirically captured by Boehm's data-fitting."*
